mock_tests.instruction:
The Question Paper contains three sections.
 Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any20 questions.
 Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions.
 All questions carry equal marks.
 There is no negative marking.

questions.question: Technology strives
questions.question_raw: Technology strives

options.text: to fulfill a human need such as faster cooking or sewing etc

correct_option_texts: to fulfill a human need such as faster cooking or sewing etc
questions.explanation: Science and technology will continue to generate all sorts of new enhancers, and the quest for enhancement is not necessarily unfair or unethical. We, humans, are inveterate enhancers, striving to increase our intelligence and to improve our memory and powers of perception.

questions.question: Which of the following quantities can be written in SI units in kgm^2A^-2s^-3?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following quantities can be written in SI units in kgm2A-2s-3?
questions.explanation:
[Resistance] = [ML^2T^-3A^-2]
$\therefore$ Unit of resistance = kg m^2A^-2s^-3

questions.question: For motion with uniform acceleration, x-t graph is
questions.question_raw: For motion with uniform acceleration, x-t graph is
questions.explanation:
We know that , s = ut + 0.5at^2
The slope of the x-t graph will give you velocity. So, if u=0, the slope of the graph at t=0 is 0. For u>0, the initial slope is +ve and for negative u, the initial slope is negative. Hence you will get parabolic shapes.

questions.question: Multiplying a vector $\vec v$ by a negative real number $\lambda$
questions.question_raw: Multiplying a vector {tex}\vec v{/tex} by a negative real number {tex}\lambda{/tex}

options.text: gives a scalar that is $\lambda $ times the polar angle of $\vec v$
gives a vector $\overrightarrow {v'} $ = $\lambda \vec v$ in the same direction as $\vec v$
gives a scalar that is $\lambda $ times the magnitude of $\vec v$
gives a vector $\overrightarrow {v'} $ = $\lambda \vec v$ in a direction opposite to $\vec v$

correct_option_texts: gives a vector $\overrightarrow {v'} $ = $\lambda \vec v$ in a direction opposite to $\vec v$
questions.explanation: If a vector is multiplied by a negative number (for example -2, -3, -5, -60 unit etc.) or a scalar not only its magnitude is changed but its direction also reversed.

questions.question: The number of significant digits in 3,000,000 is
questions.question_raw: The number of significant digits in 3,000,000 is
questions.explanation:
There are three rules on determining how many significant figures are in a number: 
 Non-zero digits are always significant.
 Any zeros between two significant digits are significant.
 A final zero or trailing zeros in the decimal portion ONLY are significant.
So keeping these rules in mind, there is only one significant digit.

questions.question: The moment of inertia of the ring about its diameter is I. The moment of inertia of the same ring about the axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through centre is
questions.question_raw: The moment of inertia of the ring about its diameter is I. The moment of inertia of the same ring about the axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through centre is
questions.explanation:
By perpendicular axes theorem, I_centre = I_D + I_D
= I + I = 2I

questions.question: A solid sphere, disc and solid cylinder all of the same mass and made of the same material are allowed to roll down (from rest) on the inclined plane, then
questions.question_raw: A solid sphere, disc and solid cylinder all of the same mass and made of the same material are allowed to roll down (from rest) on the inclined plane, then

options.text: solid sphere reaches the bottom last
solid sphere reaches the bottom first
all reach the bottom at the same time
disc will reach the bottom first

correct_option_texts: solid sphere reaches the bottom first
questions.explanation:
$a=\frac{g \sin \theta}{1+\frac{I}{M R^{2}}}$
For a solid sphere, $I=\frac{2}{5} M R^{2} $
$ \therefore a=\frac{5}{7} g \sin \theta$
For a disc, $I=\frac{1}{2} M R^{2}$
$\therefore a=\frac{2}{3} g \sin \theta$
For a solid cylinder,$I=\frac{1}{2} M R^{2} $
$\therefore a=\frac{2}{3} g \sin \theta$
As solid sphere has maximum acceleration, it reaches the bottom first. Both disc and cylinder reach together later.

questions.question: Two particles of equal mass go around a circle of radius R under the action of their mutual gravitational attraction. The speed v of each particle is:
questions.question_raw: Two particles of equal mass go around a circle of radius R under the action of their mutual gravitational attraction. The speed v of each particle is:
questions.explanation:
The two masses, separated by a distance 2 R, revolve about the common centre of mass O.
Centripetal force = Mutual gravitational attraction
$\frac{m v^{2}}{R}=\frac{G m \ \times \ m}{(2 R)^{2}}$
or v^2 = $\frac{\mathrm{GM}}{4 R}$
$\therefore \ v = \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{\frac{G m}{R}}$

questions.question: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of B with respect to A in m/sec? Choose the positive direction of x-axis to be from south to north.
questions.question_raw: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of B with respect to A in m/sec? Choose the positive direction of x-axis to be from south to north.
questions.explanation:
Velocity of A is given by , $v_A = +54\ km h^{–1} = + 15\ ms^{–1 }$
Velocity of B is given by , $v_B = ~– 90\ km h^{–1} =\ – 25\ m s^{–1 }$
Relative velocity of B with respect to A, $v_{BA}= v_B – v_A $$=-25-15 =\ – 40\ m s^{–1 }$
i.e. the train B appears to A to move with a speed of 40 ms^–1 from north to south.

questions.question: A batter hits a baseball so that it leaves the bat at speed ${{\rm{v}}_0}$= 37.0 m/s at an angle a = 53.1^o. Find the position of the ball and its velocity (magnitude and direction) at t = 2.00s.
questions.question_raw: A batter hits a baseball so that it leaves the bat at speed {tex}{{\rm{v}}_0}{/tex}= 37.0 m/s at an angle a = 53.1o. Find the position of the ball and its velocity (magnitude and direction) at t = 2.00s.
questions.explanation:
We want to find x, y, v_x, and v_y at t = 2.00 s.
The initial velocity of the ball has components
v_ox = v_ o cos $\alpha_o$= $37.0 \times cos 53.1°$ 
= 22.2 m/s 
v_oy = vo_ o sin $\alpha_o$= $37.0\times sin 53.1°$ 
= 29.6 m/s
From above equations value of x and y at the = 2.0 s
x = v_ox t = $22.2 \times 2.0$ = 44.4 m
$y=v_{oy} t-{1\over 2} gt^2 $ ​​​​​​​
$=29.6 \times 2.0 -{1\over 2} \times 9.8\times (2.0) ^2$ 
= 39.6 m​​​​​​​
$v_x=v_{ox}=22.2 \ m/s $ ​​​​​​​
$v_y=v_{oy}-gt$ ​​​​​​​
$= 29.6 -9.8\times 2.0 =10.0 m/s$ 
The y-component of velocity is positive at t=2.0 s, so the ball is still moving upward the magnitude and direction of the velocity are
v = $\sqrt {(v_x)^2+(v_y)^2} $ 
= $\sqrt {(22.2)^2+(10)^2}=24.4 m/s$ ​​​​​​​
$\alpha = tan^{-1}({10.0 \over22.2 }) $ 
= $tan^{-1}0.45=24.2°$ 
The ball is moving at 24.4 m/s in a direction 24.2° above the horizontal

questions.question: An army vehicle of mass 1000 kg is moving with a velocity of 10 m/s and is acted upon by a forward force of 1000 N due to the engine and a retarding force of 500 N due to friction. What will be its velocity after 10 s?
questions.question_raw: An army vehicle of mass 1000 kg is moving with a velocity of 10 m/s and is acted upon by a forward force of 1000 N due to the engine and a retarding force of 500 N due to friction. What will be its velocity after 10 s?
questions.explanation:
Net forward force = 1000 - 500 = 500 N
$a=\frac{F}{m}=\frac{500}{1000}=\frac{1}{2}$ ms^-2
v = u + at $=10+\frac{1}{2} \times 10$ = 15 ms^-1

questions.question: Power is defined as
questions.question_raw: Power is defined as

options.text: the time rate at which work is done.

correct_option_texts: the time rate at which work is done.
questions.explanation:
Power is the rate of doing work. It is the amount of energy consumed per unit time. Having no direction, it is a scalar quantity. In the SI system, the unit of power is the joule per second (J/s), known as the watt. The integral of power over time defines the work performed.
$P = {{dW} \over {dt}} = {{d(\vec F.\vec s)} \over {dt}}$

questions.question: A body is projected from the ground with some angle to the horizontal. What happens to the angular momentum about the initial position in this motion?
questions.question_raw: A body is projected from the ground with some angle to the horizontal. What happens to the angular momentum about the initial position in this motion?
questions.explanation: About the initial position, the angular momentum of the particle is in a clockwise sense. Also, the torque due to its weight mg is in a clockwise sense. Hence the angular momentum increases.

questions.question: The ratio of the accelerations due to gravity at the bottom of a deep mine and that on the surface of the earth is $\frac {978}{980}$. Find the depth of the mine if the density of the earth is uniform throughout and the radius of the earth is 6300 km.
questions.question_raw: The ratio of the accelerations due to gravity at the bottom of a deep mine and that on the surface of the earth is {tex}\frac {978}{980}{/tex}. Find the depth of the mine if the density of the earth is uniform throughout and the radius of the earth is 6300 km.
questions.explanation:
Ratio of acceleration due to gravity $\frac{g^{\prime}}{g}=\frac{978}{980}=1-\frac{d}{R}$
or $\frac{d}{R}=1-\frac{978}{980}=\frac{2}{980}$ or d = $\frac{2 R}{980}$
= $\frac{2 \ \times \ 6300}{980}$
= 12.86 km

questions.question: Science is
questions.question_raw: Science is

options.text: dependent on the emotions of scientists

questions.explanation: Objectivity in science is a value that informs how science is practiced and how scientific truths are discovered. It is the idea that scientists, in attempting to uncover truths about the natural world, must aspire to eliminate personal biases, a priori commitments, emotional involvement, etc.

questions.question: The number of significant digits in 6.320 J is
questions.question_raw: The number of significant digits in 6.320 J is
questions.explanation:
There are three rules on determining how many significant figures are in a number: 
 Non-zero digits are always significant.
 Any zeros between two significant digits are significant.
 A final zero or trailing zeros in the decimal portion ONLY are significant.
So keeping these rules in mind, there are 4 significant digits.

questions.question: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the maximum height it travels in meters. (Take g = 9.8 m/${\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}$)
questions.question_raw: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the maximum height it travels in meters. (Take g = 9.8 m/{tex}{\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex})
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity, u = 20.0 m/s
At maximum height stone will be stopped, So final velocity, v = 0 m/s
Acceleration due to gravity, a = g = -9.8 m/s^​​​​​​2 (-ve sign show the opposite direction of motion)
Let maximum height = s
We know, $v^2-u^2=2as$
$\Rightarrow$ $0^2-(20)^2= 2\times(- 9.8)s$
$\Rightarrow -400= -19.6s$
$\Rightarrow s = {-400\over -19.6}= 20.4\ m$

questions.question: The horizontal range of a projectile fired at an angle of 15° is 50 m. If it is fired with the same speed at an angle of 45°, its range will be
questions.question_raw: The horizontal range of a projectile fired at an angle of 15° is 50 m. If it is fired with the same speed at an angle of 45°, its range will be
questions.explanation:
projectile is fired at $\theta=15^{\circ}$, R = 50 m
R = $\frac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g}$
50 = $\frac{u^{2} \sin 2 \times 15^{\circ}}{g} \Rightarrow u^{2}$ = 50 g $\times$ 2
u^2 = 100 g
Now $\theta=45^{\circ}, u^{2}$=100 g
$\therefore R=\frac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta}{g}=\frac{100 g \times \sin 2 \times 45^{\circ}}{g}$
$\Rightarrow$ R = 100 m

questions.question: A block of mass m is placed on a smooth wedge of inclination $\theta$. The whole system is accelerated horizontally so that the block does not slip on the wedge. The force exerted by the wedge on the block (g is acceleration due to gravity) will be
questions.question_raw: A block of mass m is placed on a smooth wedge of inclination {tex}\theta{/tex}. The whole system is accelerated horizontally so that the block does not slip on the wedge. The force exerted by the wedge on the block (g is acceleration due to gravity) will be
questions.explanation:
macos$\theta$ = mg sin$\theta$
a = g tan$\theta$
Total reaction of the wedge on the block is
R = mgcos$\theta$ + masin$\theta$
$=m g \cos \theta+m \cdot \frac{g \sin \theta}{\cos \theta} \cdot \sin \theta$
$=\frac{m g\left(\cos ^{2} \theta+\sin ^{2} \theta\right)}{\cos \theta}=\frac{m g}{\cos \theta}$

questions.question: A 6.0-kg block initially at rest is pulled to the right along a horizontal, surface having a coefficient of kinetic friction of 0.15, by a constant horizontal force of 12 N. Find the speed of the block after it has moved 3.0 m?
questions.question_raw: A 6.0-kg block initially at rest is pulled to the right along a horizontal, surface having a coefficient of kinetic friction of 0.15, by a constant horizontal force of 12 N. Find the speed of the block after it has moved 3.0 m?
questions.explanation:
$\Delta$K = W
F = 12N
f = $\mu$R = $\mu$mg = 0.15 $\times$ 6 $\times$ 9.8 = 8.82N
F_net = F - f = 12 - 8.82 = 3.18N
1/2mv^2 - 1/2mu^2 = W
1/2mv^2-0 = F_net x $x$
$\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times v^{2}\right)$ = 3.18 $\times$ 3
3v^2 = 3.18 $\times$ 3
v^2 = 3.18v = 1.8m/sec

questions.question: A uniform square plate has a small piece Q of an irregular shape removed and glued to the centre of the plate leaving a hole behind (Fig.). The moment of inertia about the z-axis is then
questions.question_raw: A uniform square plate has a small piece Q of an irregular shape removed and glued to the centre of the plate leaving a hole behind (Fig.). The moment of inertia about the z-axis is then
questions.explanation:
As the piece Q is removed and glued to centre, the moment of inertia decreases about z -axis as the
M.I = $\sum \mathrm{m}_{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{d}_{\mathrm{i}}^{2}$
As the distance of that piece from z-axis is decreased to zero, hence its M.I will decrease.

questions.question: For the earth the escape speed in terms of g, the acceleration due to gravity and R_E, the radius of the earth is
questions.question_raw: For the earth the escape speed in terms of g, the acceleration due to gravity and RE, the radius of the earth is
questions.explanation:
The work done to displace a body from the surface of earth (r = R_e) to infinity ( $r = \infty $ ) is
$W = G{{Mm} \over {R_e{\rm{ }}}}$
If v_e is the required escape velocity then kinetic energy which should be given to the body is ${1 \over 2}{\rm{ }}m{v_e}^2$
$\therefore {1 \over 2}{\rm{ }}m{v_e}^2 = G{{Mm} \over R_e}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_e} = \sqrt {2g{R_e}} $

questions.question: A truck has a velocity of 2 m/s at time t=0. It accelerates at 2 m / ${{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}$ on seeing police. What is its velocity in m/s at a time of 2 sec?
questions.question_raw: A truck has a velocity of 2 m/s at time t=0. It accelerates at 2 m / {tex}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex} on seeing police. What is its velocity in m/s at a time of 2 sec?
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity is given by, u = 2 m/s
Final velocity is given by , = v m/s
Time duration is = final time - initial time = 2 - 0 = 2 s
Acceleration, a = 2 m/s^​​​​​​2
We know, v = u + at
$\Rightarrow$ v = $2+ 2\times 2$
$\Rightarrow$ v = 6 m/s

questions.question: Consider the quantities, pressure, power, energy, impulse, gravitational potential, electrical charge, temperature, area. Out of these, the only vector quantities are
questions.question_raw: Consider the quantities, pressure, power, energy, impulse, gravitational potential, electrical charge, temperature, area. Out of these, the only vector quantities are
questions.explanation: We know that impulse is changed in momentum per unit time and momentum is a vector quantity; therefore impulse is also a vector quantity. The area is taken as a vector quantity where the calculation of flux is required. Area's vector direction is normal to its plane

questions.question: A stone of mass m tied to a string of length l is rotated in a circle with the other end of the string as the centre. The speed of the stone is v. If the string breaks, the stone will
questions.question_raw: A stone of mass m tied to a string of length l is rotated in a circle with the other end of the string as the centre. The speed of the stone is v. If the string breaks, the stone will
questions.explanation: When the string breaks, the centripetal force becomes zero. The stone moves along the tangent to the circular path i.e., in the direction of velocity.

questions.question: A man turns on a rotation table with an angular speed $\omega $. He is holding two equal masses at arm's length. Without moving his arms, he just drops the two masses. How will his angular speed change?
questions.question_raw: A man turns on a rotation table with an angular speed {tex}\omega {/tex}. He is holding two equal masses at arm's length. Without moving his arms, he just drops the two masses. How will his angular speed change?

options.text: maybe less than, greater than or equal to $\omega $ depending on the quantity of the masses
it will be remain equal to $\omega $

correct_option_texts: it will be remain equal to $\omega $
questions.explanation: it will be remain equal to $\omega $

questions.question: A uniform sphere of mass 500 g rolls without slipping on a plane surface so that its centre moves at a speed of 0.02 m/s. The total kinetic energy of rolling sphere (in J) would be
questions.question_raw: A uniform sphere of mass 500 g rolls without slipping on a plane surface so that its centre moves at a speed of 0.02 m/s. The total kinetic energy of rolling sphere (in J) would be
questions.explanation:
Total K.E. of rolling sphere
$=\frac{7}{10} m v^{2}$
$=\frac{7}{10} \times \frac{500}{1000} \times(0.02)^{2}$
= 1.4 $\times$ 10^-4 J

questions.question: In our solar system, the inter-planetary region has chunks of matter (much smaller in size compared to planets) called asteroids. They
questions.question_raw: In our solar system, the inter-planetary region has chunks of matter (much smaller in size compared to planets) called asteroids. They

options.text: will move around the sun in closed orbits but not obey Kepler’s laws.
will move in an irregular way because of their small masses and will drift away into outer space.
will not move around the sun since they have very small masses compared to sun.
will move in orbits like planets and obey Kepler’s laws

correct_option_texts: will move in orbits like planets and obey Kepler’s laws
questions.explanation:
Like any other planet, asteroids also follow Kepler's law and move in elliptical orbits around the planet due to the central gravitational force acting on them.
a small mass of asteroid does not escape the gravitational force due to the sun because of the large mass of the sun.

questions.question: During inelastic collision between two bodies, which of the following quantities always remain conserved?
questions.question_raw: During inelastic collision between two bodies, which of the following quantities always remain conserved?
questions.explanation: Since no external forces are acting on the colliding bodies during a collision, thus total linear momentum is always conserved in all types of collisions.

questions.question: A particle moves along the x axis. Its position is given by the equation $x = 2.00 + 3.00t - 4.00{t^2}$ with x in meters and t in seconds. Determine its velocity in m/s when it returns to the position it had at t = 0.
questions.question_raw: A particle moves along the x axis. Its position is given by the equation {tex}x = 2.00 + 3.00t - 4.00{t^2}{/tex} with x in meters and t in seconds. Determine its velocity in m/s when it returns to the position it had at t = 0.
questions.explanation:
The given equation is $x = 2.00 + 3.00t - 4.00{t^2}$
At t = 0, we have x = 2,
$\therefore$ 2 = 2 + 3.0t - 4t^2
$t(3-4 t)=0\\ t=0~and ~t=\frac{3}{4}$
Now Velocity is given by , v = ${dx\over dt}= 3-8t$
= 3 - $8\times {3\over 4}$ = 3 - 6 = - 3 m/s
Negative sign shows direction of velocity is opposite.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements not true?
 The net acceleration of a particle in a circular motion is always along the radius of the circle towards the centre
 The acceleration vector of a particle in uniform circular motion averaged over one cycle is a null vector
 The net acceleration of a particle in uniform circular motion is always along the radius of the circle towards the centre
 The velocity vector of a particle at a point is always along the tangent to the path of the particle at that point.
questions.question_raw:
Which of the following statements not true?

  1. The net acceleration of a particle in a circular motion is always along the radius of the circle towards the centre
  2. The acceleration vector of a particle in uniform circular motion averaged over one cycle is a null vector
  3. The net acceleration of a particle in uniform circular motion is always along the radius of the circle towards the centre
  4. The velocity vector of a particle at a point is always along the tangent to the path of the particle at that point.
questions.explanation: The net acceleration of a particle in a circular motion is along the radius of the circle towards the centre Only in case of uniform circular motion.

questions.question: If maximum and minimum values of the resultant of two forces acting at a point are 7 N and 3 N respectively, the smaller force is equal to
questions.question_raw: If maximum and minimum values of the resultant of two forces acting at a point are 7 N and 3 N respectively, the smaller force is equal to
questions.explanation:
P + Q = 7 N and P - Q = 3 N
$\therefore$ P = 5 N, Q = 2 N
Thus smaller force is equal to 2 N.

questions.question: The earth circles around the sun once a year. The work which would have to be done on the earth to bring it: to rest relative to the sun is, (Ignore the rotation of earth about its own axis) given that the mass of the earth = 6 $\times$ 10^24 kg and distance between sun and earth is 1.5 $\times$ 10^8 km:
questions.question_raw: The earth circles around the sun once a year. The work which would have to be done on the earth to bring it: to rest relative to the sun is, (Ignore the rotation of earth about its own axis) given that the mass of the earth = 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 1024 kg and distance between sun and earth is 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 108 km:
questions.explanation:
$\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}$
$=\frac{2 \pi}{365 \times 24 \times 3600}$ = 1.99 $\times$ 10^-7 rad
W = K_f - K_i = 0 - $\frac{1}{2} $mv^2 (v = $\omega$R)
$=-\frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times 10^{24} \times\left(1.5 \times 10^{11} \times 1.99 \times 10^{-7}\right)^{2}$
= -2.7 $\times$ 10^33 J

questions.question: Which one is a vector quantity?
questions.question_raw: Which one is a vector quantity?
questions.explanation: Torque is a vector while energy is a scalar.

questions.question: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of a monkey running on the roof of the train A against its motion (with a velocity of 18 km/hr with respect to the train A) as observed by a man standing on the ground? Choose the positive direction of x-axis to be from the south to north.
questions.question_raw: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of a monkey running on the roof of the train A against its motion (with a velocity of 18 km/hr with respect to the train A) as observed by a man standing on the ground? Choose the positive direction of x-axis to be from the south to north.
questions.explanation:
Let the velocity of the monkey with respect to the ground be v_M. The relative velocity of the monkey with respect to A,
$v_{M A}=v_{M}-v_{A}=-18 k m h^{-1}=-5 m s^{-1}$
Therefore velocity of monkey will be given by , $v_{M}=(15-5) m s^{-1}=10 m s^{-1}$ [$\because v_{A}=+54 k m h^{-1}=15 m s^{-1}$]

questions.question: A cyclist is riding with a speed of 27 km/h. As he approaches a circular turn on the road of radius 80 m, he applies brakes and reduces his speed at the constant rate of 0.50 m/s every second. What is the magnitude and direction of the net acceleration of the cyclist on the circular turn?
questions.question_raw: A cyclist is riding with a speed of 27 km/h. As he approaches a circular turn on the road of radius 80 m, he applies brakes and reduces his speed at the constant rate of 0.50 m/s every second. What is the magnitude and direction of the net acceleration of the cyclist on the circular turn?

options.text: 0.82 ms^−2, 59.5^o with the direction of the velocity
0.86 ms^−2, 54.5^o with the direction of the velocity.
0.84 ms^−2, 58.5^o with the direction of the velocity
0.85 ms^−2, 56.5^o with the direction of the velocity.

correct_option_texts: 0.86 ms^−2, 54.5^o with the direction of the velocity.
questions.explanation:
Speed of the cyclist, v = 27 km/h = 7.5 m/s
The radius of the circular turn, r = 80 m
Centripetal acceleration is given as:
a_c = ${v^2\over r} ={ (7.5)^2 \over 80 }=$ 0.7 ms^-2
The situation is shown in the given figure:
Suppose the cyclist begins cycling from point P and moves toward point Q. At point Q, he applies the breaks and decelerates the speed of the bicycle by 0.5 m/s^2.
This acceleration is along the tangent at Q and opposite to the direction of the motion of the cyclist.
Since the angle between a_c and a_T is 90^0, the resultant acceleration is given by:
a = $\sqrt {(a_c)^2 + (a_T)^2} $ = $\sqrt{ (0.7)^2 + (0.5)^2 }$
= $\sqrt {0.74}$ = 0.86 ms^-2
tan $\theta $ = $a_c \over a_T$
where $\theta $ is the angle of the resultant with the direction of velocity.
tan $\theta $ = $0.7 \over 0.5 $= 1.4
$\theta $ = tan^-1(1.4) = 54.46^o with the direction of velocity.

questions.question: Two racing cars of masses m_1 and m_2 are moving in circles of radii r_1 and r_2 respectively. Their speeds are such that each car makes a complete circle in the same time t. The ratio of the angular speed of the first to the second car is
questions.question_raw: Two racing cars of masses m1 and m2 are moving in circles of radii r1 and r2 respectively. Their speeds are such that each car makes a complete circle in the same time t. The ratio of the angular speed of the first to the second car is
questions.explanation:
$\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}$
For both cars, period of revolution T is same, so their angular speed $\omega$ is also same.

questions.question: A scientific theory is accepted if
questions.question_raw: A scientific theory is accepted if

options.text: the theory is approved by a panel of people after studying the theory only.
predictions of the theory are confirmed by experiments.
the theory is derivable from other theories logically.
the theory is very simple elegant or very complicated.

correct_option_texts: predictions of the theory are confirmed by experiments.
questions.explanation: A scientific theory is an explanation of some aspect of the natural world that can, in accordance with the scientific method, be repeatedly tested, using a predefined protocol of observations and experiments.

questions.question: Light year is the unit of
questions.question_raw: Light year is the unit of
questions.explanation: One light-year is the distance traveled by light in one year.

questions.question: A helicopter of mass 1000 kg rises with a vertical acceleration of 15 ms^-2. The crew and the passengers weigh 300 kg. Give the magnitude and direction of the force on the helicopter due to the surrounding air.
questions.question_raw: A helicopter of mass 1000 kg rises with a vertical acceleration of 15 ms-2. The crew and the passengers weigh 300 kg. Give the magnitude and direction of the force on the helicopter due to the surrounding air.
questions.explanation:
Force on the helicopter due to the surrounding air is equal and opposite to the action of the rotor of the helicopter on the surrounding air.
R′−m_tg = m_ta
= m_t(g + a)
= 1300 $\times$ (10 + 15) = 32500N
Therefore Force on the surrounding air is = 32500 N in an upward direction.

questions.question: As observed from earth, the sun appears to move in an approximate circular orbit. For the motion of another planet like mercury as observed from earth, this would
questions.question_raw: As observed from earth, the sun appears to move in an approximate circular orbit. For the motion of another planet like mercury as observed from earth, this would

options.text: not be true because mercury is influenced by forces other than gravitational forces
not be true because the major gravitational force on mercury is due to sun
not be true because the force between earth and mercury is not inverse square law

correct_option_texts: not be true because the major gravitational force on mercury is due to sun
questions.explanation: As observed from the earth, the sun appears to move in an approximately circular orbit. The gravitational force of attraction between the earth and the sun always follows inverse square law. All planets move around the sun due to the huge gravitational force of the sun acting on them. The gravitational force on the mercury due to earth is much smaller as compared to that acting on it due to sun and hence it revolves around the sun and not around the earth.

questions.question: A uniform, solid, 1000.0-kg sphere has a radius of 5.00 m. Find the gravitational force this sphere exerts on a 2.00-kg point mass placed at a distance of 5.01 m from the center of the sphere?
questions.question_raw: A uniform, solid, 1000.0-kg sphere has a radius of 5.00 m. Find the gravitational force this sphere exerts on a 2.00-kg point mass placed at a distance of 5.01 m from the center of the sphere?
questions.explanation:
We know the gravitational force
$F=\frac{G M m}{r^{2}}$
Here G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^−11Nm^2kg^−2
M = 1000 kg
m = 2.0 kg
r = 5.01 m
$\Rightarrow F=\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 1000 \times 2}{(5.01)^{2}}$
$\Rightarrow F=\frac{13.34 \times 10^{8}}{25.10}$
$\Rightarrow$ F = $\frac{13.34 \times 10^{8}}{25.10}$
$\Rightarrow$ F = 0.531 $\times$ 10^−8 = 5.31 $\times$ 10^−9N

questions.question: Knowing that mass of moon is $\frac M{81}$ (where M is the mass of earth), find the distance of the point, where gravitational field due to earth and moon cancel each other. Given that the distance between the earth and moon is 60 R, where R is the radius of earth.
questions.question_raw: Knowing that mass of moon is {tex}\frac M{81}{/tex} (where M is the mass of earth), find the distance of the point, where gravitational field due to earth and moon cancel each other. Given that the distance between the earth and moon is 60 R, where R is the radius of earth.
questions.explanation:
Gravitational field due to earth at C = Gravitational field due to moon at C
$\frac{G M}{(60 R \ - \ x)^{2}}=\frac{G(\frac M{81})}{x^{2}}$
or 81x^2 = (60 R - x)^2
or 9x = 60R - x
or x = 6R

questions.explanation: For completing the vertical loop, the minimum speed required at the lowest point $=\sqrt{5 g R}$.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Impulse has the dimensions of momentum.
Reason (R): Impulse is directly proportional to force and time.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): Impulse has the dimensions of momentum.
Reason (R): Impulse is directly proportional to force and time.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is false but R is true.
A is true but R is false.

correct_option_texts: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation:
Impulse = $\Delta$p = change in momentum, hence it has the dimensions of momentum.
By Newton's second law F = $\frac{\Delta p}{\Delta t}$ so $\Delta$p = F$\Delta$t
$\because$ impulse = $\Delta$p $\Rightarrow$ impulse = F$\Delta$t
$\therefore$ impulse $\propto$ Force $\times$ time

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A safe turn by a cyclist should neither be fast nor sharp.
Reason (R): The bending angle from the vertical would decrease with an increase in velocity.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): A safe turn by a cyclist should neither be fast nor sharp.
Reason (R): The bending angle from the vertical would decrease with an increase in velocity.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.

correct_option_texts: A is true but R is false.
questions.explanation: During a turn tan $\theta=\frac{v^{2}}{r g}$, where $\theta$ is angle of bending with vertical, when v is large and r is small, tan $\theta$ increases. Therefore, as $\theta$ increases, so chances of skidding increase. Thus for a safe turn, $\theta$ should be small, for which v should be small and r should be large i.e. turning should be at a slow speed and along a track of larger radius.

questions.question: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the time in seconds at which the stone returns to the height from which it was thrown. g = 9.8 m/sec^2.
questions.question_raw: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the time in seconds at which the stone returns to the height from which it was thrown. g = 9.8 m/sec2.
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity u = 20.0 m/s
At maximum height stone will be stopped, 
So final velocity v = 0 m/s
Acceleration due to gravity a = g = -9.8 m/s^​​​​​​2 (-ve Because it is in opposite direction of motion)
Let maximum height = s
We know,
v^2 − u^2 = 2as
$\Rightarrow$ 0^2 − (20)^2 = 2 $\times$ ( − 9.8)s
$\Rightarrow$ − 400 = − 19.6s
$\Rightarrow s = {-400\over -19.6}$ = 20.4 m
Time to cover this distance upward is given by 
$\Rightarrow$ v − u = at
$\Rightarrow$ 0-20 = (-9.8)t
$\Rightarrow$ t = ${-20\over -9.8}$ = 2.04 sec 
Again to reach the same level from where it has been thrown it has to cover the same distance downward.
For this initial velocity u = 0 
s = 20.4 m
a = 9.8 m/s^​​​​​​2 (+ve because motion is in same direction).
So,
$\Rightarrow$ 20.4 = 0 $\times$ t + $1\over 2$ $\times$ 9.8t^2
$\Rightarrow t^2 = {20.4\over 4.9}= 4.16$ 
$\Rightarrow t =\sqrt {4.16}$ = 2.04 
Total time = 2.04 + 2.04 = 4.08 s

questions.question: A man of mass 70 kg stands on a weighing scale in a lift which is moving upwards with a uniform speed of 10 ms^−1, what would be the reading on the scale?
questions.question_raw: A man of mass 70 kg stands on a weighing scale in a lift which is moving upwards with a uniform speed of 10 ms−1, what would be the reading on the scale?
questions.explanation:
Given that mass of the man, m is = 70 kg
Acceleration, a = 0 
Using Newton’s second law of motion, 
we can write the equation of motion as:
R – mg = ma
Where ma is the net force acting on the man. 
As the lift is moving at a uniform speed, so the acceleration a = 0
$\therefore$ R = mg = 70 $\times$ 10 = 700 N

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:
Certain collisions are referred to as elastic collisions. Elastic collisions are collisions in which both momentum and kinetic energy are conserved. The total system kinetic energy before the collision equals the total system kinetic energy after the collision. If total kinetic energy is not conserved, then the collision is referred to as an inelastic collision.
The coefficient of restitution, denoted by (e), is the measure of degree elasticity of collision. It is defined as the ratio of the final to inital relative speed between two objects after they collide. It normally ranges from 0 to 1 where 1 would be a perfectly elastic collision. A perfectly inelastic collision has a coefficient of 0. In real life most of the collisions are neither perfectly elastic nor perfectly inealstic and 0 < e < 1.
The following are the data of a collision between a truck and a car.
Mass of the car = 1000 kg
Mass of the truck = 3000 kg
Mass of the truck Before collision:
Speed of the car = 20 m/s
Momentum of the car = 20000 kg m/s
Speed of the truck = 20 m/s
Momentum of the truck = 60000 kg m/s
After collision:
Speed of the car = 40 m/s in the opposite direction
Momentum of the car = 40000 kg m/s in the opposite direction
Speed of the truck = 0
Momentum of the truck = 0
The collision is

mock_tests.instruction:
The Question Paper contains three sections.
 Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any20 questions.
 Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions.
 All questions carry equal marks.
 There is no negative marking.

questions.question: Good and bad effects of a particular scientific/technological advance such as prenatal sex determination should, in general, be weighed by:
questions.question_raw: Good and bad effects of a particular scientific/technological advance such as prenatal sex determination should, in general, be weighed by:

options.text: well being and welfare of the originators of the advance
well being and welfare of the society
well being and welfare of the rulers
well being and welfare of the scientists

correct_option_texts: well being and welfare of the society
questions.explanation:
The advancement of new technology has been taking place since the beginning of human history. From the invention of items like the spear and knifes made out of rocks and sticks to aid in the capturing and killing of animals for food, to items like the first printing press and the computer.
With electronic technology and machines being produced and improved all the time, it was very likely that along with the positive aspects of these new advancements, people would also consider the negative aspects and look to criticise new technology. What society is taking up from the technology advances, it should be weighed by the well being and welfare of the society.

questions.question: What is the dimensional formula of gravitational constant G?
questions.question_raw: What is the dimensional formula of gravitational constant G?
questions.explanation:
$[G]=\frac{F r^{2}}{m_{1} m_{2}}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]\left[\mathrm{L}^{2}\right]}{[\mathrm{M}][\mathrm{M}]}$
= [M^-1L^3T^-2]

questions.question: A drag racer starts her car from rest and accelerates at 10.0 ${\rm{m}}/{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}$ for the entire distance of 400 m . What is the speed of the race car in m/s at the end of the run?
questions.question_raw: A drag racer starts her car from rest and accelerates at 10.0 {tex}{\rm{m}}/{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex} for the entire distance of 400 m . What is the speed of the race car in m/s at the end of the run?
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity, u = 0
Acceleration, a = 10.0 m/s^​​​​​​2
Distance covered, s = 400 m
Final velocity, v = ?
We know that, $v^2-u^2 = 2as$
$\Rightarrow v^2-0= 2\times 10\times 400$
$\Rightarrow 8000= v^2$
$\Rightarrow v= \sqrt {8000}= 89.4\ m/s$

questions.question: Centripetal acceleration of a particle moving in a circular path with constant velocity v is given by
questions.question_raw: Centripetal acceleration of a particle moving in a circular path with constant velocity v is given by

options.text: ${{2}}{{{v}}^{{2}}}/{{R}}$, where R is the radius of the circle.
${{2}}{{{v}}^{{2}}}{{R}}$, where R is the radius of the circle.
$\frac{v^2}{R}$, where R is the radius of the circle.
$v^2\times R$, where R is the radius of the circle.

correct_option_texts: $\frac{v^2}{R}$, where R is the radius of the circle.
questions.explanation: A body that moves in a circular motion (of radius R) at constant speed ($\vec v$) is always being accelerated. The acceleration is at right angles to the direction of motion (towards the centre of the circle) and of magnitude $\frac{v^2}{R}$

questions.question: The moment of inertia of a uniform circular disc of radius R and mass M about an axis touching the disc at its diameter and normal to the disc is
questions.question_raw: The moment of inertia of a uniform circular disc of radius R and mass M about an axis touching the disc at its diameter and normal to the disc is
questions.explanation:
By using the theorem of parallel axes, the moment of inertia of a disc about a tangent perpendicular to its plane,
$I_{T}^{\prime}=I_{C}+M R^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{2} M R^{2}+M R^{2}$
$=\frac{3}{2} M R^{2}$

questions.question: The reduced mass of two particles having masses m and 2m is
questions.question_raw: The reduced mass of two particles having masses m and 2m is
questions.explanation:
Reduced mass
$=\frac{m \times 2 m}{m+2 m}=\frac{2 m}{3}$

questions.question: If the radius of earth reduces by 4% and density remains the same then escape velocity will be:
questions.question_raw: If the radius of earth reduces by 4% and density remains the same then escape velocity will be:
questions.explanation:
Escape velocity v_e $\propto$R$\sqrt \rho$ and if density remains constant $\Rightarrow$ $ {v_e}{\text{ }}\propto$R
So if the radius reduces by 4% then escape velocity also reduces by 4%

questions.question: Among the four graphs, there is only one graph for which average velocity over the time interval (0, T) can vanish for a suitably chosen T. Which one is it?
questions.question_raw: Among the four graphs, there is only one graph for which average velocity over the time interval (0, T) can vanish for a suitably chosen T. Which one is it?
questions.explanation:
Main concept used: Average velocity of the body will be zero here is one displacement for different timings in any time interval-T in the x-t graph.
We need to identify the graph in which there is one displacement for different timings. It means that these displacements would be in opposite directions and when we add these opposite displacements, net displacement would be zero or average velocity would be zero. This thing is only possible in graph(b).
If we draw a line parallel to the time axis from point (A) on the graph at t = 0 sec. This line Can intersect the graph again at B. At this point, The change in displacement (O-T) time is zero i.e. displacement at A and B are equal so as the change in displacement is zero so the average velocity of the body vanishes to zero.

questions.explanation:
Initial Velocity v_o = 20 ms^-1 and $\theta = 50^°$|
Horizontal Range, R =$v_o^2sin2\theta\over g$ $={400\sin\ 100^°\over 9.8}$ = 40.2 m

2^2 = 2^2 + 2^2 + 2 $\times$ 2 $\times$ 2 cos $\theta$
$\cos \theta=-\frac{1}{2}$ or $\theta=120^{\circ}$

questions.question: A rocket is fired from the earth towards the sun. At what distance from the earth’s centre is the gravitational force on the rocket zero? Mass of the sun = 2 $ \times $ 10^30 kg, mass of the earth = 6 $ \times $ 10^24 kg. Neglect the effect of other planets etc. (orbital radius = 1.5 $ \times $ 10^11 m).
questions.question_raw: A rocket is fired from the earth towards the sun. At what distance from the earth’s centre is the gravitational force on the rocket zero? Mass of the sun = 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 1030 kg, mass of the earth = 6 {tex} \times {/tex} 1024 kg. Neglect the effect of other planets etc. (orbital radius = 1.5 {tex} \times {/tex} 1011 m).
questions.explanation:
Mass of the Sun,M_s = 2 $\times$ 10^30 kg
Mass of the Earth, M_e = 6 $\times$ 10^24 kg
Orbital radius, r = 1.5 $\times$ 10^11 m
Mass Of rocket = m
Let x be the distance from the centre of the earth where the gravitational forces acting on satellite P under the influence of Sun & the Earth as:
$G \frac{M_{s} m}{(r-x)^{2}}=G \frac{M_{e} m}{x^{2}}$
$[{(r-x) \over x}]^{2}=\frac{M_{s}}{M_{e}}$
$\frac{(r-x)}{x}=\left[\frac{2 \times 10^{30}}{6 \times 10^{24}}\right]^{\frac{1}{2}}$ = 577.35
$\Rightarrow$ 1.5 $\times$ 10^11 − x = 577.35x
$\Rightarrow$ 577.35x = 1.5 $\times$ 10^11
$\Rightarrow x=\frac{1.5 \times 10^{11}}{578.35}$ = 2.6 $\times$ 10^8m

questions.question: Strong Nuclear Force has:
questions.question_raw: Strong Nuclear Force has:
questions.explanation: Strong Nuclear Force: The nucleus is held by the forces which protect them from the enormous repulsion forces of the positive protons. It is a force with short-range and not similar to the electromagnetic force. We know that the nucleus is made up of its fundamental particles that are the protons and neutrons. These are formed with quarks that are held together with a strong force. This strong force is residual color force. The basic exchange particle is called gluon which works as mediator forces between quarks. Both the particles; gluons and quarks are present in protons and neutrons.

questions.question_raw: In SI system the fundamental units are

options.text: meter, kilogram, second, coulomb, Kelvin, mole, candela and horse power
meter, Newton, second, ampere, Kelvin, mole and candela
meter, kilogram, second, ampere, Kelvin, mole and watt
meter, kilogram, second, ampere, Kelvin, mole and candela

correct_option_texts: meter, kilogram, second, ampere, Kelvin, mole and candela

questions.question: A truck covers 40.0 m in 8.50 s while smoothly slowing down to a final speed of 2.80 m/s. Find its original speed in m/s:
questions.question_raw: A truck covers 40.0 m in 8.50 s while smoothly slowing down to a final speed of 2.80 m/s. Find its original speed in m/s:
questions.explanation:
Let initial velocity is given by = u
Final velocity is given by v = 2.80 m/s
Distance covered is ,s = 40.0 m
Time taken is, t = 8.50 s
We know,
v = u + at 
$\Rightarrow$ v-u = at .....(1)
Also 
$s = ut+{1\over 2}at^2$ 
From (1) put value of at, we get
$\Rightarrow$ s = ut + ${1\over 2}t(v-u)$ 
$\Rightarrow$ s = ut + ${1\over 2}tv-{1\over 2}ut$ 
$\Rightarrow$ s = ${1\over 2} ut+{1\over 2}tv$ 
Put all the given values, we get
$\Rightarrow$ 40 = $ {1\over 2}\times u\times 8.5+ {1\over 2}\times 2.8 \times 8.5$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 80 - 23.8 = 8.5u 
$\Rightarrow$ 8.5u = 56.2 
$\Rightarrow$ u = 6.61 m/s

questions.explanation: Vector addition is the operation of adding two or more vectors together into a vector sum. The so-called parallelogram law gives the rule for vector addition of two or more vectors. For two vectors, the vector sum is obtained by placing them head to tail and drawing the vector from the free tail to the free head.

questions.question: Considering a completely inelastic collision of a particle of mass ${{\text{m}}_{\text{1}}}$moving with initial velocity ${{\text{v}}_{\text{1}}}$ and a stationary particle of mass ${{\text{m}}_{\text{2}}}$. The ratio of final to initial kinetic energy is
questions.question_raw: Considering a completely inelastic collision of a particle of mass {tex}{{\text{m}}_{\text{1}}}{/tex}moving with initial velocity {tex}{{\text{v}}_{\text{1}}}{/tex} and a stationary particle of mass {tex}{{\text{m}}_{\text{2}}}{/tex}. The ratio of final to initial kinetic energy is
questions.explanation:
p_i = p_f
m_1v_1 + 0 = (m_1 + m_2)v
$v=\frac{m_1}{\left(m_1+m_2\right)} v_1$
$K_i=\frac{1}{2} m_1 v_1^2$
$K_f=\frac{1}{2}\left(m_1+m_2\right)\left(\frac{m_1}{\left(m_1+m_2\right)} v_1\right)^2$
$\frac{K_f}{K_i}=\frac{\frac{1}{2}\left(m_1+m_2\right)\left(\frac{m_1}{\left(m_1+m_2\right)} v_1\right)^2}{\frac{1}{2} m_1 v_1{ }^2}=\frac{m_1}{\left(m_1+m_2\right)}$

questions.question: A nucleus is at rest in the laboratory frame of reference. If it disintegrates into two smaller nuclei ______ .
questions.question_raw: A nucleus is at rest in the laboratory frame of reference. If it disintegrates into two smaller nuclei ______ .

options.text: the products must move in opposite directions
the products must move in same direction
the products must move at an angle in same direction
the products must move at an angle in opposite directions

correct_option_texts: the products must move in opposite directions
questions.explanation:
Let, M = mass of nucleus at rest. m_1 and m_2 are masses of two smaller nuclei. v_1_ and v_2 are the velocities of respective masses
Now, According to the law of conservation of momentum,
Initial momentum before disintegration = final momentum after disintegration
m_1v_1 + m_2v_2 = 0
$v_2=-\frac{m_2}{m_1}\cdot{v_1}$
As masses, m_1 and m_2 cannot be negative, v_1, and v_2 having opposite signs and so the two smaller nuclei move in opposite directions.

$\therefore h=\frac{r^{2} \omega^{2}}{2 g}$

questions.question: Determine the kinetic energy a 5000-kg spacecraft must have in order to escape the Earth’s gravitational field. Mass of the earth = 6.0 $ \times $ 10^24 kg; radius of the earth = 6.4 $ \times $ 10^6 m; G = 6.67 $ \times $ 10^-11 N m^2 kg^-2.
questions.question_raw: Determine the kinetic energy a 5000-kg spacecraft must have in order to escape the Earth’s gravitational field. Mass of the earth = 6.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 1024 kg; radius of the earth = 6.4 {tex} \times {/tex} 106 m; G = 6.67 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-11 N m2 kg-2.
questions.explanation:
Kinetic energy required for spacecraft to escape the earth′s gravitational field
$=\frac{1}{2} m_{s} v_{e s c}^{2}$
We know, $V_{e s c}=\sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}}$
Here G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^-11Nm^2kg^-2
M = 6 $\times$ 10^24 kg
R = 6.4 $\times$ 10^6 m
$\Rightarrow V_{e s c}=\sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}}$
$=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 6 \times 10^{24}}{6.4 \times 10^{6}}}$
$=\sqrt{\frac{12 \times 6.67 \times 10^{7}}{6.4}}$
= 1.12 $\times$ 10^4 m/sec. Kinetic energy required for spacecraft to escape the earth′s gravitational field = ${1\over 2}m_sv^2_{esc}$
Here m_s = mass of spacecraft = 5000 kg
$\therefore$ K.E = (1/2) $\times$ 5000 $\times$ (1.12 $\times$ 10^4 )^2J
$\Rightarrow$ K.E = 2500 $\times$( 1.12 $\times$ 10^4 )^2J
$\Rightarrow$ K.E = 3.14 $\times$ 10^11 J

questions.explanation:
Total distance covered s = 1000 m
Total time taken t = 100 - 0 = 100 s
Average speed, $v_{avg}={total \ distance \ covered\over total \ time \ taken}$ $= {1000\over 100}$ = 10 m/s

questions.question: A ball rolls without slipping. The radius of gyration of the ball about an axis passing through its centre of mass is k. If radius of the ball be R, then the fraction of total energy associated with its rotational energy will be
questions.question_raw: A ball rolls without slipping. The radius of gyration of the ball about an axis passing through its centre of mass is k. If radius of the ball be R, then the fraction of total energy associated with its rotational energy will be
questions.explanation:
$E_{r o t}=\frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{2} M k^{2}\left(\frac{v}{R}\right)^{2}$
$E_{\text {tot }}=E_{\text {tran }}+E_{\text {rot }}$
$=\frac{1}{2} M v^{2}+\frac{1}{2} M k^{2}\left(\frac{v}{R}\right)^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{2} M v^{2}\left(1+\frac{k^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)$
$\frac{E_{r o t}}{E_{\text {tot }}}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} M v^{2}\left(\frac{k^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)}{\frac{1}{2} M v^{2}\left(1+\frac{k^{2}}{R^{2}}\right)}$
$=\frac{k^{2}}{k^{2}+R^{2}}$

At the lowest point 
${F_{net}} = {T_1} - mg = \frac{{m{v_1}^2} }{ R}$ 
at the highest point 
${F_{net}} = {T_2} + mg = \frac{{m{v_2}^2}}{ R}$

questions.question: Strong Nuclear Force is:
questions.question_raw: Strong Nuclear Force is:

options.text: the strongest of all fundamental forces, about 100 times the gravitational force in strength.
the strongest of all fundamental forces, about 10 times the electromagnetic force in strength.
the strongest of all fundamental forces, about 100 times the electromagnetic force in strength.
the strongest of all fundamental forces, about 10 times the gravitational force in strength.

correct_option_texts: the strongest of all fundamental forces, about 100 times the electromagnetic force in strength.
questions.explanation: In particle physics, the strong interaction is the mechanism responsible for the strong nuclear force (also called the strong force or nuclear strong force), and is one of the four known fundamental interactions, with the others being electromagnetism, the weak interaction, and gravitation. At the range of 10^−15 m (femtometer), the strong force is approximately 100 times as strong as electromagnetism, a million times as strong as the weak interaction, and 10^38 times as strong as gravitation.

questions.explanation: The smallest value that can be measured by the measuring instrument is called its least count. Measured values are good only up to this value. The least count error is the error associated with the resolution of the instrument.

questions.question: An object of mass 3 kg is at rest. Now a force of $\vec{F}=6 t^{2} \hat{i}+4 t \hat{j}$ is applied on the object. Then velocity of object at t = 3 is
questions.question_raw: An object of mass 3 kg is at rest. Now a force of {tex}\vec{F}=6 t^{2} \hat{i}+4 t \hat{j}{/tex} is applied on the object. Then velocity of object at t = 3 is
questions.explanation:
$\vec{a}=\frac{\vec{F}}{m}=\frac{1}{3}\left(6 t^{2} \hat{i}+4 t \hat{j}\right)=\frac{d \vec{v}}{d t}$
$d \vec{v}=\frac{1}{3}\left(6 t^{2} \hat{i}+4 t j\right) d t$
Velocity at t = 3s will be
$\vec{v}=\int d \vec{v}=\frac{1}{3} \int\limits_{0}^{3}\left(6 t^{2} \hat{i}+4 t \hat{j}\right) d t$
$=\frac{1}{3}\left|2 t^{3} \hat{i}+2 t^{2} \hat{j}\right|_{0}^{3}=\frac{2}{3}\left(3^{3} \hat{i}+3^{2} \hat{j}\right)$
$=(18 \hat{i}+6 \hat{j}) \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$

questions.question: Derive an expression for the work required to move an Earth satellite of mass m from a circular orbit of radius 2 ${{\rm{R}}_{\rm{E}}}$ to one of radius 3 ${{\rm{R}}_{\rm{E}}}$.
questions.question_raw: Derive an expression for the work required to move an Earth satellite of mass m from a circular orbit of radius 2 {tex}{{\rm{R}}_{\rm{E}}}{/tex} to one of radius 3 {tex}{{\rm{R}}_{\rm{E}}}{/tex}.
questions.explanation:
The energy of a satellite in low orbit:
$E_{L}=\frac{-G M m}{2 r}=\frac{-G M_{E} m}{2\left(2 R_{E}\right)}=\frac{-G M_{E} m}{4 R_{E}} $
The energy of a satellite in high orbit:
$$$E_{H}=\frac{-G M m}{2 r}=\frac{-G M_{E} m}{2\left(3 R_{E}\right)}=\frac{-G M_{E} m}{6 R_{E}}$
Energy required to move a satellite from the orbit of radius 2R_E to orbit of radius 3R_E:
$=\Delta E$ = E_H − E_L = $\frac{-G M_{E} m}{6 R_{E}}-\left(\frac{-G M_{E} m}{4 R_{E}}\right) \Rightarrow \Delta E=\frac{G M_{E} m}{12 R_{E}}$

questions.question: A metre scale is moving with uniform velocity. This implies
questions.question_raw: A metre scale is moving with uniform velocity. This implies

options.text: neither the force nor the torque need to be zero.
the total force acting on it need not be zero but the torque on it is zero.
the force acting on the scale is zero, but a torque about the centre of mass can act on the scale.
the force acting on the scale is zero and the torque acting about centre of mass of the scale is also zero.

correct_option_texts: the force acting on the scale is zero and the torque acting about centre of mass of the scale is also zero.
questions.explanation:
The question, a metre scale is moving with a uniform velocity which means the velocity of meter scale is constant also we know the change in constant quantity is zero so the rate of change of velocity (i.e. acceleration) will also be zero.
Now from Newton's 2^nd law i.e. $\vec{F}$_net = m$\vec{a}$ but we have a(acceleration) is zero i.e. $\vec{a}$ = 0, so. $\vec{F}$_net
= m $\times$ 0 = 0
Now torque is the cross product of position vector and force vector also we have force vector, in this case, is zero i.e. $\vec{F}$ net = 0
And torque is $\vec{\tau}=\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ so putting force vector value
$\vec{\tau}=r F \sin \theta$ = r(0) sin $\theta$ = 0

questions.question:
Assertion (A): As the frictional force increases, the safe velocity limit for taking a turn on an unbanked road also increases.
Reason (R): Banking of roads will increase the value of limiting velocity.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): As the frictional force increases, the safe velocity limit for taking a turn on an unbanked road also increases.
Reason (R): Banking of roads will increase the value of limiting velocity.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

correct_option_texts: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation:
On an unbanked road, friction provides the necessary centripetal force,
$\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=F=\mu R=\mu m g$
$\therefore \quad v=\sqrt{\mu r g}$
Thus, with increase in friction, safe velocity also increases. When the road is banked with angle of $\theta$, then its limiting velocity is its given by,
$v=\sqrt{\frac{r g(\tan \theta+\mu)}{1-\mu \tan \theta}}$
Thus, limiting velocity increases with banking of road.

questions.question: If the coefficient of static friction between the tyres and road is 0.5, what is the shortest distance in which an automobile can be stopped when travelling at 72 km/h?
questions.question_raw: If the coefficient of static friction between the tyres and road is 0.5, what is the shortest distance in which an automobile can be stopped when travelling at 72 km/h?
questions.explanation:
Retardation, a = $\mu$ g = 0.5 $\times$ 9.8 ms^-2
u = 72 km h^-1 = 20 ms^-1
v^2 - u^2 = 2 as
0 - (20)^2 = -2 $\times$ 0.5 $\times$ 9.8s
$s=\frac{20 \times 20}{2 \times 0.5 \times 9.8}$ = 40.8 m
