mock_tests.instruction:
The Question Paper contains three sections.
 Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.
 Section B has 24 questions. Attempt any20 questions.
 Section C has 6 questions. Attempt any 5 questions.
 All questions carry equal marks.
 There is no negative marking.

questions.question: The major contribution of Paul Dirac was in:
questions.question_raw: The major contribution of Paul Dirac was in:
questions.explanation:
Paul Dirac was one of the greatest theoretical physicists in history. He completely reshaped quantum mechanics with the astounding Dirac Equation.
The Dirac Equation explained the behaviour of electrons and foretold the existence of antimatter. Dirac was also able to infer the existence of vacuum polarization, revealing that what we once believed was empty space is actually awash with short-lived particle-antiparticle pairs.

questions.question: The number of significant digits in 2,076 is
questions.question_raw: The number of significant digits in 2,076 is
questions.explanation:
There are three rules on determining how many significant figures are in a number: 
 Non-zero digits are always significant.
 Any zeros between two significant digits are significant.
 A final zero or trailing zeros in the decimal portion ONLY are significant.
Keeping these rules in mind, we can say that there are 4 significant digits.

questions.question: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. The height of the building is 50.0 m. Determine the velocity in m/sec when the stone hits the ground. (g =9.8 m/${\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}$)
questions.question_raw: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. The height of the building is 50.0 m. Determine the velocity in m/sec when the stone hits the ground. (g =9.8 m/{tex}{\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex})
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity is given by u = 20 m/s
Final velocity before hitting ground is given by = v
Distance covered is, s = 50 m
Acceleration due to gravity, a = 9.8 m/s^​​​​​​2
We know that, $v^2-u^2= 2as$
$\Rightarrow v^2-(-20)^2= 2\times 9.8\times 50$
$\Rightarrow v^2-400= 980$
$\Rightarrow v^2= 1380$
$\Rightarrow v= \sqrt {1380}= \pm 37.1$
As this velocity is in the opposite direction, so v = - 37.1 m/s

questions.question: We can define the difference of two vectors A and B as the sum of two vectors A and B' such that B' is equal to B multiplied by
questions.question_raw: We can define the difference of two vectors A and B as the sum of two vectors A and B' such that B' is equal to B multiplied by
questions.explanation:
Vector subtraction is defined in the following way.
 The difference of two vectors, A - B , is a vector C that is, C = A - B
 The addition of two vector such that C = A + (-B). B has been taken in opposite direction.
Thus vector subtraction can be represented as a vector addition.

questions.question: If C and R denote capacitance and resistance, the dimensional formula of CR is
questions.question_raw: If C and R denote capacitance and resistance, the dimensional formula of CR is

options.text: not expressible in terms of MLT

questions.explanation:
$[C R]=\frac{q}{V} \cdot \frac{V}{I}=\frac{q}{I}$
= $\frac{[\mathrm{IT}]}{[\mathrm{I}]}$ = [M^0L^0T^1]

questions.question: Analogue of mass in rotational motion is:
questions.question_raw: Analogue of mass in rotational motion is:
questions.explanation: The moment of inertia is a physical quantity that tells how easily a body can be rotated about a given axis. It is a rotational analog of mass. It plays the same role in rotational motion as 'inertia' does in translational motion. A large moment of inertia also helps in keeping the rotation motion uniform.

questions.question: The centre of mass of a system of particles does not depend on
questions.question_raw: The centre of mass of a system of particles does not depend on
questions.explanation: The centre of mass of a system of particles does not depend on the forces acting on the particles.

questions.question: The total energy of a circularly orbiting satellite is
questions.question_raw: The total energy of a circularly orbiting satellite is
questions.explanation:
When a satellite revolves around a planet in its orbit, it possesses both potential energy (due to its position against the gravitational pull of earth) and kinetic energy (due to orbital motion).
$ \begin{gathered} {\text{Total Energy = Potential Energy(U) + Kinetic Energy(K)}} \hfill \\ {\text{ = }}\frac{{ - GMm}}{r} + \frac{{GMm}}{{2r}} \hfill \\ {\text{ = }}\frac{{ - GMm}}{{2r}} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} $
Hence we can say that total energy of circularly orbiting satellite is negative.

questions.question: A jet airplane travelling at the speed of 500 kmh^–1 ejects its products of combustion at the speed of 1500 km h^–1 relative to the jet plane. What is the speed of the combustion with respect to an observer on ground?
questions.question_raw: A jet airplane travelling at the speed of 500 kmh–1 ejects its products of combustion at the speed of 1500 km h–1 relative to the jet plane. What is the speed of the combustion with respect to an observer on ground?
questions.explanation:
Speed of the jet airplane, v_jet = 500 km/h
Relative speed of its products of combustion with respect to the plane, v_smoke = – 1500 km/h
Speed of its products of combustion with respect to the ground = v′_smoke
Relative speed of its products of combustion with respect to the airplane, v_smoke = v′_smoke – v_jet
$\Rightarrow$ – 1500 = v′_smoke – 500
$\Rightarrow$ v′_smoke = – 1000 km/h
The negative sign indicates that the direction of its products of combustion is opposite to the direction of motion of the jet airplane.

questions.question: As a ball rises, the vertical component of its velocity
questions.question_raw: As a ball rises, the vertical component of its velocity
questions.explanation: If a ball is thrown upward at an angle, the ball has a vertical and a horizontal component of velocity. The force of gravity is acting on it and its acceleration is in the downward direction. The vertical component of velocity is therefore changing. As the motion and acceleration is in opposite direction so vertical component of velocity decrease.

questions.question: A helicopter of mass 1000 kg rises with a vertical acceleration of 15 ms^-2. The crew and the passengers weigh 300 kg. Give the magnitude and direction of the action of the rotor of the helicopter on the surrounding air.
questions.question_raw: A helicopter of mass 1000 kg rises with a vertical acceleration of 15 ms-2. The crew and the passengers weigh 300 kg. Give the magnitude and direction of the action of the rotor of the helicopter on the surrounding air.
questions.explanation:
The reaction of the rotor on the surrounding air will be due to the mass of the helicopter as well as the passengers.
Let, the mass of helicopter, M = 1000 kg
Mass of crew and passengers, m = 300 kg
acceleration in upward direction a = 15 m s^-2
Action of the rotor of the helicopter on the surrounding air is
= (M + m)(g + a)
= (1000 + 300)(10 + 15)
= 32500N downward direction
Thus, the reaction force of the rotor on the surrounding air will be 32500 N

questions.question: In which case is the work done zero?
questions.question_raw: In which case is the work done zero?

options.text: Force and displacement are perpendicular to each other
Force and displacement are in the same direction
Force and displacement are at an angle of 75$^\circ $
Force and displacement are at an angle of 45$^\circ $

correct_option_texts: Force and displacement are perpendicular to each other
questions.explanation:
Work done, W = Fdcos$\theta$
where $\theta$ is the angle between F and d. If both are perpendicular then $\theta$ = 90 degrees, so cos$\theta$ = 0 and thus work done is zero.

questions.question: A wheel is rotating about an axis through its centre at 720 r.p.m. When acted upon by a constant torque opposing its motion for 8 seconds it stops rotating. The value of this torque in Nm is (given I = $\frac {24} {\pi }$ kg ${{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}$)
questions.question_raw: A wheel is rotating about an axis through its centre at 720 r.p.m. When acted upon by a constant torque opposing its motion for 8 seconds it stops rotating. The value of this torque in Nm is (given I = {tex}\frac {24} {\pi }{/tex} kg {tex}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex})
questions.explanation:
$n = \frac {720} {60} = 12$ rev/s
angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi n $ = $ 2\pi \times 12 = 24\pi $ rad/s
moment of inertia 
I =$\frac {24}{ \pi }$kg ${{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}$
torque
$T = I\alpha $
T = $ I\frac{{\Delta \omega }}{{\Delta t}} = \frac{{24}}{\pi } \times \left( {\frac{{24 \pi- 0}}{8}} \right)$ = $ \frac{{24}}{\pi } \times \frac{{24\pi }}{8}$ = 72.0Nm

questions.question: On October 15, 2001, a planet was discovered orbiting around the star HD 68988. Its orbital distance was measured to be 10.5 million kilometers from the center of the star, and its orbital period was estimated at 6.3 days. What is the mass of HD 68988?
questions.question_raw: On October 15, 2001, a planet was discovered orbiting around the star HD 68988. Its orbital distance was measured to be 10.5 million kilometers from the center of the star, and its orbital period was estimated at 6.3 days. What is the mass of HD 68988?
questions.explanation:
Following is the equation for the period T:
$T=\frac{2 \pi r^{3 / 2}}{\sqrt{G m}}$ or $T^{2}=\frac{4 \pi^{2} r^{3}}{G m}$
Solving this equation for the mass m, we get mass of the star HD68988 as follows:
$m=\frac{4 \pi^{2} r^{3}}{T^{2} G}=\frac{4 \pi^{2}\left(10.5 \times 10^{9} m\right)^{3}}{\left(5.443 \times 10^{5} s\right)^{2}\left(6.673 \times 10^{-11} N . m^{2} / k g^{2}\right)}$ = 2.3 $\times$ 10^30kg

questions.question: The sun releases energy coming from:
questions.question_raw: The sun releases energy coming from:
questions.explanation:
In addition to intense heat, there is an incredible amount of pressure at the Sun's core. In fact, the vast amounts of hydrogen atoms in the Sun's core are compressed and heated so much that they fuse together.
This reaction, known as nuclear fusion, converts hydrogen atoms into helium. The by-product of nuclear fusion in the Sun's core is a massive volume of energy that gets released and radiates outward toward the surface of the Sun and then into the solar system beyond it.

questions.question: The dimensions of strain are:
questions.question_raw: The dimensions of strain are:
questions.explanation: Strain is a dimensionless physical quantity.

questions.question: A jet plane lands with a speed of 100 m/s and can accelerate at a maximum rate of -5.00 ${\rm{m}}/{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}$ as it comes to rest. From the instant the plane touches the runway, what is the minimum time in seconds before it can come to rest?
questions.question_raw: A jet plane lands with a speed of 100 m/s and can accelerate at a maximum rate of -5.00 {tex}{\rm{m}}/{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex} as it comes to rest. From the instant the plane touches the runway, what is the minimum time in seconds before it can come to rest?
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity, u = 100 m/s
As it stops so final velocity, v = 0
Acceleration a = - 5 m/s^​​​​​​2
We know, v - u = at
$\Rightarrow$ $ t = {v-u\over a}$
$\Rightarrow$ $ t = {0-100\over -5}={-100\over -5}$
$\Rightarrow$ t = 20.0 s

questions.question: If particles A and B are moving with velocities $\vec v_A$ and $\vec v_B$ (each with respect to some common frame of reference, say ground.). Then, velocity of particle A relative to that of B is:
questions.question_raw: If particles A and B are moving with velocities {tex}\vec v_A{/tex} and {tex}\vec v_B{/tex} (each with respect to some common frame of reference, say ground.). Then, velocity of particle A relative to that of B is:
questions.explanation:
The relative velocity of an object A with respect to another object B is the velocity that object A would appear to have to an observer situated on object B moving along with it. In simple words relative velocity of A with respect to B is the vector difference between the velocities of A and B. It is represented as 
$\vec v_{AB}=$ $\vec v_A ~-​​​\vec v_B$

questions.question: A rocket with a lift-off mass 3.5 $\times$ 10^4 kg is blast upward with an initial acceleration of 10 m/s^2. Then, the initial thrust of the blast is
questions.question_raw: A rocket with a lift-off mass 3.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 kg is blast upward with an initial acceleration of 10 m/s2. Then, the initial thrust of the blast is
questions.explanation:
Initial thrust = m(a + g)
= 3.5 $\times$ 10^4(10 + 10)N
= 7.0 $\times$ 10^5 N

questions.question: A body is moving unidirectionally under the influence of a source of constant power. Its displacement in time t is proportional to
questions.question_raw: A body is moving unidirectionally under the influence of a source of constant power. Its displacement in time t is proportional to
questions.explanation:
We know that
Power, P = Fv = k (constant)
Considering the dimensions,
[P] = [F][v]
$\Rightarrow$ [P] = [MLT^-2][LT^-1]
$\Rightarrow$ [P] = [ML^2T^-3]
So, [ML^2T^-3] = k
$\Rightarrow$ [L^2T^-3] = k ...[M is constant]
$\Rightarrow$ [L^2/T^3] = k
$\Rightarrow$ [L^2] = k[T^3]
$\Rightarrow$ [L^2] $\propto$ [T^3]
$\Rightarrow$ [L] $\propto$ [T^3/2]
 Hence, L $\propto$ t^3/2

questions.question: In the pure translational motion of a rigid body:
questions.question_raw: In the pure translational motion of a rigid body:

options.text: at any instant of time, every particle of the body has the same velocity.
at any instant of time different particles of the body have different velocities.
at different instants of time, every particle of the body has the same velocity.
at any instant of time velocity is dependent on the position vector of a point on the body.

correct_option_texts: at any instant of time, every particle of the body has the same velocity.
questions.explanation: In translational motion when the body moves along a straight line or more exactly when every point of the body travels on parallel lines, thus at any instant of time every particle of the body has the same velocity.

questions.question: The asteroid Toro has a radius of about 5.0 km. Assuming that the density of Toro is the same as that of the earth (5.5 g/cm3) find its total mass and find the acceleration due to gravity at its surface. Mass of the earth = 6.0 $ \times $ 10^24 kg; radius of the earth = 6.4 $ \times $ 10^6 m; G = 6.67 $ \times $ 10^−11 N m^2 kg^-2.
questions.question_raw: The asteroid Toro has a radius of about 5.0 km. Assuming that the density of Toro is the same as that of the earth (5.5 g/cm3) find its total mass and find the acceleration due to gravity at its surface. Mass of the earth = 6.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 1024 kg; radius of the earth = 6.4 {tex} \times {/tex} 106 m; G = 6.67 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−11 N m2 kg-2.
questions.explanation:
We can calculate the mass of Toro by mass density relationship:
 Mass = Volume $\times$ Density
 $\Rightarrow M_{t o r o}=\frac{4}{3} \pi R_{t o r o}^{3} \times \rho_{t o r o}$
 Here R_toro = 5.0 km = 5000 m
 ρtoro = 5.5 g/cm^3 = 55 $\times$ 10^2kg/m3
 $\Rightarrow M_{t o r o}=\frac{4}{3} \pi(5000)^{3} \times 55 \times 10^{2}$
 $\Rightarrow M_{\text {toro}}=\frac{4}{3} \times \frac{22}{7} \times 125 \times 10^{9} \times 55 \times 10^{2}$
 $\Rightarrow$ M_toro = 2.9 $\times$ 10^15 kg
 we can find the acceleration due to gravity at the surface of Toro by the following relation:
 $$$g=\frac{G M_{\text {toro}}}{R_{\text {toro}}^{2}}$
 $\Rightarrow g=\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 2.9 \times 10^{15}}{(5000)^{2}}$
 $\Rightarrow g=\frac{19.34 \times 10^{4}}{25 \times 10^{6}}$
 $\Rightarrow$ g = 0.77 $\times$ 10^−2 = 0.0077 m/sec^2.

questions.question: The position of an object moving in a straight line can be specified with reference to
questions.question_raw: The position of an object moving in a straight line can be specified with reference to
questions.explanation: To describe motion along a straight line, we can choose an axis, say X-axis, so that it coincides with the path of the object. We then measure the position of the object with reference to a conveniently chosen origin, say O. Positions to the right of O are taken as positive and to the left of O, as negative.

questions.question: Which of the following physical quantities is a vector?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following physical quantities is a vector?
questions.explanation: Since force is a vector quantity, impulse is also a vector in the same direction. Impulse applied to an object produces an equivalent vector change in its linear momentum, also in the same direction. The SI unit of impulse is the newton second (Ns)

questions.question: A body of mass 5 kg is acted upon by two perpendicular forces 8 N and 6 N. Give the magnitude and direction of the net force acting on the body.
questions.question_raw: A body of mass 5 kg is acted upon by two perpendicular forces 8 N and 6 N. Give the magnitude and direction of the net force acting on the body.

options.text: 16 N at an angle of 46^o to the 8 N force
12 N at an angle of 38^o to the 8 N force
10 N at an angle of 36^o52^o towards the 8 N force
14 N at an angle of 37^o to the 8 N force

correct_option_texts: 10 N at an angle of 36^o52^o towards the 8 N force
questions.explanation:
Let F_1 = 8N & F_2 = 6 N. 
then the resultant force is $F=\sqrt{F_1^2+F_2^2}$ 
$\sqrt{8^2+6^2}$
= 10N
The direction is determined as $tan\theta=\frac{F_2}{F_1}$ 
= $6\over8$ 
therefore $\theta$ = 36^o52^'

questions.question: A solid sphere of radius R is placed on smooth horizontal surface. A horizontal force F is applied at height h from the lowest point. For the maximum acceleration of centre of mass, which is correct?
questions.question_raw: A solid sphere of radius R is placed on smooth horizontal surface. A horizontal force F is applied at height h from the lowest point. For the maximum acceleration of centre of mass, which is correct?

options.text: no relation between h and R

correct_option_texts: no relation between h and R
questions.explanation: The smooth horizontal surface does not provide any friction. The sphere does not roll. Hence the acceleration of CM of the sphere is independent of the position of the applied force. There is no relation between h and R.

questions.question: A uniform metallic rod rotates about its perpendicular bisector with constant angular speed. If it is heated uniformly to raise its temperature slightly, then
questions.question_raw: A uniform metallic rod rotates about its perpendicular bisector with constant angular speed. If it is heated uniformly to raise its temperature slightly, then

options.text: its speed of rotation remains same
its speed increases because its moment of inertia increases

questions.explanation: When the metallic rod is heated, it expands. The distances of the particles from the axis of rotation increase. So the moment of inertia increases but the speed of rotation decreases so as to conserve the angular momentum (L= $\omega$ = constant).

questions.question: For a planet having a mass equal to the mass of the earth, the radius is one-fourth of the radius of the earth. Then escape velocity for this planet will be:
questions.question_raw: For a planet having a mass equal to the mass of the earth, the radius is one-fourth of the radius of the earth. Then escape velocity for this planet will be:
questions.explanation:
v_e = $\sqrt{2 g R}=\sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}}$
if R is $\frac 14$th then v_e = 2v_e−earth = 2 $\times$ 11.2 = 22.4 km/sec

questions.question: A 6.0-kg block initially at rest is pulled to the right along a horizontal frictionless surface by a constant horizontal force of 12 N. Find the speed of the block after it has moved 3.0 m?
questions.question_raw: A 6.0-kg block initially at rest is pulled to the right along a horizontal frictionless surface by a constant horizontal force of 12 N. Find the speed of the block after it has moved 3.0 m?
questions.explanation:
From work kinetic energy theorem
K.E. = $\frac{1}{2}$m v^2
$\Delta$K = W
$\frac{1}{2}$mv^2 - $\frac{1}{2}$mu^2 = Fs
$\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times v^{2}\right)$ − 0 = 12 $\times$ 3
3 v^2 = 36
v^2 = 12
v = 3.5m/sec

questions.question: At a metro station, a girl walks up a stationary escalator in time t_1. If she remains stationary on the escalator, then the escalator take her up in time t_2. The time taken by her to walk up on the moving escalator will be
questions.question_raw: At a metro station, a girl walks up a stationary escalator in time t1. If she remains stationary on the escalator, then the escalator take her up in time t2. The time taken by her to walk up on the moving escalator will be
questions.explanation:
Let L be the length of the escalator.
Velocity of girl w.r.t. ground v_g = $\frac{L}{t_{1}}$
Velocity of escalator w.r.t. ground v_e = $\frac{L}{t_{2}}$
Effective Velocity of girl on moving escalator with respect to ground = v_g + v_e = $\frac{L}{t_{1}}+\frac{L}{t_{2}}=L\left[\frac{1}{t_{1}}+\frac{1}{t_{2}}\right]$
v_ge = $L\left[\frac{t_{1}+t_{2}}{t_{1} t_{2}}\right]$
Time t taken by girl on moving escalator in going up the distance Lis
t = $\frac{d i s \tan c e}{s p e e d}=\frac{L}{L\left(\frac{t_{1}+t_{2}}{t_{1} t_{2}}\right)}=\frac{t_{1} t_{2}}{t_{1}+t_{2}}$

questions.question: To find the sum of vectors $\vec A$ and $\vec B$, we place vector $\vec B$ so that its
questions.question_raw: To find the sum of vectors {tex}\vec A{/tex} and {tex}\vec B{/tex}, we place vector {tex}\vec B{/tex} so that its

options.text: head is at the head of the vector $\vec A$
direction is the same as that of vector $\vec A$
tail is at the tail of the vector $\vec A$
tail is at the head of the vector $\vec A$

correct_option_texts: tail is at the head of the vector $\vec A$
questions.explanation:
Triangle law of vector addition states that when two vectors are represented as two sides of the triangle with the order of magnitude and direction, then the third side of the triangle represents the magnitude and direction of the resultant vector. Thus resultant vector is R= a+ b
Taken in same order mean tail of Vector B should be placed at the head of vector A as shown in image.

questions.question: When bodies are in contact with each other then:
questions.question_raw: When bodies are in contact with each other then:

options.text: contact forces are equal and opposite in direction
contact forces are not equal and opposite in direction
contact forces are not equal and in the same direction
contact forces are equal and in the same direction

correct_option_texts: contact forces are equal and opposite in direction
questions.explanation: according to newton's third law of motion for every action, there is always equal and opposite reaction or the mutual actions of two bodies upon each other. Whenever two objects interact with each other, then the pair of forces acting on them are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction. Contact forces are mutual forces (action and reaction).

questions.question: Two rings of radii R and nR made from the same wire have the ratio of moments of inertia about an axis passing through their centre equal to 1 : 8. The value of n is
questions.question_raw: Two rings of radii R and nR made from the same wire have the ratio of moments of inertia about an axis passing through their centre equal to 1 : 8. The value of n is
questions.explanation:
As radius of second ring is n times, length and hence mass of wire used is also n times.
$\therefore \frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\frac{M R^{2}}{n M(n R)^{2}}=\frac{1}{n^{3}}=\frac{1}{8}$ $\therefore n=2$

questions.question: A planet is revolving around the sun in an elliptical orbit. The maximum and the minimum distances of the planet from the sun are 3 $ \times $ ${\rm{1}}{0^{{\rm{12}}}}$ m and 2 $ \times $ ${\rm{1}}{0^{{\rm{10}}}}$ m respectively. The speed of the planet when it is nearest to sun is 2 $ \times $ ${\rm{1}}{0^{{\rm{7}}}}$ m/sec. What is the speed of the planet when it is farthest from the sun?
questions.question_raw: A planet is revolving around the sun in an elliptical orbit. The maximum and the minimum distances of the planet from the sun are 3 {tex} \times {/tex} {tex}{\rm{1}}{0^{{\rm{12}}}}{/tex} m and 2 {tex} \times {/tex} {tex}{\rm{1}}{0^{{\rm{10}}}}{/tex} m respectively. The speed of the planet when it is nearest to sun is 2 {tex} \times {/tex} {tex}{\rm{1}}{0^{{\rm{7}}}}{/tex} m/sec. What is the speed of the planet when it is farthest from the sun?
questions.explanation:
Perihelion is the nearest distance of planet from focus.
aphelion is the farthest distance of planet from focus.
v_p = 2 $\times$ 10^7m/s
v_a = ?
r_p = 2 $\times$ 10^10m
r_a= 3 $\times$ 10^12m
$\frac{v_{p}}{v_{a}}=\frac{r_{a}}{r_{p}}$
$\frac{2 \times 10^{7}}{v_{a}}=\frac{3 \times 10^{12}}{2 \times 10^{10}}$
v_a = 1.33 $\times$ 10^5m/s

questions.question: A truck is coming towards you from a distance of 1000m on a straight road, at time t=0. The driver stops for a second near a poster. He reaches you in 100 sec. The average speed in m/s is
questions.question_raw: A truck is coming towards you from a distance of 1000m on a straight road, at time t=0. The driver stops for a second near a poster. He reaches you in 100 sec. The average speed in m/s is
questions.explanation:
Total distance covered s = 1000 m
Total time taken t = 100 - 0 = 100 s
Average speed, $v_{avg}={total \ distance \ covered\over total \ time \ taken}$ $= {1000\over 100}= 10 \ m/s$

questions.question: The basic difference between a scalar and vector is one of
questions.question_raw: The basic difference between a scalar and vector is one of
questions.explanation: Scalar quantity gives you an idea about how much of an object there is, but vector quantity gives you an indication of how much of an object there is and that also in which direction. So, the main difference between these two quantities is associated with the direction, i.e. scalars do not have direction but vectors do.

questions.question: A car of mass m starts from rest and acquires a velocity along east $\boldsymbol{v}=v \hat{\mathbf{i}}$(V > 0) in two seconds. Assuming the car moves with uniform acceleration, the force exerted on the car is
questions.question_raw: A car of mass m starts from rest and acquires a velocity along east {tex}\boldsymbol{v}=v \hat{\mathbf{i}}{/tex}(V > 0) in two seconds. Assuming the car moves with uniform acceleration, the force exerted on the car is

options.text: more than $\frac{m v}{2}$ eastward exerted due to the engine and overcomes the friction of the road
$\frac{m v}{2}$ eastward and is exerted by the car engine
$\frac{m v}{2}$ eastward and is due to the friction on the tyres exerted by the road

correct_option_texts: $\frac{m v}{2}$ eastward and is due to the friction on the tyres exerted by the road
questions.explanation:
Here, mass of car $=m$
As it starts from rest, $u=0$ final velocity along east $v=v \hat{i}, t=2 {sec}$
From $v=u+a t$
$ v \hat{i}=0+\vec{a} \times 2, \vec{a}=\frac{v}{2} \hat{i}$
$\vec{F}=m \vec{a}=\frac{m v}{2} \hat{i}$, force of car is $\frac{m v}{2}$ east ward
This is due to friction on the tyres exerted by the road.

questions.question: Postulates are
questions.question_raw: Postulates are

options.text: assumed to be false for the purpose of reasoning.
assumed to be true for the purpose of reasoning.

correct_option_texts: assumed to be true for the purpose of reasoning.
questions.explanation: A postulate is an assumption, that is, a proposition or statement, that is assumed to be true without any proof. Postulates are the fundamental propositions used to prove other statements known as theorems. Once a theorem has been proven it is may be used in the proof of other theorems.

questions.question: The surface tension of a liquid is 70 dyne/cm. In MKS system its value is?
questions.question_raw: The surface tension of a liquid is 70 dyne/cm. In MKS system its value is?
questions.explanation:
$1 dyne = 10^{-5}\;N$
$1 cm =10^{-2}\;m$ hence its value in MKS is given by :-
$70 dyne/cm= 70 \times \large\frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-2}}$ = 7 ✕ 10^-2 N/m

questions.question: A body of mass 5 kg starts from the origin with an initial velocity $\vec{u}=(30 \hat{i}+40 \hat{j})$ m/s. If a constant force $(-6 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}) \mathrm{N}$ acts on the body, the time in which the y-component of the velocity becomes zero is
questions.question_raw: A body of mass 5 kg starts from the origin with an initial velocity {tex}\vec{u}=(30 \hat{i}+40 \hat{j}){/tex} m/s. If a constant force {tex}(-6 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}) \mathrm{N}{/tex} acts on the body, the time in which the y-component of the velocity becomes zero is
questions.explanation:
$\vec{u}=(30 \hat{i}+40 \hat{j}) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\therefore$ u_y = 40 m/s
$\vec{F}=(-6 \hat{i}-5 \hat{j}) \mathrm{N}$
$\therefore$ F_y = -5N
$a_{y}=\frac{F_{y}}{m}=\frac{-5}{5}$ = -1 m/s^2
v_y = 0
But v_y = u_y + a_yt
$\therefore $ 0 = 40 - 1 $\times$ or t = 40 s

questions.question: At what height from the surface of the earth the gravitational potential and the value of g are -5.4 $\times$ 10^7Jkg^-2 and 6.0 ms^-2 respectively? Take the radius of earth as 6400 km.
questions.question_raw: At what height from the surface of the earth the gravitational potential and the value of g are -5.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 107Jkg-2 and 6.0 ms-2 respectively? Take the radius of earth as 6400 km.
questions.explanation:
V = $\frac{G M}{R \ + \ h} \text { and } g_{h}=\frac{G M}{(R \ + \ h)^{2}}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\mid V]}{g_{h}}=R+h$
$\Rightarrow$ R + h = $\frac{5.4 \ \times \ 10^{7}}{6.0}=9 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{~m}$ = 9000 km
$\therefore$ h = (9000 - 6400)km
= 2600 km

questions.question: For earth satellites, the centripetal force is provided by
questions.question_raw: For earth satellites, the centripetal force is provided by

options.text: forces exerted by firing rockets on satellite

questions.explanation:
Gravity supplies the necessary centripetal force to hold a satellite in orbit about the earth. Setting the gravity force from the universal law of gravity equal to the required centripetal force yields the equation of motion of satellites in the circular orbit.
i.e., ${F_{gravity}} = {F_{centripetal}}$
$ \Rightarrow {v_{orbital}} = \sqrt {{{GM} \over r}} $

questions.question: A satellite orbits the earth at a height of 400 km above the surface. How much energy must be expended to rocket the satellite out of the earth’s gravitational influence? Mass of the satellite = 200 kg; mass of the earth = 6.0 $ \times $ 10^24 kg; radius of the earth = 6.4 $ \times $ 10^6 m; G = 6.67 $ \times $ 10^-11 N m^2 kg^-2.
questions.question_raw: A satellite orbits the earth at a height of 400 km above the surface. How much energy must be expended to rocket the satellite out of the earth’s gravitational influence? Mass of the satellite = 200 kg; mass of the earth = 6.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 1024 kg; radius of the earth = 6.4 {tex} \times {/tex} 106 m; G = 6.67 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-11 N m2 kg-2.
questions.explanation:
Mass of the Earth, M = 6.0 $\times$ 10^24kg
Mass of the satellite, m = 200 kg
Radius of the Earth, Re = 6.4 $\times$ 10^6m Universal gravitational constant, G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^-11N m^2 kg^−2
Height of the satellite, h = 400 km = 4 $\times$ 10^5m = 0.4 $\times$ 106m
Total energy of the satellite at height = $1\over2$mv^2 + $\left(\frac{-G M_{e} m}{R_{e}+h}\right)$
Orbital velocity of the satellite, $v=\sqrt{\frac{G M_{e}}{R_{e}+h}}$
Total energy at height, $h=\frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{G M_{e}}{R_{e}+h}\right)-\frac{G M_{e} m}{R_{e}+h}=-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{-G M_{e} m}{R_{e}+h}\right)$
The negative sign indicates that the satellite is bound to the Earth. 
This is called the bound energy of the satellite. The energy required to send the satellite out of its orbit = ... (Bound energy)
$=\frac{1}{2} \frac{G M_{e} m}{\left(R_{e}+h\right)}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 6.0 \times 10^{24} \times 200}{\left(6.4 \times 10^{6}+0.4 \times 10^{6}\right)}$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{6.67 \times 6 \times 2 \times 10^{15}}{6.8 \times 10^{6}}$ = 5.9 $\times$ 10^9J

questions.question: A body of mass m, having momentum p is moving on a rough horizontal surface. If it is stopped in a distance x, the coefficient of friction between the body and the surface is
questions.question_raw: A body of mass m, having momentum p is moving on a rough horizontal surface. If it is stopped in a distance x, the coefficient of friction between the body and the surface is
questions.explanation:
Friction, $f=\mu m g$
Retardation, $a=\frac{f}{m}=\mu g$
v^2 = 2ax = $2\mu g x$
p^2 = m^2v^2 $=2 \mu g m^{2} x$
$\Rightarrow \mu=\frac{p^{2}}{2 g m^{2} x}$

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Vector addition is commutative.

questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): Vector addition is commutative.
Reason (R): {tex}(\vec{A}+\vec{B}) \neq(\vec{B}+\vec{A}){/tex}

options.text: A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

correct_option_texts: A is true but R is false.
questions.explanation:
Since vector addition is commutative, therefore
$\vec{A}+\vec{B}=\vec{B}+\vec{A}$

options.text: A is false but R is true.
A is true but R is false.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): In projectile motion, the angle between the instantaneous velocity and acceleration at maximum height is 180^o.
Reason (R): At the maximum height, velocity of projectile is in horizontal direction only.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): In projectile motion, the angle between the instantaneous velocity and acceleration at maximum height is 180o.
Reason (R): At the maximum height, velocity of projectile is in horizontal direction only.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

correct_option_texts: A is false but R is true.
questions.explanation: At the maximum height, vertical component of instantaneous velocity is zero and there is an only a horizontal component. The acceleration due to gravity is acting downward. So the angle between the acceleration due to gravity and the instantaneous velocity is 90^o. So the assertion is false but the reason is true.

Work done against friction
= Initial K.E. of the cylinder
$f . s=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
or $\mu m g . s=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}$
$\therefore s=\frac{v^{2}}{2 \mu g}=\frac{10 \times 10}{2 \times 0.5 \times 10}$ = 10 m

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:
The kinetic energy of an object is the energy associated with the object which is under motion. It is defined as “the energy required by a body to accelerate from rest to stated velocity.” It is a vector quantity and the momentum of an object is the virtue of its mass. It is defined as the product of mass and velocity. It is a vector quantity. The relation between them is given by E = $P^2\over 2m$. In case of the elastic collision both of these quantities remain constant.
Two masses of 1 gm and 4gm are moving with equal linear momentum. The ratio of their kinetic energy is:

questions.question: The word "Science" originates from the Latin verb Scientia meaning
questions.question_raw: The word "Science" originates from the Latin verb Scientia meaning
questions.explanation: The phrase "scientia potentia est" (or "scientia est potentia" or also "scientia potestas est") is a Latin aphorism meaning "knowledge is power"

questions.question: Approximately how many km ${{\rm{h}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$ is 3 m ${{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$ ?
questions.question_raw: Approximately how many km {tex}{{\rm{h}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}{/tex} is 3 m {tex}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}{/tex} ?
questions.explanation:
1 km h^​​​​​​-2 = ${1000m\over 3600\times 3600 \;s^2} = {1\over 360\times 36}ms^{-2}$
$\Rightarrow$ $1ms^{-2} = 360\times 36 km\ h^{-2}$
$\Rightarrow$ $3ms^{-2} =3\times 360\times 36 km\ h^{-2}$
$\Rightarrow$ $3ms^{-2}= 38880km\ h^{-2} $
$\Rightarrow 3ms^{-2} \approx 3.9\times 10^4 km \ h^{-2}$

questions.question: A car is traveling due west at 20.0 m/s. Find the velocity of the car after 37.00 s if its constant acceleration is 1.0 m/s^2 due east. Assume the acceleration remains constant.
questions.question_raw: A car is traveling due west at 20.0 m/s. Find the velocity of the car after 37.00 s if its constant acceleration is 1.0 m/s2 due east. Assume the acceleration remains constant.
questions.explanation:
Assume west direction as +ve. Initial velocity, u = + 20.0 m/s
Final velocity = ?
Acceleration a = - 1.0 m/s^​​​​​​2
Time taken t = 37.0 s
We know, v = u + at
$v = 20.0 - 37.0\times 1$
v = -17.0
The negative sign shows direction of velocity is opposite. So velocity is 17.0 m/s due east.

questions.question: A man can swim with a speed of 4.0 km/h in still water. How long does he take to cross a river 1.0 km wide if the river flows steadily at 3.0 km/h and he makes his strokes normal to the river current? How far down the river does he go when he reaches the other bank?
questions.question_raw: A man can swim with a speed of 4.0 km/h in still water. How long does he take to cross a river 1.0 km wide if the river flows steadily at 3.0 km/h and he makes his strokes normal to the river current? How far down the river does he go when he reaches the other bank?
questions.explanation:
Speed of the man, v_m = 4 km/h
Width of the river = 1 km
Time taken to cross the river = $Width \ of \ the \ river \over Speed \ of\ the\ man$ $={ 1\over 4} h = {1 × 60 \over 4 }= 15 \min$
Speed of the river, v_r = 3 km/h
Distance covered due to the flow of river = v_r × t
$= {3 × 1 \over 4} = {3 \over 4 }\ km = {3 × 1000 \over4}$ = 750 m

questions.question: An object has a length of 0.42 cm. Which of the following statements is correct?
questions.question_raw: An object has a length of 0.42 cm. Which of the following statements is correct?

options.text: the uncertainty in the length is about 4$\% $
the uncertainty in the length is 0.01
the uncertainty in the length is 0.05
the uncertainty in the length is $ \pm $0.01

correct_option_texts: the uncertainty in the length is 0.01
questions.explanation:
Suppose, you are simply told that an object has a measured length with no indication of its precision as in the question.
In this case, all you have to go on is the number of digits contained in the data. Thus the quantity “0.42 cm” is specified to 0.01.
So the uncertainty in the length is 0.01.

questions.question: A wheel having a moment of inertia 2 kg m^2 about its vertical axis, rotates at the rate of 60 rpm about this axis. The torque which can stop the wheel's rotation in one minute would be
questions.question_raw: A wheel having a moment of inertia 2 kg m2 about its vertical axis, rotates at the rate of 60 rpm about this axis. The torque which can stop the wheel's rotation in one minute would be
questions.explanation:
I = 2 kg m^2, $\nu_0$= 60 rpm =1 rps
$\omega_{0}=2 \pi \nu_{0}=2 \pi \mathrm{rad} \mathrm{s}^{-1}, \omega=0$
t = 1 min = 60 s
$\alpha=\frac{\omega-\omega_{0}}{t}=\frac{0-2 \pi}{60}=-\frac{\pi}{30} \text { rad } s^{-2}$
$\tau=I \alpha=2 \times \frac{\pi}{30}$
$=\frac{\pi}{15} \mathrm{Nm}$

questions.question: Circular disc of mass m and radius r is rolling forward on the horizontal table with a velocity v. Its total kinetic energy is
questions.question_raw: Circular disc of mass m and radius r is rolling forward on the horizontal table with a velocity v. Its total kinetic energy is
questions.explanation:
Total K.E. of rolling circular disc
$=E_{\text {tran }}+E_{\text {rot }}=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} m r^{2} \times \frac{v^{2}}{r^{2}}=\frac{3}{4} m v^{2}$

questions.question: A body weighed 250 N on the surface. Assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform mass density, how much would it weigh halfway down to the centre of earth?
questions.question_raw: A body weighed 250 N on the surface. Assuming the earth to be a sphere of uniform mass density, how much would it weigh halfway down to the centre of earth?
questions.explanation:
Weight of a body of mass m at the surface of earth's surface, W = mg = 250 N.
Body of mass m is located at depth,
d = $\frac{1}{2}$Re
where Re = Radius of earth
Acceleration due to gravity at depth g(d)is given by the relation:
g(d) = $\left(1-\frac{d}{R_{e}}\right) g$
$=\left(1-\frac{R_{e}}{2 \times R_{e}}\right) g=\frac{1}{2} g$
Weight of the body at depth d,
W' = mg(d)
$=\mathrm{m} \times \frac{1}{2} g=\frac{1}{2} m g=\frac{1}{2} W$
$=\frac{1}{2} \times 250$ = 125N

questions.question: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of a monkey running on the roof of the train A against its motion (with a velocity of 18 km/hr with respect to the train A) as observed by a man standing on the ground in m/s ? Choose the positive direction of x-axis to be from south to north
questions.question_raw: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of a monkey running on the roof of the train A against its motion (with a velocity of 18 km/hr with respect to the train A) as observed by a man standing on the ground in m/s ? Choose the positive direction of x-axis to be from south to north
questions.explanation:
Choose the positive direction of the x-axis to be from the south to the north. 
Then, v_A = +54 km h^-1 = +15 ms^-1
v_B = -90 km h^-1 = - 25 ms^-1
The relative velocity of B with respect to A
= v_B - v_A = (-25) -15= -40 ms^-1, i.e. the train B appears to A to move with a speed of 40 ms^-1 from north to south.
The relative velocity of ground with respect to B = 0 - v_B = 0 - (-25) = 25ms^-1
Now, let the velocity of the monkey with respect to the ground be v_M. 
The relative velocity of the monkey with respect to A,
v_MA = v_M - v_A = 18 -54 kmh^-1 = - 10ms^-1.

questions.question: Which is a constant for a freely falling object?
questions.question_raw: Which is a constant for a freely falling object?
questions.explanation: An object that falls through a vacuum is subjected to only one external force, the gravitational force, expressed as the weight of the object. An object that is moving only because of the action of gravity is said to be free falling and its motion is described by Newton's second law of motion. With algebra we can solve for the acceleration of a free falling object. The acceleration is constant and equal to the gravitational acceleration g which is 9.8 meters per square second at sea level on the Earth. The weight, size, and shape of the object are not a factor in describing a free fall.

questions.question: A force vector applied on a mass is represented as $\vec{F}=6 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}$ and accelerates with 1 m/s^2. What will be the mass of the body?
questions.question_raw: A force vector applied on a mass is represented as {tex}\vec{F}=6 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}{/tex} and accelerates with 1 m/s2. What will be the mass of the body?
questions.explanation:
$\vec{F}=6 \hat{i}-8 \hat{j}+10 \hat{k}$
$F=|\vec{F}|=\sqrt{36+64+100}=10 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~N}$
$m=\frac{F}{a}=\frac{10 \sqrt{2}}{1 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}}=10 \sqrt{2} \mathrm{~kg}$

questions.question_raw: The scalar product of two vectors A and B is
questions.explanation: Scalar product means dot product and the dot product of 2 vectors gives a scalar. For example dot product of force and displacement gives work which is scalar.

questions.question: The moment of inertia of a circular loop of radius R, at a distance of $\frac{R}{2}$ around a rotating axis parallel to horizontal diameter of loop is
questions.question_raw: The moment of inertia of a circular loop of radius R, at a distance of {tex}\frac{R}{2}{/tex} around a rotating axis parallel to horizontal diameter of loop is
questions.explanation:
By parallel axes theorem,
$I=I_{\text {diameter }}+M\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{2} M R^{2}+\frac{M R^{2}}{4}=\frac{3}{4} M R^{2}$

questions.question: The gravitational force on point mass m_1 due to point mass m_2, m_3 & m_4 is the____________________of gravitational forces exerted by m2m2, m3m3 and m4.
questions.question_raw: The gravitational force on point mass m1 due to point mass m2, m3 & m4 is the____________________of gravitational forces exerted by m2m2, m3m3 and m4.
questions.explanation: According to properties of gravitational force, the force between the particles is independent of the presence or absence of other particles; so the principle of superposition is valid i.e. force on a particle due to a number of particles is the resultant of forces due to individual particles i.e., $\overrightarrow {{F_1}} + \overrightarrow {{F_2}} + \overrightarrow {{F_3}} + .........$

questions.question: What is the correct sequence of events technology gives rise to new science or new science gives rise to technology?
questions.question_raw: What is the correct sequence of events technology gives rise to new science or new science gives rise to technology?

options.text: Science can give rise to technology only
Technology can give rise to new science only
Science can give rise to technology and technology can give rise to new science

correct_option_texts: Science can give rise to technology and technology can give rise to new science
questions.explanation: Technology is often a consequence of science and engineering, although technology as a human activity precedes the two fields. For example, science might study the flow of electrons in electrical conductors by using already-existing tools and knowledge. This new-found knowledge may then be used by engineers to create new tools and machines such as semiconductors, computers, and other forms of advanced technology.

According to Hubble's lav/, speed of recession of a galaxy,
v = Hr
$\therefore$ [H] = $\frac{[v]}{[r]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{LT}^{-1}\right]}{[\mathrm{L}]}$ = [T^-1]

questions.question: A lift is coming from 8^th floor and is just about to reach 4^th floor. Taking ground floor as origin and positive direction upwards for all quantities, which one of the following is correct?

questions.explanation:
As the lift is coming from 8th to 4th then, the value of x becomes less (negative)

From 8^th floor to 4^th floor

questions.question: 300 J of work is done in sliding a 2 kg block up an inclined plane of height 10 m. Taking g = 10 $\frac{m}{s^2}$, the work done against friction is

questions.explanation:
Total work done = Work done against friction + Increase in P.E.
300 J = W + 2 $\times$ 10 $\times$ 10
W = 300 - 200 = 100 J

questions.explanation: When one body exerts a force on a second body, the second body simultaneously exerts a force equal in magnitude and opposite in direction on the first body. equal to the average of the moments of inertia of the body about a parallel axis passing through its centre of mass and the product of its mass and the square of the distance between the two parallel axes.

questions.question: Mass of moon is $\frac 1{81}$ time that of earth and its radius is $\frac 14$ the earth's radius. If escape velocity at surface of earth is 11.2 km/s, then its value at surface of moon is:
questions.question_raw: Mass of moon is {tex}\frac 1{81}{/tex} time that of earth and its radius is {tex}\frac 14{/tex} the earth's radius. If escape velocity at surface of earth is 11.2 km/s, then its value at surface of moon is:
questions.explanation:
v_e(earth) = $\sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}}$ = 11.2 km s^-1
v_e(moon) = $\sqrt{\frac{2 G(\frac M{81})}{\frac R 4}}$
= $\frac{2}{9} \sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}}=\frac{2}{9} \times 11.2 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
= 2.45 km/s

questions.question: A ball starts from rest and accelerates at 0.500 m/s^2 while moving down an inclined plane 9.00 m long. When it reaches the bottom, the ball rolls up another plane, where, after moving 15.0 m, it comes to rest. What is the speed of the ball in m/s at the bottom of the first plane?
questions.question_raw: A ball starts from rest and accelerates at 0.500 m/s2 while moving down an inclined plane 9.00 m long. When it reaches the bottom, the ball rolls up another plane, where, after moving 15.0 m, it comes to rest. What is the speed of the ball in m/s at the bottom of the first plane?
questions.explanation:
For First Plane
Initial velocity u = 0
Acceleration a = 0.500 m/s^​​​​​​2
Distance covered s = 9.0 m
Final velocity v =?
We know
v^2 − u^2 = 2as
$\Rightarrow $ v^2 − 0 = 2 $\times$ 0.500 $\times$ 9.0
v^2 = 9
$\Rightarrow$ v = 3 m/s
This is the initial velocity for the second plane

the product of component of the force in the direction of the displacement and the magnitude of the displacement
the negative product of component of the force in the direction of the displacement and the magnitude of this displacement

questions.question: A man of mass 70 kg stands on a weighing scale in a lift which is moving upwards with a uniform speed of 10 ms^-1. What would be the reading if the lift mechanism failed and it hurtled down freely under gravity?
questions.question_raw: A man of mass 70 kg stands on a weighing scale in a lift which is moving upwards with a uniform speed of 10 ms-1. What would be the reading if the lift mechanism failed and it hurtled down freely under gravity?
questions.explanation:
When the lift falls freely under gravity, a = g
Therefore Apparent weight, 
R = m(g − a) = m(g − g) = 0
This is the condition of weightlessness.

questions.question: The radius of gyration of a body about an axis at a distance of 6 cm from its centre of mass is 10 cm. Then, its radius of gyration about a parallel axis through its centre of mass will be
questions.question_raw: The radius of gyration of a body about an axis at a distance of 6 cm from its centre of mass is 10 cm. Then, its radius of gyration about a parallel axis through its centre of mass will be
questions.explanation:
I_CD = I_AB + M^2
$M k_{\mathrm{CD}}^{2}=M k_{\mathrm{CM}}^{2}+M d^{2}$
$k_{\mathrm{CM}}^{2}=k_{\mathrm{CD}}^{2}-d^{2}=(10)^{2}-6^{2}=64$
$\therefore$ k_CM = 8 Cm

questions.question: The moment of inertia of a thin uniform ring of mass 1 Kg about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the ring is 0.25 Kg m^2. Then the diameter of the ring is:
questions.question_raw: The moment of inertia of a thin uniform ring of mass 1 Kg about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to the plane of the ring is 0.25 Kg m2. Then the diameter of the ring is:
questions.explanation:
I = MR^2
I = 0.25Kgm^2
M = 1Kg
$R=\sqrt{\frac{I}{M}}=\sqrt{\frac{0.25}{1}}$ = 0.5 m
Diameter d = 2R
d = 2 $\times$ 0.5 = 1 m

questions.question: Which of the graph correctly shows the change in kinetic energy of an iron sphere falling freely in a lake having sufficient depth to impart it a terminal velocity?
questions.question_raw: Which of the graph correctly shows the change in kinetic energy of an iron sphere falling freely in a lake having sufficient depth to impart it a terminal velocity?
questions.explanation: When an iron sphere falls freely in a lake, its motion is accelerated due to gravity and retarded due to viscous force. The overall effect is an increase in velocity and hence the increase in kinetic energy till the sphere acquires terminal velocity which is constant. Hence, the kinetic energy of the sphere beyond this depth of lake becomes constant.

According to Newton's second law of motion, the acceleration of a point mass as produced by a net force is directly proportional to the magnitude of the net force at that point in the same direction as the net force at that instant

questions.question: A mass is revolving in a circle, which is in the plane of the paper. The direction of angular acceleration if any, is:
questions.question_raw: A mass is revolving in a circle, which is in the plane of the paper. The direction of angular acceleration if any, is:

options.text: At right angles to the plane of the paper
Upward from the plane of the paper

correct_option_texts: At right angles to the plane of the paper
questions.explanation: Angular acceleration is an axial vector. It is always directed along the axis of rotation according to the right-hand screw rule. Hence the direction of the angular acceleration vector is perpendicular to the plane in which the rotation takes place.

questions.question: A Merry-go-round, made of a ring-like platform of radius R and mass M, is revolving with angular speed $\omega$. A person of mass M is standing on it. At one instant, the person jumps off the round, radially away from the centre of the round (as seen from the round). The speed of the round afterwards is
questions.question_raw: A Merry-go-round, made of a ring-like platform of radius R and mass M, is revolving with angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex}. A person of mass M is standing on it. At one instant, the person jumps off the round, radially away from the centre of the round (as seen from the round). The speed of the round afterwards is

As no torque is exerted by the person jumping, radially away from the centre of the round (as seen from the round), let the total moment of inertia of the system is 21 (round + Person (because the total mass
is 2 M) and the round is revolving with angular speed wsince the angular momentum of the person when it jumps off the round is 1 $\omega$ the actual momentum of round seen from ground is 2|$\omega$-| $\omega$ = |$\omega$
So we conclude that the angular speed remains same, i.e $\omega$

questions.question: An object is said to be in uniform motion in a straight line if its displacement
questions.question_raw: An object is said to be in uniform motion in a straight line if its displacement

options.text: is equal in equal intervals of time.
is equal in not equal intervals of time.
is increasing in equal intervals of time.
is decreasing in equal intervals of time.

correct_option_texts: is equal in equal intervals of time.
questions.explanation:
Uniform motion is the kind of motion in which a body covers equal displacement in equal intervals of time. It does
not matter how small the time intervals are, as long as the displacements covered are equal. If a body is involved in rectilinear motion and the motion is uniform, then the acceleration of the body must be zero.

questions.question: Wave picture of light failed to explain_________________.
questions.question_raw: Wave picture of light failed to explain_________________.
questions.explanation:
The photoelectric effect cannot be explained according to wave theory because of the following reasons:
1. According to wave theory, after the light falls on a substance electrons are emitted after a small instant of time. However, in the photoelectric effect, the electron emissions are immediate without a time delay.
2. The energy of a wave increases with an increase in intensity. However, in the photoelectric effect, an increase in intensity has no effect on the energy of electrons emitted. All that happens is the number of electrons emitted, increases.

questions.question: Find the projection of F on d, if force ${\bf{F}} = 3{\bf{\hat i}} + 4{\bf{\hat j}} - 5{\bf{\hat k}}$ and displacement ${\bf{d}} = 5{\bf{\hat i}} + 4{\bf{\hat j}} + 3{\bf{\hat k}}$
questions.question_raw: Find the projection of F on d, if force {tex}{\bf{F}} = 3{\bf{\hat i}} + 4{\bf{\hat j}} - 5{\bf{\hat k}}{/tex} and displacement {tex}{\bf{d}} = 5{\bf{\hat i}} + 4{\bf{\hat j}} + 3{\bf{\hat k}}{/tex}
questions.explanation:
The vector projection of a vector $\vec A$ on a nonzero vector $\vec B$ is also known as the vector component $\vec A$ in the direction of $\vec B$ and is equal to $\left( {\vec A.\hat B} \right).\hat B$ 
Here projection of $\vec F$ on vector $\vec d$ = $\left( {\vec F.\hat d} \right).\hat d $ = $(\vec{F} \cdot \hat{d}) \cdot \hat{d}=\left[(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-5 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot \frac{(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{5^2+4^2+3^2}}\right] \cdot\left[\frac{(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{5^2+4^2+3^2}}\right]$
$=\left[(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}-5 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot \frac{(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{50}}\right] \cdot\left[\frac{(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{50}}\right]$
$=\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{50}}(15+16-15)\right] \cdot\left[\frac{(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{50}}\right]$
$=\frac{16}{\sqrt{50}} \cdot\left[\frac{(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})}{\sqrt{50}}\right]=\frac{8}{25}(5 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}})$

questions.question: The escape velocity from earth is 11.2 km/s. If a body is to be projected in a direction making an angle 45° to the vertical, then the escape velocity is:
questions.question_raw: The escape velocity from earth is 11.2 km/s. If a body is to be projected in a direction making an angle 45° to the vertical, then the escape velocity is:
questions.explanation: Escape velocity does not depend on the angle of projection. Escape velocity will remain the same. Hence, escape velocity is 11.2 km/s.

questions.question: If g be the acceleration due to gravity at the earth surface, then what will be the increase in potential energy if object of mass m is raised by its radius R?
questions.question_raw: If g be the acceleration due to gravity at the earth surface, then what will be the increase in potential energy if object of mass m is raised by its radius R?
questions.explanation:
At the surface of the earth,
$U_{i}=-\frac{G M m}{R}$
At a height equal to the radius of the earth,
$U_{f}=-\frac{G M m}{R \ + \ R}=-\frac{G M m}{2 R}$
$\Delta U=U_{f}-U_{i}=\frac{G M m}{R}\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)$
= $\frac{g R^{2} M}{R} \times \frac{1}{2}$ [GM = gR^2]
= $\frac{1}{2} m g R$

questions.question: A body is allowed to slide down a frictionless track freely under gravity. The track ends in a semicircular-shaped part of diameter D. What should be the height (minimum) from which the body must fall, so that it completes the circle?
questions.question_raw: A body is allowed to slide down a frictionless track freely under gravity. The track ends in a semicircular-shaped part of diameter D. What should be the height (minimum) from which the body must fall, so that it completes the circle?
questions.explanation:
The body will complete the vertical circle if its minimum speed at the bottom is $\sqrt{5 g R}$, where R is the radius of the circular track. If the body falls through height h, then
$\sqrt{2 g h}=\sqrt{5 g \frac{D}{2}}$ or $h=\frac{5 D}{4}$

correct_option_texts: A is true but R is false.

So, the vehicles cross each other at that point and they cannot have same velocity at that instant. So, the assertion is true and the reason is false.

In uniform circular motion, the magnitude of velocity and acceleration remains same, but due to change in direction of motion, the direction of velocity and acceleration changes. Also the centripetal acceleration is given by $a=\omega^{2} r$.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Fault lines in the earth's crust are like compressed springs.
Reason (R): Compressed springs possess a large amount of kinetic energy.

Reason (R): Compressed springs possess a large amount of kinetic energy.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.

correct_option_texts: A is true but R is false.

The earth's crust has many discontinuities and dislocations those are called fault lines. These fault lines in the earth's crust are like compressed springs. So, the assertion is true.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Moment of inertia plays the same role in rotational motion, as mass plays in linear motion.
Reason (R): Moment of inertia depends only on the mass of the body.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): Moment of inertia plays the same role in rotational motion, as mass plays in linear motion.
Reason (R): Moment of inertia depends only on the mass of the body.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

correct_option_texts: A is true but R is false.
questions.explanation: The mass or inertia is the property of bodies by virtue of which the bodies have a tendency to remain at rest or in a state of uniform linear motion unless an external force is exerted on them. Greater is the mass of the body, greater is the external force required to brings it in the position of rest or uniform motion. Similarly, in order to rotate a body about an axis, a torque has to be applied to the body. This is described by saying that the body has a moment of inertia about the axis of rotation. The greater is the moment of inertia of a body about an axis, the greater is the torque required to rotate or to stop the body about that axis. Thus, the moment of inertia plays the same role in the rotational motion as mass or inertia plays in translational motion.

S = 10t + 4t2. What is the initial velocity of the body?
questions.explanation:
Velocity is given by:
v = $ds\over dt$= $d(10t+4t^2)\over dt$= 10 + 8t
Velocity at t = 0, v_0 = 10 + 8 $\times$ 0 = 10 m/s

questions.question: You may have seen in a circus a motorcyclist driving in vertical loops inside a death well’ (a hollow spherical chamber with holes, so the spectators can watch from outside). What is the minimum speed required at the uppermost position to perform a vertical loop if the radius of the chamber is 25 m?
questions.question_raw: You may have seen in a circus a motorcyclist driving in vertical loops inside a death well’ (a hollow spherical chamber with holes, so the spectators can watch from outside). What is the minimum speed required at the uppermost position to perform a vertical loop if the radius of the chamber is 25 m?
questions.explanation:
As the centripetal force is equal to weight (mg) and normal reaction (R), we have
$\frac{mv^2}{r}$ = R + mg
At a minimum speed, R= 0, Therefore, we have
$\frac{mv^2}{r}$ = mg
v = $\sqrt{rg}=\sqrt{25\times 9.8}$ = 15.65ms^−1

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:
While treating the topic of projectile motion, it is assumed that the air resistance has no effect on the motion of the projectile.
Friction, force due to viscosity, air resistance are all dissipative forces. In the presence of any of such force opposing motion, any object will lose some part of its initial energy and consequently, momentum too. Thus, a projectile that traverses a parabolic path will certainly show deviation from its idealised trajectory in the presence of air resistance. It will not hit the ground with the same speed with which was projected from it.
In the absence of air resistance, the x-component of the velocity remains constant and it is only the y-component that undergoes a continuous change. However, in the presence of air resistance, both of these will get affected. That means that the range will be less than the value calculated from equation
R = $\frac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta_{0}}{g}$
Maximum height attained will also be less than the value predicted by the equation
H_max = $\frac{\left(u \sin \theta_{0}\right)^{2}}{2 g}$
In order to avoid air resistance, we will have to perform the experiment in vacuum or under low pressure.
In presence of air resistance, a projectile

In the presence of any of such force opposing motion, any object will lose some part of its initial energy and consequently, momentum too. Thus, a projectile that traverses a parabolic path will certainly show deviation from its idealised trajectory in the presence of air resistance. It will not hit the ground with the same speed with which was projected from it.
In the absence of air resistance, the x-component of the velocity remains constant and it is only the y-component that undergoes a continuous change. However, in the presence of air resistance, both of these will get affected. That means that the range will be less than the value calculated from equation
R = {tex}\frac{u^{2} \sin 2 \theta_{0}}{g}{/tex}
Maximum height attained will also be less than the value predicted by the equation
Hmax = {tex}\frac{\left(u \sin \theta_{0}\right)^{2}}{2 g}{/tex}
In order to avoid air resistance, we will have to perform the experiment in vacuum or under low pressure.


In the absence of air resistance,

  1. the x-component of the velocity remains constant and it is only the y-component that undergoes a continuous change
  2. the y-component of the velocity remains constant and it is only the x-component that undergoes a continuous change
  3. the x-component and y-component of the velocity both remain constant
  4. the x-component and y-component of the velocity both vary continuously
questions.explanation: the x-component of the velocity remains constant and it is only the y-component that undergoes a continuous change

questions.question: The microscopic domain of physics deals with
questions.question_raw: The microscopic domain of physics deals with

options.text: The constitution and structure of matter at the scales of atoms and nuclei and their interaction with different elementary particles
the constitution and structure of matter at the scales of stars and planets and their interaction with different elementary particles
the constitution and structure of matter at the scales of atoms and nuclei and their interaction with different bodies on the earth
the constitution and structure of matter at the plains of stars and planets and their contraction with different elementary particles

correct_option_texts: The constitution and structure of matter at the scales of atoms and nuclei and their interaction with different elementary particles
questions.explanation: Microscopic domain includes atomic,molecular and nuclear phenomena. It can be said as the study of matter.It includes the study of structure and configuration of atom,nucleus and the constitute particles of atom-electron,proton,neutron and interaction with elementary particles like electron,photon etc.

questions.question: How many g ${\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$ does 1 kg ${{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$ equal?
questions.question_raw: How many g {tex}{\rm{c}}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}{/tex} does 1 kg {tex}{{\rm{m}}^{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}{/tex} equal?
questions.explanation:
We know 1 kg = 10^3 g
1 m^​​​​​​2 = 10^4 cm^2
1 s^​​​​​​-2 = 1s^-2
Multiply all these quantities we get
$\Rightarrow$ 1 kg m^​​​​​​2 s^​​​​​​-2 = $10^3\times 10^4 gcm^2s^{-2}$
$\Rightarrow 1kgm^2s^{-2}= 10^7 gcm^2s^{-2}$

questions.question: SI unit of acceleration is
questions.question_raw: SI unit of acceleration is
questions.explanation:
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity of an object with respect to time.
a = $change \ in \ velocity \over time \ taken $
SI unit of velocity = m/s
SI unit of time = s
So SI unit of acceleration = ${m\over s\times s}= m/s^2$

questions.question: A particle starts from the origin at t = 0 s with a velocity of 10.0 $\hat j$ m/s and moves in the x-y plane with a constant acceleration of (8.0$\hat i$ + 2.0$\hat j$) m ${{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$. At what time is the x-coordinate of the particle 16 m? What is the y-coordinate of the particle at that time?
questions.question_raw: A particle starts from the origin at t = 0 s with a velocity of 10.0 {tex}\hat j{/tex} m/s and moves in the x-y plane with a constant acceleration of (8.0{tex}\hat i{/tex} + 2.0{tex}\hat j{/tex}) m {tex}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}{/tex}. At what time is the x-coordinate of the particle 16 m? What is the y-coordinate of the particle at that time?
questions.explanation:
u = u_x$\hat i$ + u_y$\hat j$ = 10$\hat j$, u_x = 0 and u_y = 10 m/s
a = a_x$\hat i$ + a_y$\hat j$ = 8i + 2j,
a_x = 8 and a_y = 2 m/s^2
x = u_xt +$1\over 2$a_xt^2 
$\Rightarrow$ 16 = $8 t^2\over 2$
$\Rightarrow$ t = 2 sec 
y = u_yt + $a_yt^2\over 2$ = 10 $\times$ 2 + $2\times 2^2\over 2$ = 24 m

questions.question: The number of significant digits in 6.032 N ${{\rm{m}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$ is
questions.question_raw: The number of significant digits in 6.032 N {tex}{{\rm{m}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}{/tex} is
questions.explanation:
There are three rules on determining how many significant figures are in a number: 
 Non-zero digits are always significant.
 Any zeros between two significant digits are significant.
 A final zero or trailing zeros in the decimal portion ONLY are significant.
So keeping these rules in mind, there are 4 significant digits.

questions.question: Four particles of mass 2 kg, 3 kg, 4 kg and 8 kg are situated at the corners of a square of side length 2m. The centre of mass may be given as
questions.question_raw: Four particles of mass 2 kg, 3 kg, 4 kg and 8 kg are situated at the corners of a square of side length 2m. The centre of mass may be given as
questions.explanation:
$x_{C M}=\frac{2 \times 0+3 \times 2+4 \times 2+8 \times 0}{2+3+4+8}=\frac{14}{17}$
$y_{C M}=\frac{2 \times 0+3 \times 0+4 \times 2+8 \times 2}{2+3+4+8}=\frac{24}{17}$

questions.question: A disc is rotating with angular speed $\omega$. If a child sits on it, what is conserved
questions.question_raw: A disc is rotating with angular speed {tex}\omega{/tex}. If a child sits on it, what is conserved
questions.explanation: When the child sits on the rotating disc, his weight acts downwards and does not produce any torque. Hence the angular momentum of the system is conserved.

questions.question: A body is projected from earth's surface to become its satellite. Its time period of revolution will not depend upon:
questions.question_raw: A body is projected from earth's surface to become its satellite. Its time period of revolution will not depend upon:
questions.explanation: T = $2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{(R \ + \ h)^{3}}{G M}}$, Clearly, T does not depend on the mass m of the satellite.

questions.question: Jules Verne in 1865 proposed sending people to the Moon by firing a space capsule from 220-m-long cannon with a final velocity of 10.97 km/s. What would have been the unrealistically large acceleration experienced by the space travelers during launch?
questions.question_raw: Jules Verne in 1865 proposed sending people to the Moon by firing a space capsule from 220-m-long cannon with a final velocity of 10.97 km/s. What would have been the unrealistically large acceleration experienced by the space travelers during launch?
questions.explanation:
We know the equation of motion
v^2 − u^2 = 2as 
Given: v = 10.97 km/s = 10.97 $\times$ 10^3 m/s
u = 0
S = 220 m
Acceleration $a = {v^2 - u^2 \over 2s}$ =$ (10.97 \times 10^3 )^2- (0)^2\over 2 \times 220 $ = $ (10.97 \times 10^3 )^2\over 440 $ 
= 2.74 $\times$ 10^5 m/s^2

questions.question: The magnitude of displacement of a particle is
questions.question_raw: The magnitude of displacement of a particle is

options.text: is equal to the path length of the particle between two points.
is more than the path length of the particle between two points.
is less than the path length of the particle between two points.
is either less or equal to the path length of the particle between two points.

correct_option_texts: is either less or equal to the path length of the particle between two points.
questions.explanation: The maximum possible value for displacement is the distance travelled, so it cannot be of a greater value than distance (path length). The magnitude of the displacement is always less than or equal to the distance traveled. If two displacements in the same direction are added, then the magnitude of their sum will be equal to the distance traveled.

questions.explanation:
v^2 - 0^2 = 2gh
$v=\sqrt{2 g h}$
This velocity does not depend on mass.

questions.question: A cricket ball of mass 150 g moving with a speed of 126 km/h hits at the middle of the bat, held firmly at its position by the batsman. The ball moves straight back to the bowler after hitting the bat. Assuming that collision between ball and bat is completely elastic and the two remain in contact for 0.001s, the force that the batsman had to apply to hold the bat firmly at its place would be
questions.question_raw: A cricket ball of mass 150 g moving with a speed of 126 km/h hits at the middle of the bat, held firmly at its position by the batsman. The ball moves straight back to the bowler after hitting the bat. Assuming that collision between ball and bat is completely elastic and the two remain in contact for 0.001s, the force that the batsman had to apply to hold the bat firmly at its place would be
questions.explanation:
Here, We havw given that
mass = 150 g = 0.15 kg
time = 0.001 s
speed (v) = 126 km /h = 35 m /s
Force = $\frac{\text { change in momentum }}{\text { time }}$
= $\frac{m v-(\mathrm{mv})}{t}=\frac{2 \mathrm{mv}}{t}$
= $\frac{2(0.15 \mathrm{kg})(35 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s})}{0.001 \mathrm{s}} $
=10500 N = 1.05 $\times$ 10^4 N

questions.question: the centre of mass of a system of particles moves
questions.question_raw: the centre of mass of a system of particles moves

options.text: as if all the mass of the system was concentrated at the axis of rotation and all the external forces were absent
as if all the mass of the system was concentrated at the centre of mass and all the external forces were zero
as if all the mass of the system was concentrated at the centre of mass and all the external forces were applied at that point
as if all the mass of the system was concentrated in a sphere around the centre of mass and all the external forces were zero

correct_option_texts: as if all the mass of the system was concentrated at the centre of mass and all the external forces were applied at that point
questions.explanation: The centre of mass of a system of particles moves as if all the mass of the system is concentrated at the centre and all the external forces were applied at that point. In order to ease the calculations involved in the dynamics of an extended body, we treat any extended body as the system of particles, and the motion of centre of mass of the system is calculated by taking the mass of the whole system to be concentrated at the centre of mass and the external forces acting on system are said to be acting on the centre of mass.

questions.question: A rocket is fired ‘vertically’ from the surface of mars with a speed of 2 km s^−1. If 20 percent of its initial energy is lost due to Martian atmospheric resistance, how far will the rocket go from the surface of Mars before returning to it? Mass of mars = 6.4 $ \times $ 10^23 kg; radius of mars = 3395 km; G = 6.67 $ \times $ 10^−11 N m^2 kg^-2.
questions.question_raw: A rocket is fired ‘vertically’ from the surface of mars with a speed of 2 km s−1. If 20 percent of its initial energy is lost due to Martian atmospheric resistance, how far will the rocket go from the surface of Mars before returning to it? Mass of mars = 6.4 {tex} \times {/tex} 1023 kg; radius of mars = 3395 km; G = 6.67 {tex} \times {/tex} 10−11 N m2 kg-2.
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity of the rocket, v = 2 km/s = 2 $\times$ 10^3 m/s
Mass of Mars, M = 6.4 $\times$ 10^23 kg
Radius of Mars, R = 3395 km = 3.395 $\times$ 10^6 m
Universal gravitational constant, G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^−11 N m^2 kg^−2
Mass of the rocket = m , Initial kinetic energy of the rocket = $1\over2$mv^2
Initial potential energy of the rocket = $-GMm\over r$
Total initial energy = $1\over 2$mv^2 - $GMm\over r$
If 20% of initial kinetic energy is lost due to Martian atmospheric resistance, then only 80 % of its kinetic energy helps in reaching a height.
Total initial energy available = $\frac{80}{100} \times \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}-\frac{G M m}{R}=0.4 m v^{2}-\frac{G M m}{R}$
The maximum height reached by rocket = h
At this height, the velocity and hence, the kinetic energy of the rocket will become zero. The total energy of the rocket at height h = $-G M m \over {R + h }$
Applying the law of conservation of energy for the rocket, we can write: 
$0.4 m v^{2}-\frac{G M m}{R}=\frac{-G M m}{(R+h)}$
$0.4 m v^{2}=\frac{G M m}{R}-\frac{G M m}{(R+h)}$
$0.4 v^2=\operatorname{GM}\left(\frac{1}{R}-\frac{1}{(R+h)}\right)$
$\Rightarrow \frac{R+h}{h}=\frac{G M}{0.4 v^{2} R}$
$\frac{R}{h}+1=\frac{G M}{0.4 v^{2} R}$
$\frac{R}{h}=\frac{G M}{0.4 v^{2} R}-1$
$h=\frac{R}{\frac{G M}{0.4 v^{2} R}-1}$
$h=\frac{0.4 v^{2} R^{2}}{G M-0.4 v^{2} R}$
$=\frac{0.4 \times (2 \times 10^3)^2\times \left(3.395 \times 10^{6}\right)^{2} }{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 6.4 \times 10^{23}-0.4 \times (2\times 10^3)^2 \times \left(3.395 \times 10^{6}\right) }$
$=\frac{18.442 \times 10^{18}}{42.688 \times 10^{12}-5.432 \times 10^{12}}=\frac{18.442}{37.256} \times 10^{6}$
= 495 $\times$ 10^3m = 495 km

questions.question: What is the relation between morality and science?
questions.question_raw: What is the relation between morality and science?

options.text: Science has proved that morality is impractical
Morality can be derived from scientific principles
Morality is not the subject matter of science

correct_option_texts: Morality is not the subject matter of science
questions.explanation: Science can help us learn about terminal illnesses and the history of human and animal rights - and that knowledge can inform our opinions and decisions. But ultimately, individual people must make moral judgments. Science helps us describe how the world is, but it cannot make any judgments about whether that state of affairs is right, wrong, good, or bad.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most precise device for measuring length?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following is the most precise device for measuring length?

options.text: An optical instrument that can measure length to within a wavelength of light.
A vernier callipers with 20 divisions on the sliding scale.
A screw gauge of pitch 1 mm and 100 divisions on the circular scale.

correct_option_texts: An optical instrument that can measure length to within a wavelength of light.
questions.explanation: An optical instrument that can measure length to within a wavelength of light.

questions.question: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 30 m/s. It stops in 2.0 s. What is the displacement of the plane when it stops?
questions.question_raw: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 30 m/s. It stops in 2.0 s. What is the displacement of the plane when it stops?
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity u, = 30 m/s
As it stop so final velocity, v = 0 m/s
Time t = 2 s
Distance covered = s
we know that ,v= u +at
0= 30 + a(2)
a= - 15m/s^2
We know, s = $ut+{1\over 2}at^2$
$\Rightarrow s = 30\times 2+{1\over 2}(-15)\times2^2$
$\Rightarrow$ s = 30 m
