The instantaneous power applied to the particle is

questions.question: A stone of mass 0.25 kg tied to the end of a string is whirled round in a circle of radius 1.5 m with a speed of 40 rev./min in a horizontal plane. What is the maximum speed with which the stone can be whirled around if the string can withstand a maximum tension of 200 N?
questions.question_raw: A stone of mass 0.25 kg tied to the end of a string is whirled round in a circle of radius 1.5 m with a speed of 40 rev./min in a horizontal plane. What is the maximum speed with which the stone can be whirled around if the string can withstand a maximum tension of 200 N?
questions.explanation:
Given that T_max = 200 N
$\frac{m v^{2}}{r}=T_{\max }$
$v_{\max }=\sqrt{\frac{T_{\max } \times r}{m}}$
$=\sqrt{\frac{200 \times 1.5}{0.25}}$
= 34.6 m s^-1
= 35 m s^-1

questions.question: A person trying to lose weight (dieter) lifts a 10 kg mass, one thousand times, to a height of 0.5 m each time. Assume that the potential energy lost each time she lowers the mass is dissipated. Fat supplies 3.8 ${\times }$ 10^7 J of energy per kilogram which is converted to mechanical energy with a 20% efficiency rate. How much fat will the dieter use up?
questions.question_raw: A person trying to lose weight (dieter) lifts a 10 kg mass, one thousand times, to a height of 0.5 m each time. Assume that the potential energy lost each time she lowers the mass is dissipated. Fat supplies 3.8 {tex}{\times }{/tex} 107 J of energy per kilogram which is converted to mechanical energy with a 20% efficiency rate. How much fat will the dieter use up?
questions.explanation:
Work done by force applied against gravity for one lift will be
W = Fs = mgh = 10 $\times$ 9.8 $\times$ 0.5 = 49J
So work done for 1000 lifts = 49 $\times$ 1000 = 49000 J
efficiency = $Work \ done \over energy \ from \ fat$
energy from fat used = $Work \ done \over efficiency$
E = $ {W \over \eta } = {{49000} \over {0.2}} $ = 245000J
Energy from per kilogram fat = 3.8 ${\times }$ 10^7 J
Fat used $ = {{245000} \over {3.8 \times {{10}^7}}} $ = 64473 $\times$ 10^-7 = 6.45 $\times$ 10^-3 Kg

questions.question: A ring of mass m and radius r rotates about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to its plane with angular velocity $\omega$. Its kinetic energy is
questions.question_raw: A ring of mass m and radius r rotates about an axis passing through its centre and perpendicular to its plane with angular velocity {tex}\omega{/tex}. Its kinetic energy is
questions.explanation:
For a ring, I = mr^2
Rotational K.E. $=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{\omega}^{2}=\frac{1}{2} m r^{2} \omega^{2}$

questions.question: The escape velocity of a body, when projected from the earth's surface is 11.2 km s^-1. If it is projected at an angle of 60° with the horizontal, then escape velocity will be:
questions.question_raw: The escape velocity of a body, when projected from the earth's surface is 11.2 km s-1. If it is projected at an angle of 60° with the horizontal, then escape velocity will be:
questions.explanation:
The escape velocity of a body does not depend on its angle of projection from the earth's surface.
So, v_e = 11.2 km s^-1

questions.question: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 63 m/s. What is the displacement of the plane in m while it stopped in 2 s:
questions.question_raw: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 63 m/s. What is the displacement of the plane in m while it stopped in 2 s:
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity u = 63 m/s
At it stops final velocity v = 0 m/s
Time taken t = 2 s
v = u+ at
0 = 63 + 2a
a = -31.5 m/s^2
We know,
$s = ut +{1\over 2}at^2$
s = 63 $\times$2 + 1/2 $\times$ (-31.5)$\times$ 2^2
s = 63 m

questions.question: A bomb of mass 3.0 kg explodes in air into two pieces of masses 2.0 kg and 1.0 kg. The smaller mass goes at a speed of 80 ms^-1. The total energy imparted to the two fragments is
questions.question_raw: A bomb of mass 3.0 kg explodes in air into two pieces of masses 2.0 kg and 1.0 kg. The smaller mass goes at a speed of 80 ms-1. The total energy imparted to the two fragments is
questions.explanation:
By conservation of momentum,
MV = m_1v_1 + m_2v_2
3 $\times$ 0 = 2 $\times$ v_1 + 1 $\times$ 80
v_1 = -40 ms^-1
Total energy imparted to the two fragments
$=\frac{1}{2} m_{1} v_{1}^{2}+\frac{1}{2} m_{2} v_{2}^{2}$
= $\frac{1}{2} \times$ 2 $\times$ (40)^2 + $\frac{1}{2} \times$ 1 $\times$ (80)^2
= 4800 J = 4.8 kJ

questions.question: Static friction force:
questions.question_raw: Static friction force:

options.text: increases until the body start to move
decreases before moving and increases later

correct_option_texts: increases until the body start to move
questions.explanation:
It is the maximum friction, where a body just starts to move over the surface. 
for example. A large block of mass m is placed on a horizontal table. Apply a small force. the block does not move due to the friction force which balances the applied force. Now Gradually increase the applied force until it starts moving. This maximum applied force acting in the opposite direction is called Static Friction.

if radius of gyration is k then

questions.question: Two stars each of one solar mass (= 2 $ \times $ 10^30 kg) are approaching each other for a head-on collision. When they are a distance 10^9 km, their speeds are negligible. What is the speed with which they collide? The radius of each star is 10^4 km. assume the stars to remain undistorted until they collide. (Use the known value of G).
questions.question_raw: Two stars each of one solar mass (= 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 1030 kg) are approaching each other for a head-on collision. When they are a distance 109 km, their speeds are negligible. What is the speed with which they collide? The radius of each star is 104 km. assume the stars to remain undistorted until they collide. (Use the known value of G).
questions.explanation:
Let $\vec v_1$ and $\vec v_2$ be the velocities of two stars when they collide. 
According to the law of conservation of momentum,
$M{{\vec v}_1} + M{{\vec v}_2} = 0$
$\Rightarrow {{\vec v}_1} = - {{\vec v}_2}$
$\Rightarrow {v_1} = {v_2} = v$
According to law of conservation of energy,
${\text{2}}\left[ {\frac{1}{2}M{v^2}} \right] = G{M^2}\left[ {\frac{1}{{{r_2}}} - \frac{1}{{{r_1}}}} \right]$
We Have
Mass of the star,M = 2 $\times$ 10^30 kg
Distance between the stars, r_1 = 10^9 km = 10^12 m
Radius of star, r = 10^4km = 10^7m
The distance between two stars when they collide is,
r_2 = 2r = 2 $\times$ 10^7m
$\therefore$ The speed with which the stars collide is given by,
$v = \sqrt {GM\left[ {\frac{1}{{{r_2}}} - \frac{1}{{{r_1}}}} \right]}$
$v = \sqrt {6.67 \times {{10}^{ - 11}} \times 2 \times {{10}^{30}} \times \left[ {\frac{1}{{2 \times {{10}^7}}} - \frac{1}{{{{10}^{12}}}}} \right]}$
v = 2.6 $\times$ 10^6m/sec

questions.question: A trolley of mass 200 kg moves with a uniform speed of 36 km/h on a frictionless track. A child of mass 20 kg runs on the trolley from one end to the other (10 m away) with a speed of 4 ms^−1 relatives to the trolley in a direction opposite to its motion, and jumps out of the trolley. How much has the trolley moved from the time the child begins to run?
questions.question_raw: A trolley of mass 200 kg moves with a uniform speed of 36 km/h on a frictionless track. A child of mass 20 kg runs on the trolley from one end to the other (10 m away) with a speed of 4 ms−1 relatives to the trolley in a direction opposite to its motion, and jumps out of the trolley. How much has the trolley moved from the time the child begins to run?
questions.explanation:
Mass of trolly M = 200Kg
mass of child m = 20Kg
speed of trolley v = 36Km/hr = 36 $\times$ $5\over 18$ = 10m/s
Let v' be the final velocity of the trolley with respect to the ground.
The final velocity of the boy with respect to the ground = v' - 4 
from conservation of linear momentum
p_i = p_f
(M + m)v = Mv' + m(v' − 4)
(200 + 20) $\times$ 10 = 200v' + 20(v' − 4)
2200 = 220v' - 80
v' = $2280\over 220$ = 10.36m/s
Time taken by the boy to run, t = $10\over 4$ = 2.5 sec
Distance moved by the trolley = v't = 10.36 $\times$ 2.5 = 25.9 m

questions.question: A truck is coming towards you from a distance of 1000m on a straight road at time t=0. At time t=10 seconds it is at a distance of 900 m. The average velocity in m/sec is
questions.question_raw: A truck is coming towards you from a distance of 1000m on a straight road at time t=0. At time t=10 seconds it is at a distance of 900 m. The average velocity in m/sec is

Initial distance x_​​​​​​1 = 1000 m
Initial time t_​​​​​​1= 0 sec
Final distance, x_​​​​​​2 = 900 m
Final time t_​​​​​​2 = 10 sec
Average velocity, $v_{avg}={distance \ covered \over time \ taken}$ = ${1000-900\over 10-0}={100\over 10}= 10\ m/s$

questions.question: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 60 m/s. It stops in 2.0 s. What is the displacement of the plane when it stops?
questions.question_raw: A jet lands on an aircraft carrier at 60 m/s. It stops in 2.0 s. What is the displacement of the plane when it stops?
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity, u = 60 m/s
As it stops so final velocity, v = 0 m/s
Time taken t = 2 seconds
We know, v - u = at
$\Rightarrow a = {v-u\over t}\ \ .....(1)$
Also, $s = ut+{1\over 2}at^2$
From (1), we have
$s = ut+{1\over 2}({v-u\over t})t^2$
$\Rightarrow s = ut+{1\over 2}({v-u})t$
After putting given values, we have 
$\Rightarrow s = (60\times 2)+{1\over 2}(0-60)\times 2$
$\Rightarrow$ s = 120-60 = 60 m

questions.question:
Figure shows the orientation of two vectors u and v in the XY plane.
If u = a$\hat i$ + b$\hat j$ and v = p$\hat i$ + q$\hat j$
Which of the following is correct?
questions.question_raw:
Figure shows the orientation of two vectors u and v in the XY plane.
If u = a{tex}\hat i{/tex} + b{tex}\hat j{/tex} and v = p{tex}\hat i{/tex} + q{tex}\hat j{/tex}

Which of the following is correct?

options.text: a, q and b are positive while p is negative.
s and p are positive while b and q are negative.
a, b, p and q are all positive.
a, p and b are positive while q is negative.

correct_option_texts: a, p and b are positive while q is negative.
questions.explanation:
Main concept used: Sign of a, b, p and q are the sign of their resolving components in the XY direction.
Explanation: Components along X and Y axis of the vector $\bar{u}$ are both +X and Y direction, so a, b are positive.
Now if we resolve $\bar{v}$ its X component is in the +ve X direction but Y component will be in -ve Y
direction.
Hence, a, b and p are positive but q is negative.

questions.question: The potential energy of a system increases if work is done
questions.question_raw: The potential energy of a system increases if work is done

options.text: upon the system by a non-conservative force
upon the system by a conservative force
by the system against a conservative force
by the system against a non-conservative force

correct_option_texts: by the system against a conservative force
questions.explanation:
The work done by a conservative force is equal to the negative of the potential energy.
W_conservative = $-\Delta U$
$\Rightarrow \Delta U$ > 0 if W < 0
$\therefore$ The potential energy of the system increases if work is done by the system against a conservative force.

questions.question: Polar satellites:
questions.question_raw: Polar satellites:

options.text: are high altitude satellites, and go around the equator of the earth in a west-east direction
are low altitude satellites, and go around the poles of the earth in a north-south direction
are high altitude satellites, and go around the equator of the earth in a north-east direction
are low altitude satellites, and go around the equator of the earth in a north-east direction

correct_option_texts: are low altitude satellites, and go around the poles of the earth in a north-south direction
questions.explanation: A polar-orbiting satellite closely parallels the earth's meridian lines. It passes over the north and south poles each revolution. As the earth rotates to the east beneath the satellite, each pass monitors an area to the west of the previous pass at intervals of roughly 90 to 100 minutes. These strips can be pieced together to produce a picture of a larger area. They are used in monitoring the weather, observing the Earth’s surface, military uses including spying.

questions.question: The value of the gravitational constant G is
questions.question_raw: The value of the gravitational constant G is
questions.explanation:
The value of G was experimentally determined by Lord Henry Cavendish using a torsion balance. Cavendish brought two large lead spheres near the smaller spheres attached to the rod. Since all masses attract, the large spheres exerted a gravitational force upon the smaller spheres and twisted the rod a measurable amount. Once the torsional force balanced the gravitational force, the rod and spheres came to rest and Cavendish was able to determine the gravitational force of attraction between the masses. Cavendish expressed his result in terms of the density of the Earth.
$G = g{{R_{earth}^2} \over {{M_{earth}}}} = {{3g} \over {4\pi {R_{earth}}{\rho _{earth}}}}$
After converting to SI units, Cavendish's value for the Earth's density, 5.448 g cm^−3, gives
G = 6.74×10^−11 m^3 kg^–1 s^−2
Today, the currently accepted value is 6.67259 x 10^-11 N m^2/kg^2.

questions.question: A stone of mass 0.25 kg tied to the end of a string is whirled round in a circle of radius 1.5 m with a speed of 40 rev./min in a horizontal plane. What is the tension in the string?
questions.question_raw: A stone of mass 0.25 kg tied to the end of a string is whirled round in a circle of radius 1.5 m with a speed of 40 rev./min in a horizontal plane. What is the tension in the string?
questions.explanation:
Let, m = 0.25 kg, r = 1.5m and f = 40 rv/min = 40 rev /60 s = 2/3 rps
$\omega=2\pi f =2\pi \times\frac{2}{3}=\frac{4\pi}{3} \,rad\, s^{-1}$ 
Tension in the string = Centripetal force
so, $T=mr\omega^2=0.25\times1.5\times(\frac{4\pi}{3})^2$
6.6 N

questions.question:
Assertion (A): It is possible that acceleration decreases, while the velocity increases.
Reason (R): Acceleration is the change of velocity with respect to time.
questions.question_raw:

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.

correct_option_texts: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation: Acceleration is the change of velocity with respect to time. Therefore, when the acceleration decreases, it means that the change of velocity is less, in other words, the velocity is increasing slowly. So, the assertion and reason both are true, but the reason does not explain the assertion.

options.text: A is true but R is false.

According to definition of coefficient of restitution,
e = $-\frac{\text { velocity of separation }}{\text { velocity of approach }}$
For an elastic collision, 
velocity of separation = - velocity of approach

Assertion (A): It is most difficult to rotate a rod about an axis passing through one of its ends.
Reason (R): Moment of inertia about an axis passing through one end of the rod is more than the moment of inertia about an axis passing its through centre.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is false but R is true.

correct_option_texts: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation: Moment of inertia of a rod (r << l) about an axis passing through the centre and perpendicular to its length is $\frac{M l^{2}}{12}$, whereas the moment of inertia about the axis passing through one end is $\frac{M l^{2}}{3}$. So, it is comparatively difficult to rotate a rod about an axis passing through one of its ends.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Newton's universal law of gravitation is an inverse square law.
Reason (R): The force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): Newton's universal law of gravitation is an inverse square law.
Reason (R): The force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.

correct_option_texts: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation:
According to Newton's universal law of gravitation, everybody in the universe attracts every other body with a force that is directly proportional to the product of their masses and inversely proportional to the square of the distance between them.
So, this law is an inverse square law since the force is inversely proportional to the square of the distance between the two bodies.
So, the assertion and reason both are true and the reason explains the assertion.

questions.question: A drag racer starts her car from rest and accelerates at 10.0 ${\rm{m}}/{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}$ for the entire distance of 400 m.How long did it take the race car to travel this distance in seconds?
questions.question_raw: A drag racer starts her car from rest and accelerates at 10.0 {tex}{\rm{m}}/{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex} for the entire distance of 400 m.How long did it take the race car to travel this distance in seconds?
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity u = 0
Acceleration a = 10.0 m/s^​​​​​​2
Distance covered s = 400 m
Time is taken, t = ?
We know
$s = ut+{1\over 2}at^2$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $ 400= 0\times t+{1\over 2}\times 10\times t^2$ 
$\Rightarrow$ 400 = 5t^2 
$\Rightarrow$ t^2 = 80 
$\Rightarrow$ t = $ \sqrt {80}$ = 8.94 s

questions.question: Two bodies of masses 10 kg and 20 kg respectively kept on a smooth, horizontal surface are tied to the ends of a light string, a horizontal force F = 600 N is applied to the 10 kg mass so as to pull it. What is the tension in the string?
questions.question_raw: Two bodies of masses 10 kg and 20 kg respectively kept on a smooth, horizontal surface are tied to the ends of a light string, a horizontal force F = 600 N is applied to the 10 kg mass so as to pull it. What is the tension in the string?
questions.explanation:
Let, T= tension on the string
a = acceleration, Given that , m_1 = mass of light body is = 10 kg and m_2 = mass of heavy body is = 20 kg
$a=\frac{F}{m_1+m_2}=\frac{600}{30}$ = 20ms^-2
Now the tension in the string will be,
T = m_2a = 20 $\times$ 20 = 400N

questions.question: No physicist has ever “seen” an electron. Yet, all physicists believe in the existence of electrons. An intelligent but superstitious man advances this analogy to argue that ‘ghosts’ exist even though no one has seen one. How will you refute his argument ?
questions.question_raw: No physicist has ever “seen” an electron. Yet, all physicists believe in the existence of electrons. An intelligent but superstitious man advances this analogy to argue that ‘ghosts’ exist even though no one has seen one. How will you refute his argument ?

options.text: There is no scientific study of ghosts so they cannot exist
science does not deal with ghosts because they don’t exist
electrons have been discovered by great scientists but no great scientist claimed to find ghosts
Existence of electrons can be demonstrated by experiments but existence of ghosts cannot be

correct_option_texts: Existence of electrons can be demonstrated by experiments but existence of ghosts cannot be
questions.explanation: It is simply a superstition that ghost exist. There is not even single authentic evidence that ghosts exists. On the other hand it is a fact that atoms exists. There are many examples to prove this fact. Atomic power plant, atomic bombs, atomic clocks etc., exist of the correlation between two parts of the statement.

questions.question:
The measure of two quantities along with the precision of the respective measuring instrument is
A = 2.5 ms^-1 $\pm$ 0.5 ms^-1
B = 0.10 s $\pm$ 0.01 s
The value of AB will be
questions.question_raw:
The measure of two quantities along with the precision of the respective measuring instrument is
A = 2.5 ms-1 {tex}\pm{/tex} 0.5 ms-1
B = 0.10 s {tex}\pm{/tex} 0.01 s
The value of AB will be
questions.explanation:
According to the problem,
A = 2.5 ms^-1 $\pm$ 0.5 ms^-1, B = 0.10s $\pm$ 0.01 s
Z = AB = (2.5)(0.10) = 0.25 m
$\frac{\Delta Z}{Z}=\frac{\Delta A}{A}+\frac{\Delta B}{B}$ 
$\Delta Z$ = $Z\left(\frac{\Delta A}{A}+\frac{\Delta B}{B}\right)$ 
= $0.25\left(\frac{0.5}{2.5}+\frac{0.01}{0.10}\right)$ = 0.25 (0.2 + 0.1) = 0.075
$\Delta Z$ = 0.075 = 0.08m (rounding off to two significant figures.)
thus, measured value of AB, i.e., $Z \pm \Delta Z$ = (0.25 $\pm$ 0.08) m

questions.question: A truck on a straight road starts from rest, accelerating at 2.00 m/s^2 until it reaches a speed of 20.0 m/s. Then the truck travels for 20.0 s at a constant speed until the brakes are applied, stopping the truck in a uniform manner in an additional 5.00 s. What is the average velocity in m/s of the truck for the motion described?
questions.question_raw: A truck on a straight road starts from rest, accelerating at 2.00 m/s2 until it reaches a speed of 20.0 m/s. Then the truck travels for 20.0 s at a constant speed until the brakes are applied, stopping the truck in a uniform manner in an additional 5.00 s. What is the average velocity in m/s of the truck for the motion described?
questions.explanation:
As start from rest,
So Initial velocity u = 0 m/s
Final velocity v = 20 m/s
Acceleration a = 2 m/s^​​​​​​2
Let Time during this period = t_​​​​​​1
Also let distance covered = s_1
We know,
v - u = at
So, 20 − 0 = 2t_1
$t_1 = {20\over 2}$ =10 s
Also,
v^2_ − u^2= 2as_1
$\Rightarrow$ 400 − 0 = 2 × 2 × s_1
$s_1 = {400\over 4}$ =100 m 
Now travel with constant speed of 20 m/s for time t_2 = 20 s
Distance covered 
s_2 = 20 $\times$ 20 = 400 m
Time taken to stop
t_3 = 5s
Before stopping it covers distance $= s_3$
$s_3={1\over 2}(20-0)5$ = 50 m
Total distance covered = 100 + 400 + 50 = 550 m
Total time of motion t = 10 + 20 + 5 = 35 s
Average velocity $v_{avg} = {total\ distance\over total \ time}$ 
$= {550\over 35}$ = 15.7 m/s

questions.question: A man stands on the roof of a 15.0 m tall building and throws a rock with a velocity of magnitude 30.0 m/s at an angle of 33.0° above the horizontal. You can ignore air resistance. Calculate the magnitude of the velocity of the rock just before it strikes the ground.
questions.question_raw: A man stands on the roof of a 15.0 m tall building and throws a rock with a velocity of magnitude 30.0 m/s at an angle of 33.0° above the horizontal. You can ignore air resistance. Calculate the magnitude of the velocity of the rock just before it strikes the ground.
questions.explanation:
Let downward be the y-direction.
v_ox = v_o $\times$ cos$\theta$ = 30.0 $\times$ cos33.0^o
= 25.2 m/s
v_oy = v_o $\times$ sin$\theta$ = 30.0 $\times$ sin33.0^o
= 16.3 m/s
The velocity in the x direction is constant all the time,
At the ground, we have h = -15.0 m, 
We know,
(v_y)^2 = (v_oy)^2 + 2a_yh
$\Rightarrow$ $v_y=\sqrt {( v_{oy})^2+2a_yh}$ 
$v_y=\sqrt {( 16.3)^2+2(-9.8)(-15.0)} $
$\Rightarrow$ v_y = 23.7 m/s
The magnitude of the velocity of the rock just before it strikes the ground is
v = $\sqrt {(v_x)^2+(v_y)^2} $ 
$\Rightarrow$ v = $\sqrt{ (25.2)^2+(23.7)^2} $ = 34.6 m/s

There are three rules on determining how many significant figures are in a number: 
 Non-zero digits are always significant.
 Any zeros between two significant digits are significant.
 A final zero or trailing zeros in the decimal portion ONLY are significant.
So keeping these rules in mind, there are 3 significant digits.

questions.question: The asteroid Toro has a radius of about 5.0 km. assuming that the density of Toro is the same as that of the earth (5.5 g/cm^3) Suppose an object is to be placed in a circular orbit around Toro, with a radius just slightly larger than the asteroid’s radius. What is the speed of the object?. Mass of the earth = 6.0 $ \times $ 10^24 kg; radius of the earth = 6.4 $ \times $ 10^6 m; G = 6.67 $ \times $ 10^-11 Nm^2 kg^-2.
questions.question_raw: The asteroid Toro has a radius of about 5.0 km. assuming that the density of Toro is the same as that of the earth (5.5 g/cm3) Suppose an object is to be placed in a circular orbit around Toro, with a radius just slightly larger than the asteroid’s radius. What is the speed of the object?. Mass of the earth = 6.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 1024 kg; radius of the earth = 6.4 {tex} \times {/tex} 106 m; G = 6.67 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-11 Nm2 kg-2.
questions.explanation:
We know Orbital velocity:
V_orbital = $=\sqrt{\frac{G M_{t o r o}}{r}}$ .....(1)
We can calculate the mass of Toro by mass density relationship:
Mass = Volume $\times$ Density
$\Rightarrow$ $M_{\text {toro}}=\frac{4}{3} \pi R_{\text {toro}}^{3} \times \rho_{\text {toro}}$
Here R_toro = 5.0 km = 5000 m 
$\rho_{\text {toro}}=5.5 \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{cm}^{3}=55 \times 10^{2} \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{m}^{3}$
$\Rightarrow $ M_toro = (4/3)$\pi$(5000)^3 $\times$ 55 $\times$ 10^2
$\Rightarrow $ M_toro = (4/3) $\times$ (22/7) $\times$ 125 $\times$ 10^9 $\times$ 55 $\times$ 10^2 $\Rightarrow $ M_toro = 2.9 $\times$ 10^15kg
Putting Value Of M_toro,G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^-11and r $\approx$ R_toro = 5000 in Eqn (1)
$V_{o r b i t a l}=\sqrt{\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 2.9 \times 10^{15}}{5000}}$
$\Rightarrow V_{\text {orbital}}=\sqrt{\frac{19.34 \times 10^{4}}{5 \times 10^{3}}}$
$\Rightarrow V_{\text {orbital}}=\sqrt{3.886 \times 10}$
$\Rightarrow V_{\text {orbital}}=\sqrt{38.86}$ = 6.2m/sec

questions.question: The escape speed of a projectile on the earth’s surface is 11.2 km sec^-1. A body is projected out with thrice this speed. What is the speed of the body far away from the earth? Ignore the presence of the sun and other planets.
questions.question_raw: The escape speed of a projectile on the earth’s surface is 11.2 km sec-1. A body is projected out with thrice this speed. What is the speed of the body far away from the earth? Ignore the presence of the sun and other planets.
questions.explanation:
The escape velocity of a projectile from the Earth, V_esc = 11.2 km/s
Projection velocity of the projectile, V_p = 3V_esc
Mass of the projectile = m
The velocity of the projectile far away from the Earth = V_f
The total energy of the projectile on the earth$= \frac{1}{2}m{v_p}^2 - \frac{1}{2}m{v_{esc}}^2$ 
The gravitational potential energy of the projectile far away from the Earth is zero.
The total energy of the projectile far away from the Earth = $\frac{1}{2}m{v_f}^2$ 
From the law of conservation of energy, we have
$= \frac{1}{2}m{v_p}^2 - \frac{1}{2}m{v_{esc}}^2 = \frac{1}{2}m{v_f}^2$ 
${v_f} = \sqrt {{v_p}^2 - {v_{esc}}^2}$ 
$ = \sqrt {{{\left( {3{v_{esc}}} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {{v_{esc}}} \right)}^2}}$ 
$= \sqrt {{{\left( {3{v_{esc}}} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {{v_{esc}}} \right)}^2}}$
$=\sqrt 8 {v_{esc}}$
$=\sqrt 8 \times 11.2 $ = 31.68 km/sec

questions.question: Two heavy spheres each of mass 100 kg and radius 0.10 m are placed 1.0 m apart on a horizontal table. What is the gravitational force and potential at the midpoint of the line joining the centers of the spheres? Is an object placed at that point in equilibrium? If so, is the equilibrium stable or unstable?
questions.question_raw: Two heavy spheres each of mass 100 kg and radius 0.10 m are placed 1.0 m apart on a horizontal table. What is the gravitational force and potential at the midpoint of the line joining the centers of the spheres? Is an object placed at that point in equilibrium? If so, is the equilibrium stable or unstable?
questions.explanation:
Here, G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^−11N m^2 kg^−2
M = 100 kg
R = 0.1 m
Distance between the two spheres d = 1.0 m
Suppose that the distance of either sphere from the midpoint of the line joining their centre is r. Then
r = $d\over2$ = 0.5 m
The gravitational field at the midpoint due to two spheres will be equal and opposite.
Hence, the resultant gravitational field at the midpoint = 0
The gravitational potential at the midpoint.
$=\left(\frac{-G M}{r}\right) \times 2$
$=-\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 100 \times 2}{0.5}$ 
= −2.7 $\times$ 10^−8 Jkg^-1
As the effective force on the body placed at the midpoint is zero, so the body is in equilibrium, if the body is displaced a little towards either mass body from its equilibrium position, it will not return to its initial position of equilibrium. Hence, the body is in unstable equilibrium.

$\Rightarrow$ v = 2 m/s

The kinetic energy of rotation
K =$\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ 
$n = \frac{600} {60} = 10$ revolution per second
angular velocity $\omega = 2\pi n $ = 2$\pi$ $\times$ 10 = 20 rad/sec
moment of inertia about an axis passing through centre of a solid sphere
$I =\frac {2}{ 5}M{R^2}$ 
The kinetic energy of rotation
$K=\frac{1}{2} I \omega^2=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{5} M R^2 \times(20 \pi)^2$ = 80$\pi$^2MR^2

$=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} m r^{2} \times \frac{v^{2}}{r^{2}}$
$=\frac{1}{2} m v^{2}+\frac{1}{4} m v^{2}=\frac{3}{4} m v^{2}$
$\frac{E_{\text {rot }}}{E_{\text {tot }}}=\frac{\frac{1}{4} m v^{2}}{\frac{3}{4} m v^{2}}=\frac{1}{3}$ = 1 : 3

questions.question: The unit of length convenient on the atomic scale is known as an angstrom and is denoted by ${\mathop A\limits^0}:{\rm{ 1 }}{\mathop A\limits^0}{\rm{ }} = {\rm{ 1}}{0^{ - {\rm{1}}0}}$ m. questions.question: At what distance above the surface of the earth is the acceleration due to the earth’s gravity 0.980 m/s^2 if the acceleration due to gravity at the surface has magnitude 9.8 m/s^2.
questions.question_raw: At what distance above the surface of the earth is the acceleration due to the earth’s gravity 0.980 m/s2 if the acceleration due to gravity at the surface has magnitude 9.8 m/s2.
questions.explanation:
We know:
$g^{\prime}=g\left(\frac{R^{2}}{(R+h)^{2}}\right)$
or 
g' = $g\left(\frac{R^{2}}{r^{2}}\right)$
where R = Radius of earth = 6371 km = 6371$\times$ 10^3 m
r = R + h
g = Acceleration due to gravity on the surface of earth
g′ = Acceleration due to gravity at height h
Putting above values in the relation, we get:
$r^{2}=\frac{9.8 \times\left(6371 \times 10^{3}\right)^{2}}{0.98}$
$\Rightarrow r=\sqrt{10} \times 6371 \times 10^{3}$
$\Rightarrow$ r = 20146.87 $\times$ 10^3
We know r = R + h
$\Rightarrow$ h = r - R
$\Rightarrow h=20132.36 \times 10^{3}-6371 \times 10^{3}$
$\Rightarrow$ h = 10^3(20132.36 - 6371)
$\Rightarrow$ h = 13761.36 $\times$ 10^3 = 1.37 $\times$ 10^7m

questions.question: A rocket is fired vertically with a speed of 5 km sec^-1 ^ ^ from the earth’s surface. How far from the earth does the rocket go before returning to the earth? Mass of the earth = 6.0 $ \times $ 10^24 kg; mean radius of the earth = 6.4 $ \times $ 10^6 m; G = 6.67 $ \times $ 10^-11 N m^2 kg^-2.
questions.question_raw: A rocket is fired vertically with a speed of 5 km sec-1   from the earth’s surface. How far from the earth does the rocket go before returning to the earth? Mass of the earth = 6.0 {tex} \times {/tex} 1024 kg; mean radius of the earth = 6.4 {tex} \times {/tex} 106  m; G = 6.67 {tex} \times {/tex} 10-11 N m2 kg-2.
questions.explanation:
Work done in bringing a mass m against a mass M at a distance 'x' through a distance dx 
= Gravitational force $\times$ Distance = $\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{x^{2}} d x$
Work done in bringing it from $\infty$ to r = GMm$\left[\frac{1}{\infty}-\frac{1}{R}\right]$
$=-\mathrm{GMm}\left[\frac{1}{R}-\frac{1}{\infty}\right]$
Therefore Gravitational Potential Energy, $W =\frac{-\mathrm{GMm}}{R}$
Initial Total Energy = Kinetic Energy + Potential Energy
$=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}-\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{R}$
Final Total Energy = 0 - $\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{(R+h)}$
Applying law of conservation of energy:
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}-\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{R}=0-\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{(R+h)}$
$\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{(R+h)}=\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{R}-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mv}^{2}$
$\frac{G M}{(R+h)}=\frac{1}{R}\left(G M-\frac{1}{2} R v^{2}\right)$
$\frac{(R+h)}{R}=\frac{G M}{\left(G M-\frac{1}{2} R v^{2}\right)}$
$(R+h)=\frac{G M R}{\left(G M-\frac{1}{2} R v^{2}\right)}$
h = $\frac{G M R}{\left(G M-\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Rv}^{2}\right)}-R$
$h=\frac{\frac{1}{2} R^{2} v^{2}}{\left(G M-\frac{1}{2} R v^{2}\right)}$
$h=\frac{\left[\frac{1}{2} \times\left(6.4 \times 10^{6}\right)^{2} \times\left(5 \times 10^{3}\right)^{2}\right]}{\left[\left(6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 6 \times 10^{24}\right)-\left(\frac{1}{2} \times 6.4 \times 10^{6} \times\left(5 \times 10^{3}\right)^{2}\right]\right.} $m
$=\frac{\left(512 \times 10^{18}\right)}{40 \times 10^{13}-80 \times 10^{12}}$
=1.6 × 10^6m
Height achieved by the rocket with respect to the centre of the Earth = Re + h
= 6.4 $\times$ 10^6 + 1.6 $\times$ 10^6 = 8 $\times$ 10^6 m.

questions.question: A body of mass 10 kg lies on a rough horizontal surface. When a horizontal force of F Newtons acts on it, it gets an acceleration of 5 m/s^2 and when the horizontal force is doubled, it gets an acceleration of 18 m/s^2. The coefficient of friction between the body and the horizontal surface (assume g = 10 m/s^2) is
questions.question_raw: A body of mass 10 kg lies on a rough horizontal surface. When a horizontal force of F Newtons acts on it, it gets an acceleration of 5 m/s2 and when the horizontal force is doubled, it gets an acceleration of 18 m/s2. The coefficient of friction between the body and the horizontal surface (assume g = 10 m/s2) is
questions.explanation:
If f is the force friction, then
F - f = m $\times$ 5
2F - f = m $\times$ 18
On solving,
f = 8m
$\mu=\frac{f}{R}$
$=\frac{8 m}{m g}=\frac{8}{10}$ = 0.8

options.text: A is true but R is false.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is false but R is true.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): During a turn, the value of centripetal force should be less than the limiting frictional force.
Reason (R): The centripetal force is provided by the frictional force between the tyres and the road.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): During a turn, the value of centripetal force should be less than the limiting frictional force.
Reason (R): The centripetal force is provided by the frictional force between the tyres and the road.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.

correct_option_texts: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation:
The body is able to move on a circular path due to centripetal force. The centripetal force in case of vehicle is provided by frictional force. Thus, if the value of frictional force, $\mu$mg is less than centripetal force, then it is not possible fbr a vehicle to take a turn and the bicycle would overturn. Thus, condition for no overturning of vehicle is,
$\mu m g \geq \frac{m v^{2}}{r}$

Reason (R): Total degree of freedom is addition of translatory motion and rotatory motion.

Reason (R): Total degree of freedom is addition of translatory motion and rotatory motion.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

correct_option_texts: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation: Let the diatomic molecule has centre of mass at O. Such a molecules have three degree of freedom due to translational motion of the centre of mass. The molecule can rotate about the axes XX', YY', and ZZ' all are perpendicular to each other. The rotatory motion provides two more degrees of freedom. Thus the diatomic molecule has five degrees of freedom.

questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): A body becomes weightless at the centre of earth.
Reason (R): As the distance from centre of earth decreases, acceleration due to gravity increases.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is false but R is true.

correct_option_texts: A is true but R is false.
questions.explanation:
Variation of g with depth from surface of earth is given by g' = $g\left(1-\frac{d}{R}\right)$
At the centre of earth, d = R,
$\therefore$ g' = $g\left(1-\frac{R}{R}\right)$ = 0
$\therefore$ Apparent weight of body = mg' = 0

questions.question: A monkey of mass 40 kg climbs on a rope which can stand a maximum tension of 600 N. What is the tension in the rope if the monkey falls down the rope nearly freely under gravity?
questions.question_raw: A monkey of mass 40 kg climbs on a rope which can stand a maximum tension of 600 N. What is the tension in the rope if the monkey falls down the rope nearly freely under gravity?
questions.explanation:
When the monkey fall down down the rope nearly free under the gravity, then a = g and we have
T = m(g − a) = m(g − g) = 0N

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:
A swimmer has to cross a river. The swimmer can swim at a velocity v in still water. The river is flowing with a velocity u.
Swimmer starts swimming from point A at an angle $\theta$ with the direction of flow of river.
The effective velocities of the swimmer are Along the flow: u + v cos $\theta$
Perpendicular to the flow: v sin $\theta$
 If the swimmer wishes to cross the river in minimum time:
 t = $\frac{\text{width of river}}{velocity}$
 = $\frac{b}{v} \sin \theta$
 For t_min, sin $\theta$ = 1, i.e., $\theta$ = 90^o and t_min = $\frac{b}{v}$
 So, the swimmer should start swimming along AB, i.e., perpendicular to the direction of flow. But he will be dragged to point C.
 BC = $(u+v \cos \theta) \times t_{\min }$
 = $\left(u+v \cos 90^{\circ}\right) \times \frac{b}{v}$ 
 = $\frac{u b}{v}$
 The resultant velocity $\sqrt{u^{2}+v^{2}}$ is along AC.
 If the swimmer wishes to cross the river by swimming minimum distance:
 There should be no velocity component along the flow of river.
 So, u + v cos $\theta$ = 0
 $\theta$ = cos^-1 $\left(-\frac{u}{v}\right)$
If a swimmer wants to cross a river in minimum time then he should swim at an angle ________ with the direction of flow.
questions.question_raw:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:

A swimmer has to cross a river. The swimmer can swim at a velocity v in still water. The river is flowing with a velocity u.

Swimmer starts swimming from point A at an angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the direction of flow of river.
The effective velocities of the swimmer are Along the flow: u + v cos {tex}\theta{/tex}
Perpendicular to the flow: v sin {tex}\theta{/tex}

  1. If the swimmer wishes to cross the river in minimum time:
    t = {tex}\frac{\text{width of river}}{velocity}{/tex}
    = {tex}\frac{b}{v} \sin \theta{/tex}
    For tmin, sin {tex}\theta{/tex} = 1, i.e., {tex}\theta{/tex} = 90o and tmin = {tex}\frac{b}{v}{/tex}
    So, the swimmer should start swimming along AB, i.e., perpendicular to the direction of flow. But he will be dragged to point C.
    BC = {tex}(u+v \cos \theta) \times t_{\min }{/tex}
    = {tex}\left(u+v \cos 90^{\circ}\right) \times \frac{b}{v}{/tex} 
    = {tex}\frac{u b}{v}{/tex}
    The resultant velocity {tex}\sqrt{u^{2}+v^{2}}{/tex} is along AC.
  2. If the swimmer wishes to cross the river by swimming minimum distance:
    There should be no velocity component along the flow of river.
    So, u + v cos {tex}\theta{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos-1 {tex}\left(-\frac{u}{v}\right){/tex}

If a swimmer wants to cross a river in minimum time then he should swim at an angle ________ with the direction of flow.
questions.explanation:
If the swimmer wishes to cross the river in minimum time, then
t = $\frac{\text{width of river}}{velocity}$
= $\frac{b}{v}$ sin $\theta$
For t_min, sin $\theta$ = 1, i.e., $\theta$ = 90^o

options.text: Yes, if the velocity of flow of river is equal to the speed of the swimmer.
No. He will be dragged away to a point opposite the direction of flow of river

Swimmer will be dragged to point C along the direction of flow of river.
BC = $(u+v \cos \theta) \times t_{\min }$ 
= $\left(u+v \cos 90^{\circ}\right) \times \frac{b}{v}$
= $\frac{u b}{v}$

The Question Paper contains three sections.
 Section A has 25 questions. Attempt any 20 questions.

 All questions carry equal marks.

questions.question: Which of these were not major technological advances during the industrial revolution in England and Europe?
questions.question_raw: Which of these were not major technological advances during the industrial revolution in England and Europe?
questions.explanation: In 1957, the world was surprised to see the Soviets launch the first satellite into orbit, with most expecting the USA to be the first to do so. Satellite technology got wings after this year which is so far after the industrial revolution in England and Europe.

questions.question: A swimmer wishes to cross a 500 m wide river flowing at 5 km/hr. His speed with respect to water is 3 km/hr. If he heads in the direction making an angle $\theta $ with the flow, find the time he takes to cross the river?
questions.question_raw: A swimmer wishes to cross a 500 m wide river flowing at 5 km/hr. His speed with respect to water is 3 km/hr. If he heads in the direction making an angle {tex}\theta {/tex} with the flow, find the time he takes to cross the river?
questions.explanation:
The velocity of man w.r.t. water = 3 kmph. Thus the velocity of water is not necessary here because the velocity of man includes the contribution of the velocity of water because the given velocity of man is with respect to the flowing water which is the resultant velocity. The breadth of the river = 500 m = 0.5 km. Component of the velocity of man w.r.t. river along horizontal axis, v = 3sin($\theta$)
Now time is taken to cross the river, t = $\frac{\text { Distance }}{\text { velocity along horizontal axis }}=\frac{0.5}{3 \sin \theta}$ hrs 
$=\frac{0.5 \times 60}{3 \sin \theta}=\frac{10}{\sin \theta}$ minutes

questions.question: A graph of x versus t is shown in Fig. Choose the correct alternatives from below.
questions.question_raw: A graph of x versus t is shown in Fig. Choose the correct alternatives from below.

options.text: At C, the velocity and the acceleration vanish.
The particle was released from rest at t = 0.
The speed at D exceeds that at E.
Average velocity for the motion between A and D is positive.

correct_option_texts: The speed at D exceeds that at E.
questions.explanation: Velocity at A is Zero as x -t parallel to time axis so average velocity at A is zero. At D displacement or slope is negative. So, the average velocity at D is negative, not positive.

questions.question: Rain is falling vertically with a speed of 30 m${{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{1}}}}$. A woman rides a bicycle with a speed of 10 m${{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{1}}}}$ in the north to south direction. What is the direction in which she should hold her umbrella?
questions.question_raw: Rain is falling vertically with a speed of 30 m{tex}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{1}}}}{/tex}. A woman rides a bicycle with a speed of 10 m{tex}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{1}}}}{/tex} in the north to south direction. What is the direction in which she should hold her umbrella?

options.text: about ${\rm{28}}^\circ $ with the vertical, towards the south
about ${\rm{18}}^\circ $ with the vertical, towards the south
about ${\rm{28}}^\circ $ with the vertical, towards the north
about ${\rm{18}}^\circ $ with the vertical, towards the north

correct_option_texts: about ${\rm{18}}^\circ $ with the vertical, towards the south
questions.explanation:
The described situation is shown in the given figure.
Here,
v_c = Velocity of the cyclist
v_r = Velocity of falling rain
In order to protect herself from the rain, the woman must hold her umbrella in the direction of the relative velocity (v) of the rain with respect to the woman.
v = v_r + (-v_c) = 30 + (-10) = 20 m/s
tan $\theta $ = ${v_c \over v_r}= {10\over 30}$ 
$\theta $ = tan^-1 $1 \over 3$
$\theta $ $\approx$ 18^0
Hence, the woman must hold the umbrella toward the south, at an angle of nearly 18° with the vertical.

questions.question: A ball is thrown upwards with an initial velocity of 10 m ${{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{1}}}}$. Determine the maximum height reached above the thrower’s hand.
questions.question_raw: A ball is thrown upwards with an initial velocity of 10 m {tex}{{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{1}}}}{/tex}. Determine the maximum height reached above the thrower’s hand.
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity is given by ,u = 10m/s
As at the maximum height ball will stop, so final velocity is given by v = 0 m/s
Only acceleration working on it is the acceleration due to gravity g = -9.8m/s^​​​​​​2
Let height = h
We know that, $v^2-u^2= 2as$
$\Rightarrow$ $(0)^2-(10)^2= 2\times (-9.8)h$

questions.question: The angular velocity of a body changes forms 1 rev/sec to 16 rev/sec. Without applying any external torque. The ratio of its radius of gyration in the two cases is:

questions.explanation:
I_1$\omega$_1 = I_2$\omega$_2
$\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\frac{\omega_{2}}{\omega_{1}}$ 
$\omega$_1 = 1rev/s
$\omega$_2 = 16rev/s
if the radius of gyration is k_1 and k_2 then 
$\frac{M k_{1}^{2}}{M k_{2}^{2}}=\frac{\omega_{2}}{\omega_{1}}$
$\frac{k_{1}}{k_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{\omega_{2}}{\omega_{1}}}=\sqrt{\frac{16}{1}}=\frac{4}{1}$
k_1:k_2 = 4:1

$=72 \times \frac{5}{18} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}=20 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$

$\theta$ = 2 $\pi \times$ 20 rad
As $\omega^{2}-\omega_{0}^{2}=2 \alpha \theta$
$\therefore \quad 0^{2}-(80)^{2}=2 \alpha \times 2 \pi \times 20$

So, $\vec v = 3.0\hat i+4t\hat j$
Velocity after 2 seconds is:
$\vec v= 3\hat i+8\hat j$
Magnitude of velocity = $\sqrt {(3)^2+(8)^2}$ = $\sqrt {73}$= 8.54 ms^​​​​​-1
Direction is given by
$\theta = tan^{-1}({8\over 3})= 69.5\approx 70° $ with x-axis

questions.question: A 3.00-kg crate slides down a ramp. The ramp is 1.00 m in length and inclined at an angle of 30.0^o. The crate starts from rest at the top, experiences a constant frictional force of magnitude 5.00 N, and continues to move a short distance on the flat floor after it leaves the ramp. questions.question_raw: A 3.00-kg crate slides down a ramp. The ramp is 1.00 m in length and inclined at an angle of 30.0o. The crate starts from rest at the top, experiences a constant frictional force of magnitude 5.00 N, and continues to move a short distance on the flat floor after it leaves the ramp. Use energy methods to determine the speed of the crate at the bottom of the ramp?
questions.explanation:
Height of ramp 
h = lsin30^o = 1 $\times$ 1/2 = 0.5m
from work kinetic energy theorem
K_f - K_i = W_mg + W_f
$1\over 2$mv^2 - 0 = mgh + fd
$1\over 2$ $\times$ 3v^2 = (3 $\times$ 9.8 $\times$0.5) + [5 $\times$ ( - 1)]
$\frac{3}{2}$v^2 = 14.7 - 5
v^2 = 9.7 $\times$ $\frac{2}{3}$
v = $\sqrt{\frac{9.7 \times 2}{3}}$ = 2.54m/s

questions.question: A child swinging on a swing in a sitting position stands up. Then, the time period of the swing will:
questions.question_raw: A child swinging on a swing in a sitting position stands up. Then, the time period of the swing will:

options.text: Increase if the child is tall and decreases if the child is short

questions.explanation:
As the child stand up then Centre of mass is shifted in upwards direction from the sitting position of the child, thus decreasing the effective length of the swing, so as the time period is proportional to the square root of length, thus time period decreases.
time period 
$T = 2\pi \sqrt {{l \over g}} $
$T \propto \sqrt l $
The time period of swing decreases as effective length decreases.

questions.question: For a circularly orbiting satellite the potential energy:
questions.question_raw: For a circularly orbiting satellite the potential energy:

options.text: is negative, and having a magnitude twice that of the negative kinetic energy.
is positive, and having a magnitude twice that of the positive kinetic energy.
is negative, and has a magnitude thrice that of the positive kinetic energy.
is negative, and has a magnitude twice that of the positive kinetic energy.

correct_option_texts: is negative, and has a magnitude twice that of the positive kinetic energy.
questions.explanation:
In the case of circularly orbiting satellite,
Potential Energy = ${\text{ }}\frac{{ - GMm}}{r} = \frac{{ - {L^2}}}{{m{r^2}}}$
Kinetic Energy = $\frac{G M m}{2 r}=\frac{L^{2}}{2 m r^{2}}$ 
Comparing both, we can say that the potential energy is negative and has a magnitude twice that of the positive kinetic energy.

questions.question: A car traveling at a constant speed of 45.0 m/s passes a trooper hidden behind a billboard. One second after the speeding car passes the billboard; the trooper sets out from the billboard to catch it, accelerating at a constant rate of 3 m/s^2. How long does it take her to overtake the car?
questions.question_raw: A car traveling at a constant speed of 45.0 m/s passes a trooper hidden behind a billboard. One second after the speeding car passes the billboard; the trooper sets out from the billboard to catch it, accelerating at a constant rate of 3 m/s2. How long does it take her to overtake the car?
questions.explanation:
The initial speed of the car u = 45 m/s
Let t = 0 s when the car passes the trooper.
The trooper starts from rest 1 s after the car passes the billboard.
In this 1 s the car would have covered a distance of 45 m.
Let y be the time at which the trooper overtakes the car.
Distance covered by car at time y
S = 45 + uy = 45 + 45y......(1)
(The distance is measured from the billboard)
The same distance is covered by the trooper also.
S = 0 + $1\over 2$ $\times$ 3 $\times$(y)^2....(2)
(Initial speed of trooper = 0).
Equating (1) and (2)
45 + 45y = $3\over 2$(y)^2
$\Rightarrow$ 3y^2 - 90y - 90 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ y^2 - 30y - 30 = 0
Using quadratic formula, we get:
$\Rightarrow$ $ {30 ± \sqrt {900+120} \over 2}$ = ${30 \pm 31.93\over 2 }$ = 30.97 s $\approx$ 31 s 
neglected negative value of y.

questions.question: A spaceship is stationed on Mars. How much energy must be expended on the spaceship to launch it out of the solar system? Mass of the space ship = 1000 kg; the mass of the sun = 2 $ \times $ 10^30 kg; mass of mars = 6.4 $ \times $ 10^23 kg; radius of mars = 3395 km; radius of the orbit of mars = 2.28 $ \times $ 10^8 km; G = 6.67 $ \times $ 10^11 N m^2 kg^-2.
questions.question_raw: A spaceship is stationed on Mars. How much energy must be expended on the spaceship to launch it out of the solar system? Mass of the space ship = 1000 kg; the mass of the sun = 2 {tex} \times {/tex} 1030 kg; mass of mars = 6.4 {tex} \times {/tex} 1023 kg; radius of mars = 3395 km; radius of the orbit of mars = 2.28 {tex} \times {/tex} 108 km; G = 6.67 {tex} \times {/tex} 1011 N m2 kg-2.
questions.explanation:
Mass of the spaceship is given by, m_s = 1000 kg
Mass of the Sun is given by, M = 2 $\times$ 10^30kg
Mass of Mars is given by, M_m = 6.4 $\times$ 10^23kg
Orbital radius of Mars, r = 3395 km = 3.395 $\times$ 10^6m
Universal gravitational constant, G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^-11 Nm^2kg^-2
Potential energy of the spaceship due to gravitational attraction of the Sun = - $\frac{GM{m_s}}{R}$
Potential energy of the spaceship due to gravitational attraction of the Mars = - $\frac{{G{M_m}{m_s}}}{r}$
Since the spaceship is stationed on Mars, its velocity and hence, its kinetic energy will be zero.
Total energy of the spaceship = $- \frac{{GM{m_s}}}{R}{\text{ - }}\frac{{G{M_m}{m_s}}}{r}$
$= - G{m_s}\left[ {\frac{M}{R} + \left( {\frac{{{M_m}}}{r}} \right)} \right]$
The negative sign indicates that the system is in bound state.
Energy required for launching the spaceship out of the solar system
= - Total energy of the spaceship = $G{m_s}\left[ {\frac{M}{R} + \left( {\frac{{{M_m}}}{r}} \right)} \right] $$= 6.67 \times {10^{ - 11}} \times {10^3} \times \left( {\frac{{2 \times {{10}^{30}}}}{{2.28 \times {{10}^{11}}}} + \frac{{6.4 \times {{10}^{23}}}}{{3.395 \times {{10}^6}}}} \right) $
= 6.67 $\times$ 10^-8 (87.72 $\times$ 10^17 + 1.88 $\times$ 10^17) 
= 6.67 $\times$ 10^-8 $\times$ 89.50 $\times$ 10^17
= 596.96 $\times$ 10^9
= 5.96 $\times$ 10^11 J

questions.question: A block having a mass of 0.80 kg is given an initial velocity of 1.2 m/s to the right and collides with a spring of negligible mass and force constant k = 50 N/m. Assuming the surface to be frictionless, calculate the maximum compression of the spring after the collision.
questions.question_raw: A block having a mass of 0.80 kg is given an initial velocity of 1.2 m/s to the right and collides with a spring of negligible mass and force constant k = 50 N/m. Assuming the surface to be frictionless, calculate the maximum compression of the spring after the collision.
questions.explanation:
for maximum compression 
${1 \over 2}k{x^2} = {1 \over 2}m{v^2}$
x = $ \sqrt {{{m{v^2}} \over k}} = \sqrt {{{0.8 \times 1.2 \times 1.2} \over {50}}} $ = 0.15m
the maximum compression of the spring after the collision = 0.15m.

questions.question: For Geostationary Satellites:

circular orbit is in the equatorial plane of the earth and period of rotation is 14 hrs

correct_option_texts: a circular orbit is in the equatorial plane of the earth and period of rotation is 24 hrs
questions.explanation:
If there is a satellite in the equatorial plane rotating in the direction of earth’s rotation from west to east, then for an observer, on the earth, the angular velocity of the satellite will be $({\omega _s} - {\omega _E})$ 
The time interval between the two consecutive appearances overhead will be,
$T = \frac{{2\pi }}{{{\omega _s} - {\omega _E}}} = \frac{{{T_s}{T_E}}}{{{T_E} - {T_s}}}$ 
If ${\omega _s} = {\omega _E}$, T = $\infty$, i.e. satellite will appear stationary relative to earth. Such satellites are called geostationary satellites and will have a period of rotation as the period of rotation of earth i.e 24 Hrs.

questions.question: A cricket ball of mass 150 g has an initial velocity $\mathbf{\vec u}=(3 \mathbf{\hat i}+4 \mathbf{\hat j}) \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ and a final velocity $\mathbf{\vec v}=-(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$ after being hit. The change in momentum (final momentum-initial momentum) is (in kg ms^-1).
questions.question_raw: A cricket ball of mass 150 g has an initial velocity {tex}\mathbf{\vec u}=(3 \mathbf{\hat i}+4 \mathbf{\hat j}) \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} and a final velocity {tex}\mathbf{\vec v}=-(3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex} after being hit. The change in momentum (final momentum-initial momentum) is (in kg ms-1).
questions.explanation:
We know that change in momentum =final momentum - initial momentum 0 $\Delta \vec{p}=m_{1} \vec{v}-m_{2} \vec{u}$
now we have given mass 150 g i.e. m_1 = m_2 = m = 0.15 kg also $\vec{v}=-(3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}) \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$ and $\vec{u}=(3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath}) m s^{-1}$ so
$\Delta \vec{p}=m(\hat{v}-\hat{u})=0.15[-(3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath})-(3 \hat{\imath}+4 \hat{\jmath})]$
= 0.15[-3$\hat{\imath}$ - 4$\hat{\jmath}$ -3$\hat{\imath}$ - 4$\hat{\jmath}$]
= 0.15[-6$\hat{\imath}$ - 8$\hat{\jmath}$]
= -0.9$\hat{\imath}$ -1.2$\hat{\jmath}$
So $\Delta \vec{p}$ = -[0.9$\hat{\imath}$ + 1.2$\hat{\jmath}$]

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is false but R is true.
A is true but R is false.

correct_option_texts: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation:
Power = $\frac{W}{t}=\frac{\vec{F} \cdot \vec{s}}{t}$ = $\vec F {\cdot }\vec v$ = $ma \times(at)$ = ma^2t
$\therefore$ P $\propto$ t
Thus power is proportional to time.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): When the distance between two bodies is doubled and also a mass of each body is also doubled, the gravitational force between them remains the same.
Reason (R): According to Newton's law of gravitation, force is directly proportional to the mass of bodies and inversely proportional to the distance between them.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): When the distance between two bodies is doubled and also a mass of each body is also doubled, the gravitational force between them remains the same.
Reason (R): According to Newton's law of gravitation, force is directly proportional to the mass of bodies and inversely proportional to the distance between them.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

correct_option_texts: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation:
According to Newton's law of gravitation, F = $\frac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{r^{2}}$
When m_1, m_2 and r all are doubled, F = $\frac{G\left(2 m_{1}\right)\left(2 m_{2}\right)}{(2 r)^{2}}$
$\frac{G m_{1} m_{2}}{r^{2}}$, i.e. F remains the same.

questions.question: A ball starts from rest and accelerates at 0.500 m/s^2 while moving down an inclined plane 9.00 m long. When it reaches the bottom, the ball rolls up another plane, where, after moving 15.0 m, it comes to rest. How many seconds does it take to roll down the first plane?
questions.question_raw: A ball starts from rest and accelerates at 0.500 m/s2 while moving down an inclined plane 9.00 m long. When it reaches the bottom, the ball rolls up another plane, where, after moving 15.0 m, it comes to rest. How many seconds does it take to roll down the first plane?
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity is given by ,u = 0
Acceleration a = 0.500 m/s^​​​​​​2
Distance covered s = 9.0 m
Time taken t =?
We know
s = ut + ${1\over 2}at^2$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $ 9 = 0\times t+{1\over 2}\times (0.5)t^2$ 
$\Rightarrow$ $t^2= {9\times 2\over 0.5} = 36$ 
$\Rightarrow$ t = 6 s

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:
5 students were asked to measure the time period of a pendulum. They obtained the following result:
 Student

The teacher explained 4 different types of errors involved in their observations:
Absolute error: It is the difference of the arithmetic value of all the measured values and each of the measured value.
Mean absolute error: It is the arithmetic mean of value of absolute errors.
Fractional error: It is the ratio of the mean absolute error to the arithmetic mean of the measured values.
Percentage error: It is the fractional error multiplied by 100.

questions.question: One example where technology predates science is:
questions.question_raw: One example where technology predates science is:
questions.explanation:
Thomas Newcomen's atmospheric engine was the first commercial true steam engine using a piston and was used in 1712 for pumping in a mine. They became popular for mining and were in use by 1733, eventually, over two thousand of them were installed.
In1781 Scottish engineer James Watt patented a steam engine that produced continuous rotary motion. Watt's ten-horsepower engines enabled a wide range of manufacturing machinery to be powered. The engines could be sited anywhere that water and coal or wood fuel could be obtained.

questions.question: A motorcycle stunt rider rides off the edge of a cliff. Just at the edge his velocity is horizontal, with magnitude 9.0 m/s. Find the magnitude of the motorcycle’s position vector after 0.50s it leaves the edge of the cliff.
questions.question_raw: A motorcycle stunt rider rides off the edge of a cliff. Just at the edge his velocity is horizontal, with magnitude 9.0 m/s. Find the magnitude of the motorcycle’s position vector after 0.50s it leaves the edge of the cliff.
questions.explanation:
The motorcycle’s x- and y-coordinates at t=0.50 s are given by ;
x = v_ox t = $9.0 \times 0.50$ = 4.5 m
y = $-{1\over 2} gt^2$ = $- {1\over 2}\times 9.8\times (0.50)^2$= -1.2m
The negative value of y shows that the motorcycle is below its starting point.
The motorcycle’s distance from the origin is given by , r = $\sqrt {x^2+y^2}= \sqrt {(4.5)^2+(-1.2)^2}$ = 4.7 m

questions.question: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 27 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 45 km/ hr. What is the velocity of B with respect to A in m/s? Choose the positive direction of the x-axis to be from the south to north:
questions.question_raw: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 27 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 45 km/ hr. What is the velocity of B with respect to A in m/s? Choose the positive direction of the x-axis to be from the south to north:
questions.explanation:
The velocity of A is given by, v_A = +27 kmh^–1 = +7.5 ms^–1
The velocity of B is given by , v_B = -45 kmh^–1 = -12.5 ms^–1
The relative velocity of B with respect to A is given by, v_BA = v_B – v_A = −7.5−12.5 = –20 ms^–1
i.e. the train B appears to A to move with a speed of 20 ms^–1 from north to south.

questions.question:
A passenger arriving in a new town wishes to go from the station to a hotel located 10 km away on a straight road from the station. A dishonest cabman takes him along a circuitous path 23 km long and reaches the hotel in 28 min. What is
 the average speed of the taxi,
 the magnitude of average velocity?
questions.question_raw:
A passenger arriving in a new town wishes to go from the station to a hotel located 10 km away on a straight road from the station. A dishonest cabman takes him along a circuitous path 23 km long and reaches the hotel in 28 min. What is

  1. the average speed of the taxi,
  2. the magnitude of average velocity?
questions.explanation:
Total distance travelled = 23 km
 Total time taken = 28 min = $28\over 60$ h
 $\therefore$ The average speed of the taxi = $Total\ distance \ travelled \over Total\ time\ taken$ 
 = $ {{23 \over 1}\over {28\over 60}} $ = 49.3 km/h
 Distance between the hotel and the station = 10 km = Displacement of the car
 $\therefore$ Average velocity = ${{10 \over 1}\over {28\over 60}} $ = 21.4 km/h

questions.question: Null vector or a zero vector has a magnitude
questions.question_raw: Null vector or a zero vector has a magnitude
questions.explanation:
A null vector is a vector having a magnitude equal to zero. It is represented by $\vec 0$. A null vector has no direction or it may have any direction. Generally a null vector is either equal to resultant of two equal vectors acting in opposite directions or multiple vectors in different directions.
$\vec 0 = \vec A+(-\vec A)$

questions.question: The ratio of radii of gyration of a circular disc and a circular ring of the same radii and same mass about a tangential axis in the plane is
questions.question_raw: The ratio of radii of gyration of a circular disc and a circular ring of the same radii and same mass about a tangential axis in the plane is
questions.explanation:
By perpendicular axes theorem,$I_{\text {ring }}=I_{D}+M R^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{2} M R^{2}+M R^{2}=\frac{3}{2} M R^{2}$
$I_{\text {disc }}=I_{D}+M R^{2}$
$=\frac{1}{4} M R^{2}+M R^{2}=\frac{5}{4} M R^{2}$
$\frac{{{I_{{\text{disc }}}}}}{{{I_{{\text{ring }}}}}} = \frac{{\frac{5}{4}}}{{\frac{3}{2}}} = \frac{5}{6}$
or $\frac{M k_{\text {disc }}^{2}}{M k_{\text {ring }}^{2}}=\frac{5}{6}$
$\therefore \frac{k_{\text {disc }}}{k_{\text {ring }}}=\sqrt{5}: \sqrt{6}$

questions.question: A wheel has angular acceleration of 3.0 rad/ sec^2 and an initial angular speed of 2.00 rad/ sec. In a time of 2 sec, it has rotated through an angle (in radian) of

questions.explanation:
$\theta=a_{0} t+\frac{1}{2} \alpha t^{2}$
= 2 $\times$ 2 + $\frac 12\times$ 3.0 $\times$ 2^2 = 4 + 6 = 10

questions.question: Time period of an earth satellite very close to the surface of earth is given by
questions.question_raw: Time period of an earth satellite very close to the surface of earth is given by
questions.explanation:
It is the time taken by satellite to go once around the earth
$\therefore \mathrm{T}=\frac{\text { Circumference of the orbit }}{\text { orbitalvelocity }}$
$ \Rightarrow T = \frac{{2\pi r}}{\nu } = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{r}{{GM}}} $ ${\text{[As }}\nu {\text{ = }}\sqrt {\frac{{GM}}{r}{\text{ }}} ]$ 
Since Satellite is very close to the surface of earth, so here, we can take r = R (Radius of earth)
$ \Rightarrow T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{{R^3}}}{{GM}}} $
$ \Rightarrow T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{{R^3}}}{{g{R^2}}}}$ $[GM = g{R^2}]$
$ \Rightarrow T = 2\pi \sqrt {\frac{{{R}}}{{g}}}$

questions.question: The change in position or displacement ($\Delta {\rm{x}}$) divided by the time intervals ($\Delta {\rm{t}}$), in which the displacement occurs is known as
questions.question_raw: The change in position or displacement ({tex}\Delta {\rm{x}}{/tex}) divided by the time intervals ({tex}\Delta {\rm{t}}{/tex}), in which the displacement occurs is known as
questions.explanation:
Average velocity is the displacement of an object, divided by the time it took to cover that distance.
$V_{average}= {∆x\over ∆t}$
Displacement is the straight line distance between the starting point and ending point of an object's motion.
Velocity is referred to as a vector quantity because it has both magnitude and direction.

questions.question: The moment of the force, $\vec{F}=4 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}$ at (2, 0, -3), about the point (2, -2, -2), is given by
questions.question_raw: The moment of the force, {tex}\vec{F}=4 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}{/tex} at (2, 0, -3), about the point (2, -2, -2), is given by
questions.explanation:
$\vec{F}=4 \hat{i}+5 \hat{j}-6 \hat{k}$
$\vec{r}-\vec{r}_{0}=(2-2) \hat{i}+(0+2) \hat{j}+(-3+2) \hat{k}$
$=0 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}-\hat{k}$
$\vec{\tau}=\left(\vec{r}-\vec{r}_{0}\right) \times \vec{F}$
$=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 0 & 2 & -1 \\ 4 & 5 & -6 \end{array}\right|=-7 \hat{i}-4 \hat{j}-8 \hat k$

questions.question: Potential energy of a satellite having mass m and rotating at a height of 6.4 $\times$ 10^6 m from the earth centre is:
questions.question_raw: Potential energy of a satellite having mass m and rotating at a height of 6.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 m from the earth centre is:
questions.explanation:
Height h = 6.4 $\times$ 10^6 m is the radius R_e of the earth.
$\therefore \quad U=-\frac{G M m}{R_{e} \ + \ R_{e}}$
= $-\frac{g R_{r}^{2} m}{2 R_{e}}$ = -0.5 mgR_e

questions.question: The scientific method is
questions.question_raw: The scientific method is

options.text: a method for proposing new theories.
a prescribed method for investigating phenomena, acquiring new knowledge.
a body of techniques for investigating phenomena, acquiring new knowledge.
a procedure for proposing new hypothesis.

correct_option_texts: a body of techniques for investigating phenomena, acquiring new knowledge.
questions.explanation: The scientific method is a body of techniques for investigating phenomena, acquiring new knowledge, or correcting and integrating previous knowledge. To be termed scientific, a method of inquiry is commonly based on empirical or measurable evidence subject to specific principles of reasoning.

questions.question: A stone tied to the end of a string 80 cm long is whirled in a horizontal circle with a constant speed. If the stone makes 14 revolutions in 25 s, what is the magnitude and direction of acceleration of the stone?
questions.question_raw: A stone tied to the end of a string 80 cm long is whirled in a horizontal circle with a constant speed. If the stone makes 14 revolutions in 25 s, what is the magnitude and direction of acceleration of the stone?

options.text: 11.9 m ${{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$ , along the radius at every point away from the center
9.9 m ${{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$ , along the radius at every point away from the center
9.9 m ${{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$, along the radius at every point towards the center
10.2 m ${{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$ , along the radius at every point towards the center

correct_option_texts: 9.9 m ${{\rm{s}}^{ - {\rm{2}}}}$, along the radius at every point towards the center
questions.explanation:
Length of the string, $l$ = 80 cm = 0.8 m
Number of revolutions = 14
Time taken = 25 s
Frequency, $\nu ={ Number \ of\ revolutions \over Time\ taken} = {14 \over 25} Hz$
Angular frequency, $\omega = 2\pi\nu$ = $2 ×{ 22\over 7}× {14\over25} = {88 \over 25} \ rad \ s^{-1}$
Centripetal acceleration, a_c = ω^2r = $ ({88\over 25})^2 × 0.8$ = 9.9 ms^-2
The direction of centripetal acceleration is always directed along the string, towards the centre, at all points.

questions.explanation:
W = Fscos$\theta$ = mghcos0^o
W = 2 $\times$ 10^ − 3 $\times$ 10 $\times$ 1000 $\times$ 1 = 20J

questions.question: A satellite in a force-free space sweeps stationary interplanetary dust at the rate $\frac{d M}{d t}=\beta v$. The acceleration of the satellite is
questions.question_raw: A satellite in a force-free space sweeps stationary interplanetary dust at the rate {tex}\frac{d M}{d t}=\beta v{/tex}. The acceleration of the satellite is
questions.explanation:
Thrust, $F=-v \frac{d M}{d t}=-v \cdot \beta v=-\beta v^{2}$
Acceleration, $a=\frac{F}{M}=-\frac{\beta v^{2}}{M}$

questions.question: In which of the following cases is the work done positively?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following cases is the work done positively?

options.text: Work is done by friction on a body sliding down an inclined plane.
Work is done by an applied force on a body moving on a rough horizontal plane with uniform velocity.
Work is done by gravitational force while a man in lifts a bucket out of a well by means of a rope tied to the bucket.
Work is done by the resistive force of air on a vibrating pendulum in bringing it to rest.

correct_option_texts: Work is done by an applied force on a body moving on a rough horizontal plane with uniform velocity.
questions.explanation:
When a body is moving on a rough horizontal surface then there will be 2 forces acting on the body 
 Applied force ( in the direction of motion)
 friction ( opposite to the direction of motion)
As applied force is in the same direction as displacement so work is done will be positive.

questions.question: When does a disc rotate with uniform angular velocity, which of the following is not true?
questions.question_raw: When does a disc rotate with uniform angular velocity, which of the following is not true?

options.text: The sense of rotation remains same.
The orientation of the axis of rotation remains same.
The angular acceleration is non-zero and remains same
The speed of rotation is non-zero and remains same.

correct_option_texts: The angular acceleration is non-zero and remains same
questions.explanation:
As disc is rotating with uniform angular velocity, $\omega$ is constant.
Therefore
Angular acceleration = $\alpha$
= $\frac{\mathrm{d} w}{\mathrm{dt}}$ (Differentiation of constant is 0)
= 0
So, its angular acceleration is zero.

questions.question: Consider a spacecraft in an elliptical orbit around the earth. At the low point, or perigee, of its orbit, it is 400 km above the earth’s surface; at the high point, or apogee, it is 4000 km above the earth’s surface. Using conservation of angular momentum, find the ratio of the spacecraft’s speed at perigee to its speed at apogee.
questions.question_raw: Consider a spacecraft in an elliptical orbit around the earth. At the low point, or perigee, of its orbit, it is 400 km above the earth’s surface; at the high point, or apogee, it is 4000 km above the earth’s surface. Using conservation of angular momentum, find the ratio of the spacecraft’s speed at perigee to its speed at apogee.
questions.explanation:
At Apogee(High Point):
R_a = R_E + h_a = 6.38 $\times$ 10^6m + 4000 $\times$ 10^3m
= 6.38 $\times$ 10^6m + 4 $\times$ 10^6m 
= 10.38 $\times$ 10^6m
At Perigee(Low Point):
R_p = R_E + h_p
= 6.38 $\times$ 10^6m + 400 $\times$ 10^3m
= 6.38 $\times$ 10^6m + 0.4 $\times$ 10^6m 
= 6.78 $\times$ 10^6 m
a = $\frac{R_{p}+R_{a}}{2}$ = 8.58 $\times$ 10^6m
Angular momentum, L is conserved because no torque acts on on the system i.e.
L = mv_aR_a = mv_pR_p
Thus, $\frac{v_{p}}{v_{a}}=\frac{R_{a}}{R_{p}}=\frac{10.38 \times 10^{6}}{6.78 \times 10^{6}}$ = 1.531

questions.question: A ball starts from rest and accelerates at 0.500 m/s^2 while moving down an inclined plane 9.00 m long. When it reaches the bottom, the ball rolls up another plane, where, after moving 15.0 m, it comes to rest. What is the ball’s speed in m/s 8.00 m along the second plane?
questions.question_raw: A ball starts from rest and accelerates at 0.500 m/s2 while moving down an inclined plane 9.00 m long. When it reaches the bottom, the ball rolls up another plane, where, after moving 15.0 m, it comes to rest. What is the ball’s speed in m/s 8.00 m along the second plane?
questions.explanation:
For First Plane
Initial velocity u = 0
Acceleration a = 0.500 m/s^​​​​​​2
Distance covered s = 9.0 m
Final velocity v =?
We know
v^2 - u^2 = 2as
$\Rightarrow$ v^2 - 0 = 2 $\times$ 0.500 $\times$ 9.0
$\Rightarrow$ v^2 = 9
$\Rightarrow$ v = 3 m/s
This is initial velocity for second plane
For second plane 
Initial velocity u = 3 m/s
Final velocity v = 0 (as it stop)
Distance covered s = 15 m
Acceleration a = ?
$\Rightarrow$ 0 - 9 = 2 $\times$ a $\times$ 15
$\Rightarrow$ a = - 9/30 = - 0.3 m/s^2
Now velocity of the ball at distance = 8.0 m, is given by
$\Rightarrow$ v^2 - 9 = 2 $\times$ (- 0.3) $\times$ 8.0
$\Rightarrow$ v^2 = -4.8 + 9 = 4.2
$\Rightarrow$ v = 2.05 m/s

questions.question: A 50.0-kg marathon runner runs up the stairs to the top of a 443-m-tall Tower. To lift herself to the top in 15.0 minutes, what must be her average power output?
questions.question_raw: A 50.0-kg marathon runner runs up the stairs to the top of a 443-m-tall Tower. To lift herself to the top in 15.0 minutes, what must be her average power output?
questions.explanation:
P = $ {W \over t}$
W = mgh = 50 $\times$ 9.8 $\times$ 443
t = 15 $\times$ 60sec
P = ${{50 \times 9.8 \times 443} \over {15 \times 60}} $ = 241 W.

questions.question_raw: If a cycle wheel of radius 4 m completes one revolution in two seconds, then acceleration of the cycle is
questions.explanation:
$\omega=\frac{2 \pi}{T}=\frac{2 \pi}{2 \mathrm{~s}}=\pi $ rad s^-1
a = $r \omega^{2}$ = 4m ($\pi$ rads^-1)^2
= $4\pi^2$ m/s^2

options.text: Moment of inertia and angular velocity

questions.explanation:

if r is the perpendicular to P (in case of circular motion)
L = mvr

questions.question: What is not conserved in the case of celestial bodies revolving around sun?

questions.explanation: K.E. changes due to the change in the speed of celestial body around the sun.

questions.question: You are asked to design spring bumpers for the walls of a parking garage. A freely rolling 1200-kg car moving at 0.65 m/s is to compress the spring no more than 0.090 m before stopping. What should be the force constant of the spring? Assume that the spring has negligible mass.
questions.question_raw: You are asked to design spring bumpers for the walls of a parking garage. A freely rolling 1200-kg car moving at 0.65 m/s is to compress the spring no more than 0.090 m before stopping. What should be the force constant of the spring? Assume that the spring has negligible mass.
questions.explanation:
The kinetic energy of the car will be converted into the potential energy of spring.
${1 \over 2}k{x^2} = {1 \over 2}m{v^2}$
$k = {{m{v^2}} \over {{x^2}}}$
m = 1200Kg
v = 0.65m/s
x = 0.09m
$k = {{1200 \times 0.65 \times 0.65} \over {0.09 \times 0.09}} $ = 6.25 $\times$ 10^4N/m

questions.question: A truck is coming towards you from a distance of 1000 m on a straight road. At time t=0 it starts from rest. At time t=10 seconds its velocity is 20m/s. What is the average acceleration in m/ ${\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}$?
questions.question_raw: A truck is coming towards you from a distance of 1000 m on a straight road. At time t=0 it starts from rest. At time t=10 seconds its velocity is 20m/s. What is the average acceleration in m/ {tex}{\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex}?
questions.explanation:
Average acceleration is the change in velocity divided by an elapsed time.
Initial velocity, u = 0 m/s
Final velocity, v = 20 m/s
Time elapsed = 10-0 = 10 s
Average acceleration, $a_{avg} = {change \ in \ veloctiy \over time \ elapsed}$ $= {20-0\over 10}= 2\ m/s^2$

questions.question: A body of mass 2 kg is placed on a rough horizontal plane. The coefficient of friction between body and plane is 0.2. Then
questions.question_raw: A body of mass 2 kg is placed on a rough horizontal plane. The coefficient of friction between body and plane is 0.2. Then

options.text: body will move in forward direction if F = 5 N
body will move in backward direction with acceleration 0.5 m/s^2, if force F = 3 N
if F = 3 N, then body will be in rest condition
body will move in the forward direction if F = 5 N and if F = 3 N, then the body will be in rest condition

correct_option_texts: body will move in the forward direction if F = 5 N and if F = 3 N, then the body will be in rest condition
questions.explanation:
Limiting friction,
f = $\mu$R = $\mu$mg
= 0.2 $\times$ 2 $\times$ 9.8 = 3.92 N
because F > 3.92 N and body will move in forward direction.
When F = 3N, the body will remain at rest.

questions.question: If the radii of circular paths of two particles of same masses are in the ratio of 6 : 8, then to have a constant centripetal force, their velocities should be in a ratio of
questions.question_raw: If the radii of circular paths of two particles of same masses are in the ratio of 6 : 8, then to have a constant centripetal force, their velocities should be in a ratio of
questions.explanation:
Centripetal force, $F=\frac{m v^{2}}{r}$
$v=\sqrt{\frac{F r}{m}}$
For same F and m, $v \propto \sqrt{r}$
$\therefore \frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}}=\sqrt{\frac{6}{8}}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$

questions.question: The angular velocity of a body changes from 1 rev/sec to 25 rev/sec. without applying any external torque. The ratio of the radii of gyration in the two cases is:
questions.question_raw: The angular velocity of a body changes from 1 rev/sec to 25 rev/sec. without applying any external torque. The ratio of the radii of gyration in the two cases is:
questions.explanation:
I_1$\omega$_1 = I_2$\omega$_2
$\frac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}=\frac{\omega_{2}}{\omega_{1}}$ 
$\omega$_1 = 1rev/s
$\omega$_2 = 25rev/s
if the radius of gyration is k_1 and k_2 then 
$\frac{M k_{1}^{2}}{M k_{2}^{2}}=\frac{\omega_{2}}{\omega_{1}}$
$\frac{k_{1}}{k_{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{\omega_{2}}{\omega_{1}}}=\sqrt{\frac{25}{1}}=\frac{5}{1}$
k_1:k_2 = 5:1
