questions.question_raw: If the position- time graph is a straight line then
questions.explanation: The principle is that the slope of the line on a position-time graph reveals useful information about the velocity of the object. If the velocity is constant, then the slope is constant (i.e., a straight line).

questions.question: An aircraft executes a horizontal loop of radius 1.00 km with a steady speed of 900 km/h. Compare its centripetal acceleration with the acceleration due to gravity.
questions.question_raw: An aircraft executes a horizontal loop of radius 1.00 km with a steady speed of 900 km/h. Compare its centripetal acceleration with the acceleration due to gravity.
questions.explanation:
Radius of the loop, r = 1 km = 1000 m
Speed of the aircraft, v = 900 km/h
= $900\times {5\over 18}= 250\ m/s$
Centripetal acceleration, $a_c={v^2\over r}$ = ${(250)^2\over 1000}={62500\over 1000}=62.5\ m/s^2$
Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.8 m/s​​​​​^​​​​​​2
${a_c\over g}={62.5\over 9.8}$ = 6.4 g

questions.question: A bob of mass 0.1 kg hung from the ceiling of a room by a string 2 m long is set into oscillation. The speed of the bob at its mean position is 1 m s^−1. What is the trajectory of the bob if the string is cut when the bob is at one of its extreme positions.
questions.question_raw: A bob of mass 0.1 kg hung from the ceiling of a room by a string 2 m long is set into oscillation. The speed of the bob at its mean position is 1 m s−1. What is the trajectory of the bob if the string is cut when the bob is at one of its extreme positions.

options.text: bob will go downwards at an angle
bob will go down in a parabolic path

questions.explanation: At the extreme position of the oscillation, the speed of the bob is zero. So the bob is momentarily at rest. If the string is cut, the bob will fall vertically downwards.

questions.question: Reductionism is:
questions.question_raw: Reductionism is:

options.text: reducing all phenomena to logical consequences of Newton’s laws
reducing all phenomena to logical consequences of Einstein’s laws.
deriving the properties of a bigger, more complex, system from first principles.
deriving the properties of a bigger, more complex, system from the properties and interactions of its constituent simpler parts.

correct_option_texts: deriving the properties of a bigger, more complex, system from the properties and interactions of its constituent simpler parts.
questions.explanation: Reductionism is breaking down of a complex system into simple constituent systems so that laws of physics can be applied to these systems and we can understand the working of the complex system.

questions.question: For the case of uniformly accelerated motion in two dimensions it is possible to treat it as
questions.question_raw: For the case of uniformly accelerated motion in two dimensions it is possible to treat it as

options.text: Two separate simultaneous one-dimensional motions with constant acceleration along two perpendicular directions.
Two separate simultaneous one-dimensional motions with constant acceleration along a single direction.
Two separate simultaneous one-dimensional motions with constant acceleration along one direction and a non constant acceleration along perpendicular direction.
Two separate simultaneous one-dimensional motions with varying acceleration along one direction and a non constant acceleration along perpendicular direction

correct_option_texts: Two separate simultaneous one-dimensional motions with constant acceleration along two perpendicular directions.
questions.explanation:
Consider a particle moving on x-y plane along a curved pat at time t, as shown in figure given below. Its displacement from origin is measured by vector $ \vec{r} $ its velocity is indicated by vector $​​\vec {v}$ (tangent to the path of the particle) and acceleration $\vec{a} $ as shown in the figure.
A Particle Moving in a xy Plane The vectors $ \vec{r}, \vec{v} \ and \ \vec{a}$ are interrelated and can be expressed in terms of their components, using unit vector notation as,
$\vec r= x\hat i+ y\hat j$ 
$v = {d\vec r\over dt}= {dx\over dt}\hat i + {dy\over dt}\hat j$ 
$= v_x\hat i + v_y\hat j$ 
And $\vec a= {dv\over dt}= {dv_x\over dt}\hat i+ {dv_y\over dt}\hat j$ 
$\vec a = a_x\hat i + a_y\hat j$ 
From the above relations we can say that two-dimensional motion is nothing but superimpose of two one dimension motions.
This is for the reason that in any two-dimensional motion, all the parameters can be resolved in two mutually perpendicular directions.

questions.question: If pendulum bob on a 2 m string is displaced 60^o from the vertical and then released, what is the speed of the bob as it passes through the lowest point in its path?
questions.question_raw: If pendulum bob on a 2 m string is displaced 60o from the vertical and then released, what is the speed of the bob as it passes through the lowest point in its path?
questions.explanation:
v = $\sqrt{2 g l(1-\cos \theta)}$
$=\sqrt{2 \times 9.8 \times 2\left(1-\cos 60^{\circ}\right)}\frac{m}{s}$
$=\sqrt{2 \times 9.8}\frac{m}{s}$

questions.question: A man is standing on an international space station, which is orbiting earth at an altitude 520 km with a constant speed 7.6 km/s. If the man's weight is 50 kg, his acceleration is:
questions.question_raw: A man is standing on an international space station, which is orbiting earth at an altitude 520 km with a constant speed 7.6 km/s. If the man's weight is 50 kg, his acceleration is:
questions.explanation:
A man of mass 50 kg is standing on an international space station.
Then, distance of station from centre of earth r = R + h = 6400 + 520 [$\because$ radius of earth R = 6400 km] = 6920 km
Now, centripetal acceleration a = $\frac{v^{2}}{r} =\frac{\left(7.6 \ \times \ 10^{3}\right)^{2}}{6920 \ \times \ 10^{3}}$ = 8.4 km/s^2

questions.question: Geostationary Satellites:
questions.question_raw: Geostationary Satellites:

options.text: are not useful for communication applications
need more powerful rockets to put them into larger distance orbits
need more powerful rockets to put them into smaller distance orbits
needless powerful rockets to put them into larger distance orbits

correct_option_texts: need more powerful rockets to put them into larger distance orbits
questions.explanation: The orbital velocity of the satellite depends on its altitude above Earth. The nearer Earth, the faster the required orbital velocity. At an altitude of 200 kilometers, the required orbital velocity is just over 27,400 kph. To maintain an orbit that is 35,786 km above Earth, the satellite must orbit at a speed of about 11,300 kmph. That orbital speed and distance permit the satellite to make one revolution in 24 hours. To achieve his speed, Judging from the mass of fuel needed, it stands to reason that you'd use a more powerful launch vehicle with a bi-propellant upper stage to get the satellite to GEO.

questions.question: A shell of mass 0.020 kg is fired by a gun of mass 100 kg. If the muzzle speed of the shell is 80 ms^-1, what is the recoil speed of the gun?
questions.question_raw: A shell of mass 0.020 kg is fired by a gun of mass 100 kg. If the muzzle speed of the shell is 80 ms-1, what is the recoil speed of the gun?
questions.explanation:
Mass of shell, m = 0.02kg 
Mass of gun, M = 100kg
Speed of shell, v = 80ms^−1
Let V be the recoil speed of the gun. According to the law of conservation of momentum,
Initial momentum = Final momentum
0 = mv + MV
V = $-\frac{mv}{M}=-\frac{0.02\times80}{100}$
 recoil speed of the gun is ,V = −0.016ms^−1 = −1.6cms^−1
A negative sign indicates that the gun moves backward as the bullet moves forward.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): The scalar product of two vectors will be zero if angle between them is 180^o.
Reason (R): The scalar product of two vectors $\vec{A}$ and $\vec{B}$ is given by $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}$ = AB cos $\theta$.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): The scalar product of two vectors will be zero if angle between them is 180o.
Reason (R): The scalar product of two vectors {tex}\vec{A}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{B}{/tex} is given by {tex}\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}{/tex} = AB cos {tex}\theta{/tex}.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
A is false but R is true.

correct_option_texts: A is false but R is true.
questions.explanation: If angle between two vectors is 180^o, then their scalar product $\vec{A} \cdot \vec{B}$ = AB cos 180^o = -AB. It will be zero, if angle between vector is 90^o.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Current density is a scalar quantity.
Reason (R): Current density is the electric current passing through a unit area.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): Current density is a scalar quantity.
Reason (R): Current density is the electric current passing through a unit area.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

correct_option_texts: A is false but R is true.
questions.explanation:
Current density is a vector quantity. Hence, the assertion is false.
Current density = $\frac iA = \frac{n e A \overrightarrow{v_{d}}}{A}=n e A \overrightarrow{v_{d}}$
Hence the reason is true.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): When a gas is allowed to expand, work done by the gas is positive.
Reason (R): Force due to gaseous pressure and displacement (of the piston) are in the same direction.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): When a gas is allowed to expand, work done by the gas is positive.
Reason (R): Force due to gaseous pressure and displacement (of the piston) are in the same direction.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
A is true but R is false.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

correct_option_texts: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation:
Since the gaseous pressure and the displacement (of the piston) are in the same direction. Therefore $\theta=0^{\circ}$
$\therefore $ Work done = Fscos$\theta$ = Fs = Positive
Thus during expansion work done by gas is positive.

questions.question:

Reason (R): The distance between two particles remains constant in a rigid body.
questions.question_raw:

Reason (R): The distance between two particles remains constant in a rigid body.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

correct_option_texts: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): A person sitting in an artificial satellite revolving around the earth feels weightless.
Reason (R): There is no gravitational force on the satellite.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): A person sitting in an artificial satellite revolving around the earth feels weightless.
Reason (R): There is no gravitational force on the satellite.

options.text: Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.

correct_option_texts: A is true but R is false.
questions.explanation: A person feels his weight only when the surface on which he is standing exerts a reactionary force on him. Because the acceleration of the person and that of the satellite revolving around the earth are equal (=g), hence the acceleration of the person with respect to the satellite is zero. Therefore person feels weightless on a satellite, although the gravitational force is acting on a satellite.

questions.question: In one dimensional motion, instantaneous speed v satisfies 0 $\le$ v < v_0.
questions.question_raw: In one dimensional motion, instantaneous speed v satisfies 0 {tex}\le{/tex} v < v0.

options.text: The displacement x in time T satisfies -v_oT < x < v_oT.
The displacement in time T must always take non-negative values.
The motion has no turning points.
The acceleration is always a non-negative number.

correct_option_texts: The displacement x in time T satisfies -v_oT < x < v_oT.
questions.explanation:
For maximum and minimum displacement, we have the magnitude and direction of maximum and minimum velocity.
As maximum velocity in positive direction is v_0.
maximum velocity in opposite direction(Or minimum velocity) is also v_o.
So, we can say;
Maximum displacement in one direction = v_oT
Maximum displacement in opposite direction = -v_oT
Hence, -v_oT < x < v_oT is the correct statement.

questions.question: A man fires a bullet of mass 200 g at a speed of 5 m/s. The gun is of one kg mass. By what velocity the gun rebounds backwards?
questions.question_raw: A man fires a bullet of mass 200 g at a speed of 5 m/s. The gun is of one kg mass. By what velocity the gun rebounds backwards?
questions.explanation:
By conservation of momentum,
mv + MV = 0
$\frac{200}{1000} \times 5+1 \times V=0$ or V = -1 m/s

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:
Vernier callipers is used to measure the thickness of thin plates, diameters of small cylinders etc.
It has two scales - one main scale and a vernier scale, which slides along the main scale. The main scale and vernier scale are divided into small divisions though of different magnitudes.
The difference in the magnitude of one main scale division (M.S.D.) and one vernier scale division (V.S.D.) is called the least count of the instrument, as it is the smallest distance that can be measured using the instrument. It is also known as vernier constant.
Least count may also be defined as:
L.C. = $\frac{\text{Value of one main scale division}}{\text{Number of divisions on vernier scale}}$
When the jaws of vernier callipers touch each other, the zero of the vernier should coincide with the zero of the main scale. If it is not so, then the instrument is said to have zero error.
Zero error may be positive or negative, depending upon whether the zero of vernier scale lies to the right or to the left of the zero of the main scale.
The least count of the instrument when 1 MSD = 0.1 cm and number of vernier scale divisions = 10 is

The difference in the magnitude of one main scale division (M.S.D.) and one vernier scale division (V.S.D.) is called the least count of the instrument, as it is the smallest distance that can be measured using the instrument. It is also known as vernier constant.
Least count may also be defined as:
L.C. = {tex}\frac{\text{Value of one main scale division}}{\text{Number of divisions on vernier scale}}{/tex}
When the jaws of vernier callipers touch each other, the zero of the vernier should coincide with the zero of the main scale. If it is not so, then the instrument is said to have zero error.
Zero error may be positive or negative, depending upon whether the zero of vernier scale lies to the right or to the left of the zero of the main scale.


Which of the following figures has no zero error?
questions.explanation: When the jaws of vernier callipers touch each other, the zero of the vernier should coincide with the zero of the main scale. In that case it is said to have no zero error.

questions.question: Model in physics refers to
questions.question_raw: Model in physics refers to

options.text: an explanation of a particular phenomenon.

correct_option_texts: an explanation of a particular phenomenon.
questions.explanation: A model is a representation of something that is often too difficult (or impossible) to display directly. While a model's design is justified using experimental information, it is only accurate under limited situations. An example is the commonly used "planetary model" of the atom, in which electrons are pictured as orbiting the nucleus, analogous to the way planets orbit the Sun.

questions.question: Which of the following physical quantity has the dimension of [ML^2T^-3]?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following physical quantity has the dimension of [ML2T-3]?

questions.question: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of ground with respect to B in m/s? Choose the positive direction of the x-axis to be from the south to the north.
questions.question_raw: Two parallel rail tracks run north-south. Train A moves north with a speed of 54 km/ hr, and train B moves south with a speed of 90 km/ hr. What is the velocity of ground with respect to B in m/s? Choose the positive direction of the x-axis to be from the south to the north.
questions.explanation:
Choose the positive direction of the x-axis to be from the south to the north. 
Then, v_A = +54 km h^–1= +15 ms^–1
v_B= –90 km h^–1= –25 ms^–1
The relative velocity of B with respect to A = v_B – v_A = -25 − (15) = -40 ms^–1, i.e. the train B appears to A to move with a speed of 40 ms^–1 from north to south.
The relative velocity of ground with respect to B = 0 – v_B = 0 − (−25) = 25ms^–1.

questions.question: If $\vec r$ = $x\hat i + y\hat j$ is the position vector of a particle then the instantaneous acceleration is given by
questions.question_raw: If {tex}\vec r{/tex} = {tex}x\hat i + y\hat j{/tex} is the position vector of a particle then the instantaneous acceleration is given by
questions.explanation:
The exact measure of the city of a body at an instant or over a very-very short time interval ∆t is known as instantaneous velocity. Mathematically instantaneous velocity of an object is the limiting value of average velocity as ∆t tends towards 0. Instantaneous velocity = $\displaystyle\lim_{\Delta t\to 0}\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} =\frac{dx}{ dt}$
So for given position vector, $\vec v$ = $\frac{dr}{dt}$
Instantaneous acceleration, $\vec a=$ $\frac{dv}{dt}= \frac{d^2r}{dt^2}$

$[h]=\frac{\text { Energy }}{\text { Frequency }}$
$=\frac{\left[\mathrm{ML}^{2} \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{T}^{-1}\right]}$ = [ML^2T^-1]

questions.question: Torque $\tau $ and angular momentum L are related by:
questions.question_raw: Torque {tex}\tau {/tex} and angular momentum L are related by:
questions.explanation:
$\vec L = \vec r \times \vec p$ 
on differentiation with respect to time 
$\frac{d \vec{L}}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}(\vec{r} \times \vec{p})$ 
Applying the product rule for differentiation to the right-hand side,
$\frac{d}{d t}(\vec{r} \times \vec{p})=\left(\frac{d \vec{r}}{d t} \times \vec{p}\right)+\left(\vec{r} \times \frac{d \vec{p}}{d t}\right)$
$\frac{d \vec{r}}{d t}=\vec{v}, \vec{p}=m \vec{v}$
$\left(\frac{d \vec{r}}{d t} \times \vec{p}\right)=\vec{v} \times m \vec{v}=0$
$\frac{d \vec{p}}{d t}=\vec{F}$
$\left(\vec{r} \times \frac{d \vec{p}}{d t}\right)=\vec{r} \times \vec{F}=\vec{\tau}$
Hence $\vec{\tau}=\frac{d \vec{L}}{d t}$

questions.question: Let r_i be the position vector of the i^th particle having mass m_i and R be the position vector of the centre of mass. The formula for R is:
questions.question_raw: Let ri be the position vector of the ith particle having mass mi and R be the position vector of the centre of mass. The formula for R is:
questions.explanation:
Let us consider a system consisting of N – particles of masses m_1, m_2,….. m_N having position vectors ${\vec r_1},{\vec r_2}$,...... $ {\vec r_N} $ respectively.
The total mass M of the system is given by 
M = m­_1 +m_2 +…….+ m_N 
We can generalize the definition of the position of centre of mass consisting of N particles, hence the position vector of centre of mass is given below:-
$\vec{R}=\frac{m_1 \vec{r}_1+m_2 \vec{r}_2+\ldots \ldots \ldots+m_N \vec{r}_N}{m_1+m_2+\ldots \ldots+m_N}=\frac{\sum_\limits{i=1}^N m_i \vec{r}_i}{\sum_\limits{i=1}^N m_i}=\frac{\sum_\limits{i=1}^N m_i \vec{r}_i}{M}$

questions.question: The component of a vector $\vec r$ along X-axis will have maximum value if

questions.explanation:
Let $\vec r$ makes an angle θ with the positive x-axis component of r along X-axis.
$ r_{x}=|r| \cos \theta$
$\left(r_{x}\right)_{\text {maximum }}=|r|(\cos \theta)_{\text {maximum }}$
$r_{x}=|r| \cos \theta$
$=|r| \cos 0^{\circ}=|r|\left(\therefore \cos \theta \text { is maximum } \mathrm{} \mathrm{} \text {of } \theta=0^{\circ}\right)$
$As~ \theta=0^{\circ}$
$\vec r$ is along positive x-axis

questions.question: Instantaneous acceleration is defined as
questions.question_raw: Instantaneous acceleration is defined as
questions.explanation:
Instantaneous acceleration is measured over a "short" time interval. The word short in this context means infinitely small or infinitesimal — having no duration or extent whatsoever. It's a mathematical ideal that can can only be realized as a limit. The limit of a rate as the denominator approaches zero is called a derivative.
Instantaneous acceleration is then the limit of average acceleration as the time interval approaches zero — or alternatively, acceleration is the derivative of velocity.
$a=\lim _\limits{\mathrm{t} \rightarrow 0} \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}=\frac{d v}{d t}$

questions.question: A fighter plane flying horizontally at an altitude of 1.5 km with speed 720 km/h passes directly overhead an anti-aircraft gun. At what angle from the vertical should the gun be fired for the shell with muzzle speed 600 ms^−1 to hit the plane? At what minimum altitude should the pilot fly the plane to avoid being hit? (Take g = 10 ms^−2).
questions.question_raw: A fighter plane flying horizontally at an altitude of 1.5 km with speed 720 km/h passes directly overhead an anti-aircraft gun. At what angle from the vertical should the gun be fired for the shell with muzzle speed 600 ms−1 to hit the plane? At what minimum altitude should the pilot fly the plane to avoid being hit? (Take g = 10 ms−2).

options.text: at an angle of 20.5^o with the vertical, 12 km
at an angle of 21.5^o with the vertical, 15 km
at an angle of 19.5^o with the vertical, 16 km
at an angle of 23.5^o with the vertical, 17 km

correct_option_texts: at an angle of 19.5^o with the vertical, 16 km
questions.explanation:
Height of the fighter plane = 1.5 km = 1500 m
Speed of the fighter plane, v = 720 km/h = 200 m/s
Let $\theta$ be the angle with the vertical so that the shell hits the plane. The situation is shown in the given figure.
Muzzle velocity of the gun, u = 600 m/s
Time taken by the shell to hit the plane = t
Horizontal distance travelled by the shell = u_xt
Distance travelled by the plane = vt
The shell hits the plane. Hence, these two distances must be equal.
u_xt = vt
u Sin $\theta$ = v
Sin $\theta$ = ${v \over u} = {200 \over 600} $ = 0.33
$\theta$ = Sin^-1(0.33) = 19.5^0
In order to avoid being hit by the shell, the pilot must fly the plane at an altitude (H) higher than the maximum height achieved by the shell.
$\therefore$ H = ${u^2sin^2 (90 – θ) \over 2g} ={ (600)^2 cos^2 θ \over 2g}$
= $360000 \times cos^2 (19.5)\over 2 \times 10$
= 16006.482 m
$\approx$ 16 km

questions.question: A cricketer catches a ball of mass 150 g in 0.1 s moving with speed 20 m/s. Then he experiences a force of
questions.question_raw: A cricketer catches a ball of mass 150 g in 0.1 s moving with speed 20 m/s. Then he experiences a force of
questions.explanation:
$F=m\left(\frac{v-u}{t}\right)=\frac{150}{1000}\left(\frac{0-20}{0.1}\right)$ = -30 N
Force experienced by the cricketer = 30 N

questions.question: The scalar product of two vectors $\overrightarrow A$ and $\overrightarrow B$ in terms of the projections of the vectors on the x, y and z axis is
questions.question_raw: The scalar product of two vectors {tex}\overrightarrow A{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow B{/tex} in terms of the projections of the vectors on the x, y and z axis is

$\overrightarrow A$ = A_x i + A_y j+ A_zk
$\overrightarrow B$ = B_x i + B_y j+ B_zk
$\overrightarrow A$.$\overrightarrow B$ = (A_x i + A_y j+ A_zk). (B_x i+ B_y j+ B_zk) 
$\overrightarrow A$.$\overrightarrow B$ = A_xB_x + A_yB_y + A_zB_z , this is required result.

questions.question: The radius of gyration of a rod of mass 100 gm and length 100 cm about an axis passing through its edge and perpendicular to its length is given by:
questions.question_raw: The radius of gyration of a rod of mass 100 gm and length 100 cm about an axis passing through its edge and perpendicular to its length is given by:
questions.explanation:
Moment of inertia of the rod about an axis passing through its centre of gravity and perpendicular to its length
$I = \frac{M{l^2}} {{3}}$ 
Moment of inertia of rod in terms of the radius of gyration 
I = Mk^2
M = 100 gm
l = 100 cm
Mk^2 = $\frac{Ml^2}{3}$
$k=\sqrt{\frac{l^{2}}{3}}=\sqrt{\frac{100 \times 100}{3}}$
$k=\frac{100}{\sqrt{3}} \mathrm{cm}$

questions.explanation: Vector addition is commutative, just like addition of real numbers. If you start from a point say P, you end up at the same spot no matter which displacement (a or b) you take first. The head-to-tail rule yields vector c for both a + b and b + a.

questions.question: Law of conservation of mass has been changed by Einstein to:
questions.question_raw: Law of conservation of mass has been changed by Einstein to:

options.text: law of conservation of angular momentum.
law of conservation of mass and energy together.

correct_option_texts: law of conservation of mass and energy together.
questions.explanation:
German-born physicist Albert Einstein’s theory of special relativity that expresses the fact that mass and energy are the same physical entity and can be changed into each other.
He stated, E = mc^​​​​​2
In the equation, the increased relativistic mass (m) of body times the speed of light squared (c^​​​​​​2) is equal to the kinetic energy (E) of that body.

questions.question: The unit of a in van der Waal's gas equation is:
questions.question_raw: The unit of a in van der Waal's gas equation is:
questions.explanation:
Van der Wall's equation is
$\left(P+\frac{a}{V^{2}}\right)(V-b)=R T$
$\therefore$ [P] = $\left[\frac{a}{V^{2}}\right]$ or [a] = [P][V^2]
Unit of a = atm L^2 per mole.

questions.question: In which of the following examples of motion, can the body be considered approximately a point object?
questions.question_raw: In which of the following examples of motion, can the body be considered approximately a point object?

options.text: a spinning cricket ball that turns sharply on hitting the ground
a tumbling beaker that has slipped off the edge of a table
a railway carriage moving without jerks
a spinning top moving on a curved path

correct_option_texts: a railway carriage moving without jerks
questions.explanation:
Point object is used in kinematics branch of physics. It is an object whose dimensions are ignored or neglected while considering its motion.
The size of a carriage is very small as compared to the distance between two stations. Thus, the carriage can be treated as a point sized object.

questions.question: The Sun converts an enormous amount of matter to energy. Each second, 4.19 ${\times }$ 10^9 kg-approximately the capacity of 400 average-sized cargo ships-is changed to energy. What is the power output of the Sun?
questions.question_raw: The Sun converts an enormous amount of matter to energy. Each second, 4.19 {tex}{\times }{/tex} 109 kg-approximately the capacity of 400 average-sized cargo ships-is changed to energy. What is the power output of the Sun?
questions.explanation:
The energy liberated per second 
E = mc^2 = 4.19 $\times$ 10^9 $\times$ 3 $\times$ 10^8 $\times$3 $\times$ 10^8 = 37.71 $\times$ 10^25 J
power output of the sun is equal to energy output per second 
P = ${W \over t} = {{37.71 \times {{10}^{25}}} \over 1} $ = 3.77 $\times$ 10^26 W

questions.question: A truck starts from rest and accelerates uniformly at 2.0 ms^-2. At t = 10 s, a stone is dropped by a person standing on the top of the truck (6 m high from the ground). What is the magnitude of the velocity (in ms^-1) of the stone at t = 11s? (Neglect air resistance.)
questions.question_raw: A truck starts from rest and accelerates uniformly at 2.0 ms-2. At t = 10 s, a stone is dropped by a person standing on the top of the truck (6 m high from the ground). What is the magnitude of the velocity (in ms-1) of the stone at t = 11s? (Neglect air resistance.)
questions.explanation:
During first 10 s , the horizontal component of the velocity is v_x = u + at = 0 + 2 $\times$ 10 = 20 m/s
 at t= 11s ,the horizontal component of velocity , in the absence of air resistance remains constant hence,
From 10 to 11 s, the vertical component of the velocity is v_y = u + gt = 0 + 10 $\times$ 1 = 10m/s
Relative velocity is,
$v=\sqrt{v_{x}^{2}+v_{y}^{2}}$
$=\sqrt{20^{2}+10^{2}}$
$=\sqrt{500} $
= 22.4 m/s

questions.question:
A 0.800-kg ball is tied to the end of a string 1.60 m long and swung in a vertical circle. Calculate the total work done on the ball by
 the tension in the string and
 gravity for motion along the semicircle from the lowest to the highest point on the path.
questions.question_raw:
A 0.800-kg ball is tied to the end of a string 1.60 m long and swung in a vertical circle. Calculate the total work done on the ball by

  1. the tension in the string and
  2. gravity for motion along the semicircle from the lowest to the highest point on the path.
questions.explanation:
work done by tension will be zero because tension is perpendicular to the displacement.
W = Tscos90^o = 0
work is done by gravity in a semicircle from the lowest to the highest point on the path
W = mghcos180^o = 0.8 $\times$ 9.8 $\times$ 3.2 $\times$ (−1) = −25.1J

questions.question: A cricketer hits a cricket ball from the ground so that it goes directly upwards. If the ball takes, 10 s to return to the ground, determine its maximum height.
questions.question_raw: A cricketer hits a cricket ball from the ground so that it goes directly upwards. If the ball takes, 10 s to return to the ground, determine its maximum height.
questions.explanation:
We know that at the maximum height, the velocity of the ball is 0 m/s.
We also know that the ball takes the same time to reach its maximum height as it takes to travel from its maximum height to the initial position, due to time symmetry. The time taken is half the total time.
Therefore, we have the following information for the second (downward) part of the motion of the ball:
t = 5 seconds (half of the total time)
$v_{top} = 0 \ m/s$
g = 9.8 m/s^​​​​​​2 downward
s = ?
We know that
s = ut+${1\over 2}gt^2$
Put all the given value, we get
$\Rightarrow$ s = $0\times 5+ {1\over 2}\times 9.8\times 25$
$\Rightarrow$ s = 122.5 m

questions.question: The displacement of a particle is given by x = (t - 2)^2 where x is in metres and t in seconds. The distance covered by the particle in first 4 seconds is
questions.question_raw: The displacement of a particle is given by x = (t - 2)2 where x is in metres and t in seconds. The distance covered by the particle in first 4 seconds is
questions.explanation:
x = (t - 2)^2 
v = $\frac{d x}{d t}$ = 2(t - 2)m/s
a = $\frac{d^{2}}{d t^{2}}$ = 2(1 - 0) = 2 ms^-2 
at t = 0, v_o = 2(0 - 2) = -4 m/s
at t = 2, v_2 = 2(2 - 2) = 0 m/s
at t = 3, v_4 = 2(4 - 2) = 4 m/s
Distance = Area between time axis and(v - t) graph
= area $\Delta$OAB + area $\Delta$BCD
= $\frac{1}{2} \times O B \times O A+\frac{1}{2} \times B D \times C D$ 
= $\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 4+\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 4$ 
Distance = 8 m

questions.question: A body of mass 0.5 kg travels in a straight line with velocity v = $a x^{\frac 3 2}$ where a = $5 m^{\frac {−1}{2}}s^{−1}$. What is the work done by the net force during its displacement from x = 0 to x = 2 m is:
questions.question_raw: A body of mass 0.5 kg travels in a straight line with velocity v = {tex}a x^{\frac 3 2}{/tex} where a = {tex}5 m^{\frac {−1}{2}}s^{−1}{/tex}. What is the work done by the net force during its displacement from x = 0 to x = 2 m is:
questions.explanation:
Mass of the body, m = 0.5 kg
The velocity of the body v = $ax^{\frac 3 2}$
a = 5 $m^{\frac {-1}{2}}$s^-1
Initial velocity at x = 0 is u = 0
Final velocity at x = 2 m is v = 10$\sqrt2$ m/s
work done = Change in kinetic energy
W = K_f - K_i
W =$\frac 12$mv^2 - 0
W = $\frac 12 \times$ 0.5 $\times$ (10$\sqrt 2$)^2 = $\frac 12 \times$0.5 $\times$ 200 = 50J

questions.question: A body with mass 5 kg is acted upon by a force $\mathbf{F}=(-3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \mathrm{N}$. If its initial velocity at t = 0 is $\boldsymbol{\vec v}=(6 {\mathbf{\hat i}}-12 {\mathbf{\hat j}}) \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}$, the time at which it will just have a velocity along the y-axis is
questions.question_raw: A body with mass 5 kg is acted upon by a force {tex}\mathbf{F}=(-3 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{j}}) \mathrm{N}{/tex}. If its initial velocity at t = 0 is {tex}\boldsymbol{\vec v}=(6 {\mathbf{\hat i}}-12 {\mathbf{\hat j}}) \mathrm{m} \mathrm{s}^{-1}{/tex}, the time at which it will just have a velocity along the y-axis is
questions.explanation:
We have to find the time at which the final velocity along the y axis which means x-component will be zero. We have
$\vec{v}=(6 \hat{\imath}-12 \hat{\jmath}) m s^{-1}$ so V_X = 6 now X-component = v_X + a_Xt = 0
6 + $\frac{-3}{5} t \equiv \frac{3}{5} t$ = 6 or t = $\frac{30}{3}$ = 10 sec

questions.question:

$\vec{L}$ = constant
$\Rightarrow \vec{\tau}=r \times \vec{F}=\overrightarrow{0} \Rightarrow \vec{r} \| \vec{F}$
The components of $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{F}$ must be proportional.

questions.question: The addition of vectors and the multiplication of a vector by a scalar together gives rise to
questions.question_raw: The addition of vectors and the multiplication of a vector by a scalar together gives rise to
questions.explanation:
If a vector is multiplied by a scalar as in $p\vec A$ , then the magnitude of the resulting vector is equal to the product of p and the magnitude of $\vec A$, and its direction is the same as $\vec A$ if p is positive and opposite to $\vec A$ if p is negative.
Distributive law for scalar multiplication:
$p(\vec A+\vec B) = p\vec A+p\vec B$

questions.question: In a shotput event an athlete throws the shotput of mass 10 kg with an initial speed of 1ms^–1 at 45° from a height 1.5 m above ground. Assuming air resistance to be negligible and acceleration due to gravity to be 10 ms^–2, the kinetic energy of the shotput when it just reaches the ground will be
questions.question_raw: In a shotput event an athlete throws the shotput of mass 10 kg with an initial speed of 1ms–1 at 45° from a height 1.5 m above ground. Assuming air resistance to be negligible and acceleration due to gravity to be 10 ms–2, the kinetic energy of the shotput when it just reaches the ground will be
questions.explanation:
If air resistance is negligible, the total mechanical energy of the system will remain constant. And let us take the ground as a reference where potential energy will be zero.
According to the problem, h = 1.5 m, v = 1 m/s, m = 10 kg, g = 10 ms^– 2 23
Initial energy of the shotput = (PE)_i+(KE)_i = mgh + $\frac{1}{2}$ mv^2
= 10 $\times$ 10 $\times$ 1.5 + $\frac{1}{2} \times$ 10 $\times$(1)^2
= 150 + 5 = 155 J
Now from conservation of mechanical energy we have,
(PE)_i + (KE)_i = (PE)_f + (KE)_f
155_j = 0 + (KE)_f
Hence, final kinetic energy of the shotput is 155 J

questions.question: The average acceleration 'a' over a time interval is defined as
questions.question_raw: The average acceleration 'a' over a time interval is defined as

options.text: the change in velocity divided by the time interval.
average speed divided by the time interval.
average displacement divided by the time interval.
average velocity divided by the time interval.

correct_option_texts: the change in velocity divided by the time interval.
questions.explanation:
Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time. It is a vector quantity. Average acceleration is the change in velocity per unit time over an interval of time.Let initial velocity at time interval t_​​​​​​1 is v_​​​​​​1 and final velocity at time interval t_​​​​​2 is v_​​​​​​2, then
$\vec a_{avg}={∆\vec v\over ∆t}={\vec v_2-\vec v_1\over t_2-t_1}$

questions.question: How many joules of energy does a 100-watt light bulb use per hour? How fast would a 70-kg person have to run to have that amount of kinetic energy?

Energy = P $\times$ Time = 100 $\times$ 1Hr
Energy = 100 $\times$ 1 $\times$ 60 $\times$ 60 = 360000 J
for a 70 Kg man $K = {1 \over 2}m{v^2}$ 
speed of man v = $ \sqrt {{{2K} \over m}} = \sqrt {{{2 \times 360000} \over {70}}} $ = 101m/s

questions.question: A body moving with uniform acceleration has a velocity of 12.0 cm/s in the positive x-direction when its x coordinate is 3.00 cm. If its x coordinate 2.00 seconds later is -5.00 cm, what is the magnitude of its acceleration in ${\rm{cm}}/{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}$?
questions.question_raw: A body moving with uniform acceleration has a velocity of 12.0 cm/s in the positive x-direction when its x coordinate is 3.00 cm. If its x coordinate 2.00 seconds later is -5.00 cm, what is the magnitude of its acceleration in {tex}{\rm{cm}}/{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex}?
questions.explanation:
Distance covered s = Final position - initial position = -5 - 3 = -8 cm
Initial velocity u = 12.0 cm/s
Time taken t = 2.0 s
We know 
 $s =ut+{1\over 2}at^2$ 
$\Rightarrow$ - 8 = 2 $\times$ 12.0 + ${1\over 2}a\times4$ 
$\Rightarrow$ -8 = 24 + 2a
$\Rightarrow$ a =$ {-8-24\over 2}$ = −16.0 cm/s^2 hence this is required result

questions.question: A motorcycle stunt rider rides off the edge of a cliff. Just at the edge, his velocity is horizontal, with a magnitude of 9.0 m/s. Find the magnitude of the motorcycle’s velocity vector, 0.50 s after it leaves the edge of the cliff.
questions.question_raw: A motorcycle stunt rider rides off the edge of a cliff. Just at the edge, his velocity is horizontal, with a magnitude of 9.0 m/s. Find the magnitude of the motorcycle’s velocity vector, 0.50 s after it leaves the edge of the cliff.
questions.explanation:
the velocity components at t = 0.50 s are
v_x = v_ox = 9.0m/s 
v_y = −gt = −9.8 $\times$ 0.50 = −4.9m/s 
The motorcycle has the same horizontal velocity v_x as when it left the cliff at t = 0, but in addition there is a downward (negative) vertical velocity v_y.
The velocity vector at t = 0.50 s is given by ,
$\vec v= v_x\hat i+v_y\hat j= 9.0\hat i+(-4.9)\hat j$ 
at t = 0.50 s the velocity has magnitude v is given by,
v = $\sqrt {(v_x)^2+(v_y)^2}=\sqrt {(9.0)^2+(-4.9)^2}$ 
Hence, velocity is v = 10.2 m/s

questions.question: A body takes time t to reach the bottom of an inclined plane of angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. If the plane is made rough, time taken now is 2t. The coefficient of friction of the rough surface is
questions.question_raw: A body takes time t to reach the bottom of an inclined plane of angle {tex}\theta{/tex} with the horizontal. If the plane is made rough, time taken now is 2t. The coefficient of friction of the rough surface is
questions.explanation:
When there is no friction, a = y sin $\theta$
When there is friction, a' = g(sin $\theta$ - $\mu$ cos $\theta$)
If the length of the inclined plane is d, then
$d=\frac{1}{2} a t^{2}=\frac{1}{2} a^{\prime}(2 t)^{2}$
or a = 4a'
or $g \sin \theta=4 g(\sin \theta-\mu \cos \theta)$
$\sin \theta=4 \sin \theta-4 \mu \cos \theta$
$4 \mu \cos \theta=3 \sin \theta$
$\mu=\frac{3}{4} \tan \theta$

questions.question: “Politics is the art of the possible. Similarly, Science is the art of the soluble”. Which of the following statements is a case of this aphorism?
questions.question_raw: “Politics is the art of the possible. Similarly, Science is the art of the soluble”. Which of the following statements is a case of this aphorism?

options.text: A scientist looks for solutions that can be readily tested, implemented and are practical
A scientist looks for solutions that derive from other unproven hypothesis
A scientist looks for solutions that are abstract and unintelligible but elegant
A scientist looks for solutions that can are impossible to verify

correct_option_texts: A scientist looks for solutions that can be readily tested, implemented and are practical
questions.explanation: Science is a systematized study of observation. Scientist and researchers study these observations and then work out certain laws from them. There are multitude of natural phenomena taking place in this universe and all of them can be explained in terms of some basic laws.

questions.question: Population of a town is reported as 157,900. Which of the following statements is correct?
questions.question_raw: Population of a town is reported as 157,900. Which of the following statements is correct?
questions.explanation: 157900 implies that the population is believed to be within the range of about 157850 to about 157950. In other words, the population is 157900±50. The “plus-or-minus 50” appended to this number means that we consider the absolute uncertainty of the population measurement to be 50 – (–50) = 100. We can also say that the relative uncertainty is 100/157900, which we can also express as 1 part in 1579, or 1/1579 = 0.000633, or about 0.06 percent.

questions.question: A body of mass M moving with velocity V explodes into two equal parts. If one comes to rest and the other body moves with velocity v, what would be the value of v?
questions.question_raw: A body of mass M moving with velocity V explodes into two equal parts. If one comes to rest and the other body moves with velocity v, what would be the value of v?
questions.explanation:
By conservation of momentum,
$M V=\frac{M}{2} \times 0+\frac{M}{2} \times v$ or v = 2 V.

questions.question:
Two inclined frictionless tracks, one gradual and the other steep meet at A from where two stones are allowed to slide down from rest, one on each track as shown in Fig.
Which of the following statement is correct?
questions.question_raw:
Two inclined frictionless tracks, one gradual and the other steep meet at A from where two stones are allowed to slide down from rest, one on each track as shown in Fig.

Which of the following statement is correct?

options.text: Both the stones reach the bottom at the same time but not at the same speed.
Both the stones reach the bottom with the same speed and stone II reaches the bottom earlier than stone I.
Both the stones reach the bottom at different times and at different speeds.
Both the stones reach the bottom with the same speed and stone I reach the bottom earlier than stone II.

correct_option_texts: Both the stones reach the bottom with the same speed and stone II reaches the bottom earlier than stone I.
questions.explanation:
In the figure, AB and AC are two smooth planes inclined to the horizontal at $\angle \theta_1$​ and $\angle \theta_2$​ respectively.
As the height of both the planes is the same, therefore, both the stones will reach the bottom with the same speed.
According to the law of conservation of mechanical energy,
PE at the top = KE at the bottom
$\therefore $ mgh = $\frac 12 mv^2_1$ ...(i)
and mgh = $\frac 12 mv^2_2$ ...(ii)
from (i) and (ii), we get v_1 = v_2
As is clear from the figure, acceleration of the two stones is a_1 ​=gsin​$\theta_1$ and a_2​ = g sin$\theta_2$​ respectively.
As $\theta_2$ > $\theta_1$
$\therefore$ a_2​ > a_1​
From v = u + at = 0 + at or t = $\frac va$
As t $\propto \frac 1a$ and a_2 > a_1
 $\therefore$ t_2 < t_1
Hence, stone II will take lesser time and reach the bottom earlier than stone I.

questions.question: A train runs along an unbanked circular track of radius 30 m at a speed of 54 km/h. The mass of the train is 106 kg. What is the angle of banking required to prevent wearing out of the rail? (g =10m/s^2)
questions.question_raw: A train runs along an unbanked circular track of radius 30 m at a speed of 54 km/h. The mass of the train is 106 kg. What is the angle of banking required to prevent wearing out of the rail? (g =10m/s2)
questions.explanation:
Here, we have:
r = 30m and v = 54 km/h = 15 m/s
Let angle of banking = $\theta$
For safe turn $\tan \theta = {{{v^2}} \over {rg}}{\mkern 1mu} {\mkern 1mu} $
$\tan \theta = {{{v^2}} \over {rg}} $ = $ {{{{(15)}^2}} \over {30 \times 10}} = {{15 \times 15} \over {30 \times 10}} = {3 \over 4} $
Hence, angle of banking is:-
$\theta $ = $ {\tan ^{ - 1}}\left( {{3 \over 4}} \right) $ = 37^o

questions.question:

Reason (R): If angle between $\vec{P}$ and $\vec{Q}$ is $\frac \pi 2$, then dot product is zero.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

correct_option_texts: A is false but R is true.
questions.explanation:
If $\vec{P} \cdot \vec{Q}=|\vec{P} \times \vec{Q}|$
or PQ cos $\theta$ = PQ sin $\theta$
or tan $\theta$ = 1 or $\theta=\frac{\pi}{4}$
So, $\vec{P} \cdot \vec{Q}=|\vec{P} \times \vec{Q}|$, only when angle between $\vec P$ and $\vec Q$ is 45°.
Also, $\vec{P} \cdot \vec{Q}=P Q \cos \frac{\pi}{2}=0$

questions.question:
Assertion (A): If both the speed of a body and radius of its circular path are doubled, then centripetal force also gets doubled.
Reason (R): Centripetal force is directly proportional to both speed of a body and radius of circular path.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): If both the speed of a body and radius of its circular path are doubled, then centripetal force also gets doubled.
Reason (R): Centripetal force is directly proportional to both speed of a body and radius of circular path.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

correct_option_texts: A is true but R is false.
questions.explanation:
Centripetal force is defined by the formula
$F=\frac{m v^{2}}{r}$
$\therefore \frac{F_{1}}{F_{2}}=\frac{v_{1}^{2}}{r_{1}} \times \frac{r_{2}}{v_{2}^{2}}=\left(\frac{v_{1}}{v_{2}}\right)^{2} \frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}=\frac{2}{4}$
$\therefore$ F_2 = 2F_1

questions.question:
Assertion (A): When the force retards the motion of a body the work done is zero.
Reason (R): Work done depends on angle between force and displacement.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): When the force retards the motion of a body the work done is zero.
Reason (R): Work done depends on angle between force and displacement.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.

correct_option_texts: A is false but R is true.
questions.explanation: Work done W = $\vec{F} \cdot \vec{s}$. For retarding, the force $\vec{F}$ should be inclined to $\vec s$ at angle $\theta$ such that $\frac{\pi}{2}<\theta<\pi$. For this angle cos $\theta $ is negative, so work done is negative. Here negative work done implies that some external force is applied to change the state of a body.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): It is harder to open and shut the door if we apply force near the hinge.
Reason (R): Torque is maximum at hinge of the door.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): It is harder to open and shut the door if we apply force near the hinge.
Reason (R): Torque is maximum at hinge of the door.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.

correct_option_texts: A is true but R is false.
questions.explanation: Torque = Force $\times$ perpendicular distance of line of force from the axis of rotation. Hence for a given applied force, torque or true tendency of rotation will be high for large value of d. If distance d is smaller, then greater force is required to cause the same torque, hence it is harder to open or shut down the door by applying a force near the hinge.

questions.question:
Assertion (A): Orbital velocity of a satellite is greater than its escape velocity.
Reason (R): The orbit of a satellite is within the gravitational field of the earth whereas escaping is beyond the gravitational field of the earth.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): Orbital velocity of a satellite is greater than its escape velocity.
Reason (R): The orbit of a satellite is within the gravitational field of the earth whereas escaping is beyond the gravitational field of the earth.

options.text: A is true but R is false.
A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

correct_option_texts: A is false but R is true.
questions.explanation:
The orbital velocity, if a satellite close to earth is v_o = $\sqrt{g R_{e}}$, while the escape velocity for a body thrown from the earth's surface is v_c = $\sqrt{2 g R_{c}}$.
Thus $\frac{v_{o}}{v_{c}}$ = $\frac{\sqrt{g R_{e}}}{\sqrt{2 g R_{c}}}$ = $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ or, v_e = $\sqrt{2} v_{o}$

questions.question: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the time in seconds at which the stone reaches its maximum height. (Take g = 9.8 m/${\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}$)
questions.question_raw: A stone thrown from the top of a building is given an initial velocity of 20.0 m/s straight upward. Determine the time in seconds at which the stone reaches its maximum height. (Take g = 9.8 m/{tex}{\rm{se}}{{\rm{c}}^{\rm{2}}}{/tex})
questions.explanation:
Initial velocity is given by , u = 20.0 m/s
At maximum height it will stop, so final velocity is given by , v = 0 m/s
Acceleration due to gravity g = 9.8 m/s^​​​​​​2
Time taken to reach maximum height is = t
We know that, v = u + at
$\Rightarrow$ $0 = 20 +(-9.8)t$
$\Rightarrow$$​​ t = {-20\over -9.8}=2.04\ s$ [g is taken as negative because it is in the opposite direction of motion]

questions.question: A monkey of mass 40 kg climbs on a rope which can stand a maximum tension of 600 N. What is the tension in the rope if the monkey climbs up with an acceleration of 6 ms^−2?
questions.question_raw: A monkey of mass 40 kg climbs on a rope which can stand a maximum tension of 600 N. What is the tension in the rope if the monkey climbs up with an acceleration of 6 ms−2?
questions.explanation:
When the monkey climbs up with an acceleration of 6 ms^-2, we have
T − mg = ma
T = mg + ma
T = m(g + a) = 40(10 + 6) = 640N

questions.question: For motion under an external conservative force
questions.question_raw: For motion under an external conservative force

options.text: Total kinetic energy of a body is a non constant
Total kinetic energy of a body is a constant
P.E of a body is a constant
Total mechanical energy is a constant

correct_option_texts: Total mechanical energy is a constant
questions.explanation: Mechanical energy is the sum of the potential and kinetic energies in a system. The principle of the conservation of mechanical energy states that the total mechanical energy in a system (i.e., the sum of the potential and kinetic energies) remains constant as long as the only forces acting are conservative forces.

questions.question: Which of the following systems of units is not based on units of mass, length and time alone?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following systems of units is not based on units of mass, length and time alone?
questions.explanation: SI

questions.question: For motion in 3 dimensions we need
questions.question_raw: For motion in 3 dimensions we need

options.text: frame of reference consisting of a clock and a Cartesian system having three mutually $\perp$ axes, (X,Y, and Z)
No frame of reference is required ; a set of 3 points will do.
frame of reference consisting of a clock and a Cartesian system having two mutually $\perp$ axes (X and Y)
No frame of reference is required a single point will do.

correct_option_texts: frame of reference consisting of a clock and a Cartesian system having three mutually $\perp$ axes, (X,Y, and Z)
questions.explanation:
Motion is a change in position of an object with time. In order to specify the position, we need to use a reference point and a set of axes. It is convenient to choose a rectangular coordinate system consisting of three mutually perpendicular axes, labelled X-, Y-, and Z- axes.
The point of intersection of these three axes is called origin (O) and serves as the reference point. The coordinates (x, y. z) of an object describe the position of the object with respect to this coordinate system.
To measure time, we position a clock in this system. This coordinate system along with a clock constitutes a frame of reference.

questions.question: An airplane’s compass indicates that it is headed due north, and its airspeed indicator shows that it is moving through the air at 240 km/h. If there is a 100-km/h wind from west to east. In what direction should the pilot head to travel due north? What will be her velocity relative to the earth?
questions.question_raw: An airplane’s compass indicates that it is headed due north, and its airspeed indicator shows that it is moving through the air at 240 km/h. If there is a 100-km/h wind from west to east. In what direction should the pilot head to travel due north? What will be her velocity relative to the earth?
questions.explanation:
This is a relative velocity problem with vectors. The image given is a scale drawing of the situation. Again the vectors form a right triangle is given by:-
$\vec v_{P/E}= \vec v_{P/A}+ \vec v_{A/E}$ 
As shown in the image, The pilot points the nose of the airplane at an angle $\beta $ into the wind to compensate for the crosswind. This angle, which tells us the direction of the vector $\vec v_{P/A}$(the velocity of the airplane relative to the air), is one of our target variables. The other target variable is the speed of the airplane over the ground, which is the magnitude of the vector $\vec v_{P/E}$ (the velocity of the airplane relative to the earth). The known and unknown quantities are:$\vec v_{P/E} $ = is the magnitude unknown due to North
$\vec v_{P/A}$ = 240 km/h due to north 
$\vec v_{A/E}$ = 100 km/h due to east
We’ll solve for the target variables by using image and trigonometry. thus,
$v_{P/E}=\sqrt {(240)^2+(100)^2}$ = 260km/h
$\beta =sin^{-1} ({100 \over 240})$= 23^° 
The pilot should point the airplane 23^o west of north, and his ground speed will be 260 km/h.

questions.question: The number of significant digits in 48,923 is
questions.question_raw: The number of significant digits in 48,923 is
questions.explanation:
There are three rules on determining how many significant figures are in a number: 
 Non-zero digits are always significant.
 Any zeros between two significant digits are significant.
 A final zero or trailing zeros in the decimal portion ONLY are significant.
Keeping these rules in mind, we can say that there are 5 significant digits.

questions.question: It is found that |A + B| = |A|.This necessarily implies,
questions.question_raw: It is found that |A + B| = |A|.This necessarily implies,
questions.explanation:
We have to identity statements which are always true. It is given that $|\vec{A}+\vec{B}|=|\vec{A}|$, it could be true in two conditions that is either $ \vec{B}$ = 0 or $\vec{B}$= -2 $\vec{A}$.
For forming a single condition we will multiply them, as either one of them is true it will uphold the necessary condition
We know $\vec{B}=0, \vec{B}-2 \vec{A}$ = 0 (from previous equations)
Therefore their magnitude's product will also be zero.
$|\vec{B}|(|\vec{B}|-2|\vec{A}|)=0$ (This will always be true)
$|\vec{B}|^{2}-2|\vec{A}||\vec{B}|$ = 0
Therefore,
$|\vec{A}||\vec{B}| \leq 0$ (Equality is true for B = 0)
Above condition is always true

mg sin $\theta$ $\times$ l = $\mu$ mg cos $\theta$ $\times$ ($\frac l2$)

questions.question: The bob of a pendulum is released from a horizontal position. If the length of the pendulum is 1.5 m, what is the speed with which the bob arrives at the lowermost point, given that it dissipated 5$\% $ of its initial energy against air resistance?
questions.question_raw: The bob of a pendulum is released from a horizontal position. If the length of the pendulum is 1.5 m, what is the speed with which the bob arrives at the lowermost point, given that it dissipated 5{tex}\% {/tex} of its initial energy against air resistance?
questions.explanation:
95% of potential energy is converted into kinetic energy.
applying conservation of mechanical energy between horizontal and lowermost points
$mgl \times {{95} \over {100}} = {1 \over 2}m{v^2}$
$gl \times {{95} \over {100}} = {1 \over 2}{v^2}$
v = $\sqrt {{{2 \times gl \times 95} \over {100}}}$ = $\sqrt {{{2 \times 9.8 \times 1.5 \times 95} \over {100}}}$ = 5.3m/s

questions.question: A batter hits a baseball so that it leaves the bat at speed v_0 = 37.0 m/s at an angle a = 53.1^o. Find the time when the ball reaches the highest point of its flight, and its height h at this time?
questions.question_raw: A batter hits a baseball so that it leaves the bat at speed v0 = 37.0 m/s at an angle a = 53.1o. Find the time when the ball reaches the highest point of its flight, and its height h at this time?
questions.explanation:
The initial velocity of the ball has components
v_ox = v_​​​​​​o cos $\alpha_o$= 37.0 $\times$ cos53.1^o
= 22.2 m/s
v_oy = v_​​​​​o sin $\alpha_o$= 37.0 $\times$ sin 53.1^o
= 29.6 m/s
At the highest point, the vertical velocity v_y is zero. Call the time when this happens t_1; then
v_y = v_oy - gt_1 = 0
$\Rightarrow$ t_1 = $v_{oy}\over g$ = $29.6\over 9.8$ = 3.02 s
The height at the highest point is the value of y at time t_​​​​​​1:
$h=v_{oy} t_1-{1\over2} g(t_1)^2 $
$=29.6\times 3.02 - {1\over 2}\times 9.8 \times (3.02 )^2$
= 44.7 m

questions.question: Which of the following time measuring devices is most precise?
questions.question_raw: Which of the following time measuring devices is most precise?
questions.explanation: The least count of a wall clock, stop watch, digital watch and atomic clock are 1 sec, $\frac{1}{10}$ sec, $\frac{1}{100}$ sec and $\frac{1}{10^{13}}$ sec respectively. So the atomic clock is most precise.

questions.question: A ball starts from rest and accelerates at 0.500 m/s^2 while moving down an inclined plane 9.00 m long. When it reaches the bottom, the ball rolls up another plane, where, after moving 15.0 m, it comes to rest. What is the acceleration in m/s^2 along the second plane?
questions.question_raw: A ball starts from rest and accelerates at 0.500 m/s2 while moving down an inclined plane 9.00 m long. When it reaches the bottom, the ball rolls up another plane, where, after moving 15.0 m, it comes to rest. What is the acceleration in m/s2 along the second plane?
questions.explanation:
For First Plane
Initial velocity u = 0
Acceleration a = 0.500 m/s^​​​​​​2
Distance covered s = 9.0 m
Final velocity v =?
We know
v^2 − u^2 = 2as
$\Rightarrow$ v^2−0 = 2 $\times$ 0.500 $\times$ 9.0
$\Rightarrow$ v^2 = 9
$\Rightarrow$ v = 3 m/s
This is initial velocity for second plane
For second plane 
Initial velocity u = 3 m/s
Final velocity v = 0 (as it stop)
Distance covered s = 15 m
Acceleration a = ?
$\Rightarrow$ 0 − 9 = 2 $\times$ a $\times$ 15
$\Rightarrow$ $a = {-9\over 30}$ = -0.3ms^-2

questions.explanation:
As the road does not move at all, therefore, work done by the cycle on the road is zero.
W = 0, because road cannot move. So, s = 0
W = F $\times$ s = F $\times$ 0 = 0J

170 $\times$ 10 = 5 $\times$ 10 $\times$ 10 + $\frac{1}{2}$ $\times$ 5 $\times$ v^2
v^2 = $\frac{2 \times 1200}{5}$ = 480

questions.question: A batter hits a baseball so that it leaves the bat at a speed ${{\rm{v}}_0}$ = 37.0 m/s at an angle a = 53.1^o. Find the horizontal range R-that is, the horizontal distance from the starting point to where the ball hits the ground?
questions.question_raw: A batter hits a baseball so that it leaves the bat at a speed {tex}{{\rm{v}}_0}{/tex} = 37.0 m/s at an angle a = 53.1o. Find the horizontal range R-that is, the horizontal distance from the starting point to where the ball hits the ground?
questions.explanation:
The initial velocity of the ball has components
v_ox = v_o cos $\alpha_o$= 37.0 $\times$ cos53.1^o
= 22.2 m/s 
v_oy = v_o sin $\alpha_o$= 37.0 $\times$ sin 53.1^o
= 29.6 m/s
We’ll find the horizontal range in two steps. First, we find the time t_2 when y = 0 (the ball is at ground level):
$y=0=v_{oy}t_2-{1\over 2} g(t_2)^2 $
$\Rightarrow t_2(v_{oy}-{1\over 2} gt_2)= 0$
This is a quadratic equation for t_2.
It has two roots:
t_2 = 0 and t_2 = $2v_{oy}\over g$ = ${2\times 29.6\over 9.80 }$ = 6.04 s
The ball is at y = 0 at both times. The ball leaves the ground at t_2 = 0, and it hits the ground at t_2 = 6.04 s.
The horizontal range R is the value of x when the ball returns to the ground at t_2 = 6.04 s:
R = vox $\times$ t_2 = 22.2 $\times$ 6.04 = 134 m

questions.question: The potential energy of a spring whose spring constant is k and is compressed by x is
questions.question_raw: The potential energy of a spring whose spring constant is k and is compressed by x is
questions.explanation:
If a spring is stretched or compressed by a distance x from its natural length under the action of restoring force then
 F = -kx
Therefore, $d U=-F . d x$
$U=\int_\limits{0}^{x}-F . d x=\int_\limits{0}^{x} k x . d x=k\left[\frac{x^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{x}$
$U=\frac{1}{2} k x^{2}$

questions.question: The M.I. of a body about a given axis is 1.2 kg m^2. Initially, the body is at rest. In order to produce rotational kinetic energy of 1500 joule, the angular acceleration of 25 rad/sec^2 must be applied about that axis for the duration of
questions.question_raw: The M.I. of a body about a given axis is 1.2 kg m2. Initially, the body is at rest. In order to produce rotational kinetic energy of 1500 joule, the angular acceleration of 25 rad/sec2 must be applied about that axis for the duration of
questions.explanation:
K = 1500 J
I = 1.2 Kgm^2
$\alpha $ = 25rad/s^2
$\Delta$t = ?
$K=\frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$
$\omega=\sqrt{\frac{2 K}{I}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1500}{1.2}}$ = 50rad/s
$\alpha = {{\Delta \omega } \over {\Delta t}} = {{\omega - {\omega _o}} \over {\Delta t}} = {{50 - 0} \over {\Delta t}}$
25 = $ {{50 - 0} \over {\Delta t}}$ 
$ \Delta t = {{50} \over {25}} $ = 2 sec

questions.question: In a harbor, wind is blowing at the speed of 72 km/h and the flag on the mast of a boat anchored in the harbor flutters along the N-E direction. If the boat starts moving at a speed of 51 km/h to the north, what is the direction of the flag on the mast of the boat?
questions.question_raw: In a harbor, wind is blowing at the speed of 72 km/h and the flag on the mast of a boat anchored in the harbor flutters along the N-E direction. If the boat starts moving at a speed of 51 km/h to the north, what is the direction of the flag on the mast of the boat?
questions.explanation:
The flag is fluttering in the north-east direction. It shows that the wind is blowing toward the north-east direction. When the ship begins sailing toward the north, the flag will move along the direction of the relative velocity (v_wb) of the wind with respect to the boat.
The angle between v_w and (–v_b) = 90° + 45°
tan $\beta$ = $51 ~Sin (90 + 45) \over 72 + 51~ Cos (90 + 45)$ 
Substituting and solving we get,
tan $\beta$ = ${51 \over 50.80} = 1.0038$ 
$\therefore$ $\beta$ = tan^-1(1.0038) = 45.110
Angle with respect to the east direction = 45.11° – 45° = 0.11° with respect to east direction.
Hence, the flag will flutter approximately due east.

questions.question: Instantaneous velocity or simply velocity v at an instant t equals
questions.question_raw: Instantaneous velocity or simply velocity v at an instant t equals
questions.explanation:
Instantaneous velocity is the velocity of an object in motion at a specific point in time. This is determined similarly to average velocity, but we narrow the period of time so that it approaches zero. The formula for instantaneous velocity is the limit as t approaches zero of the change in position over the change in t. Mathematically,
$\mathop {\lim }\limits_{{\rm{t}} \to 0} \frac{{\Delta x}}{{\Delta t}}$
The Instantaneous Velocity is expressed in m/s.

questions.question: A gun fires a bullet of mass 50 g with a velocity of 30 ms^-1. Because of this, the gun is pushed back with a velocity of 1ms^-1. The mass of the gun is
questions.question_raw: A gun fires a bullet of mass 50 g with a velocity of 30 ms-1. Because of this, the gun is pushed back with a velocity of 1ms-1. The mass of the gun is
questions.explanation:
Given : $M_{\text {bullet }}=0.05 {~kg} {v}_{\text {bullet }}=30 {~m} / {s}$
Recoil velocity of gun $V=1 {~m} / {s}$
Using $V=\frac{{M}_{\text {bullet }} {u}_{\text {bullet }}}{{M}_{\text {gun }}}$
$\therefore 1=\frac{0.05 \times 30}{{M}_{\text {gun }}}$
$ \Rightarrow {M}_{{gun}}=1.5 {~kg}$

questions.question: A bullet is fired from a rifle. If the rifle recoils freely, then the kinetic energy of the rifle is
questions.question_raw: A bullet is fired from a rifle. If the rifle recoils freely, then the kinetic energy of the rifle is

options.text: same as that of the bullet
equal or less than that of the bullet
more than that of the bullet
less than that of the bullet

correct_option_texts: less than that of the bullet
questions.explanation:
By conservation of momentum,
MV = mv or $V=\frac{m v}{M}$
The kinetic energy of the rifle is
$K_{r}=\frac{1}{2} M V^{2}=\frac{1}{2} M \frac{m^{2} v^{2}}{M^{2}}=\frac{m}{M} \cdot \frac{1}{2} m v^{2}=\frac{m}{M} K_{b}$
As m < M, K_r < K_b
Thus the K.E. of the rifle is less than that of the bullet.

questions.question: A communication satellite of 500 kg revolves around the earth in a circular orbit of radius 4.0 $\times$ 10^7 m in the equatorial plane of the earth from west to east. The magnitude of angular momentum of the satellite is
questions.question_raw: A communication satellite of 500 kg revolves around the earth in a circular orbit of radius 4.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 107 m in the equatorial plane of the earth from west to east. The magnitude of angular momentum of the satellite is
questions.explanation:
As the satellite revolves in the equatorial plane of the earth, it is a geostationary satellite.
$\therefore $ T = 24 h = 86400 s, r = 4.0 $\times$ 10^7 m, m = 500 kg
$L=m v r=m\left(\frac{2 \pi r}{T}\right) r$
$=\frac{2 \pi m r^{2}}{T}=\frac{2 \times 3.14 \times 500 \times\left(4 \times 10^{7}\right)^{2}}{86400}$
$ \simeq $ 0.58 $\times$ 10^14 kg m^2 s^-1

questions.question: A dancer on ice spins faster when she folds her hands. This is due to:
questions.question_raw: A dancer on ice spins faster when she folds her hands. This is due to:

options.text: the decrease in friction at the skates
increase in energy and an increase in angular momentum
constant angular momentum and an increase in kinetic energy
increase in energy and decrease in angular momentum

correct_option_texts: constant angular momentum and an increase in kinetic energy
questions.explanation:
L =$ I\omega $ 
if L = const.
then $I\omega $ = const.
When a dancer is spinning, she has a certain angular momentum. When the dancer folds her arms, the distance of all the points of her body decreases with respect to axis of rotation so that her moment of inertia decreases. Thus, in order to conserve angular momentum, the speed of rotation has to increase and hence the dancer spins faster.
$K = {{{L^2}} \over {2I}}$
$
 K \propto {1 \over I} $ 
Hence if the moment of inertia decrease then kinetic energy will increase.

questions.explanation: As the earth is revolving around the sun in a circular motion (approximately in actual the path of the earth around the sun is elliptical) due to gravitational attraction. When we consider the earth-sun as a single system and we are taking earth as a sphere of uniform density. Then the gravitational force (F) will be of radial nature, i.e. the angle between position vector r and force F is zero. So, torque $|\vec{\tau}|=|\vec{r} \times \vec{F}|=r F \sin 0^{\circ}$ = 0

questions.question: Consider a spacecraft in an elliptical orbit around the earth. At the low point, or perigee, of its orbit, it is 400 km above the earth’s surface; at the high point, or apogee, it is 4000 km above the earth’s surface. What is the period of the spacecraft’s orbit?
questions.question_raw: Consider a spacecraft in an elliptical orbit around the earth. At the low point, or perigee, of its orbit, it is 400 km above the earth’s surface; at the high point, or apogee, it is 4000 km above the earth’s surface. What is the period of the spacecraft’s orbit?
questions.explanation:
At Apogee(High Point):
R_P = R_E + h_P = 6.38 $\times$ 10^6m + 400 $\times$ 10^3m = 6.38 $\times$ 10^6m + 0.40 $\times$ 10^6m
At Perigee(Low Point):
R_a = R_E + h_a = 6.38 $\times$ 10^6m + 4000 $\times$ 10^3m 
= 6.38 $\times$ 10^6m + 4 $\times$ 10^6m = 10.38 $\times$ 10^6 m
$\mathrm{a}=\frac{R_{p}+R_{a}}{2}=8.58 \times 10^{6} \mathrm{m}$
$\mathrm{T}=\frac{2 \pi \mathrm{a}^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\sqrt{\mathrm{GM}}_{E}}$; M_E= 5.97 $\times$ 10^24Kg;G = 6.67 $\times$ 10^−11Nm^2kg^-2
$\Rightarrow T=\frac{2 \times \frac{22}{7} \times\left(8.58 \times 10^{6}\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}}{\sqrt{6.67 \times 10^{-11} \times 5.97 \times 10^{24}}}$
$\Rightarrow$ T = 7.91 $\times$ 10^3sec = 7910 sec

questions.question:
Assertion (A): In all collisions, even for the complex nature of force, In all collisions the total linear momentum is conserved.
Reason (R): Since the third law is true at every instant, the total impulse on the first object is equal and opposite to that on the second.
questions.question_raw:
Assertion (A): In all collisions, even for the complex nature of force, In all collisions the total linear momentum is conserved.
Reason (R): Since the third law is true at every instant, the total impulse on the first object is equal and opposite to that on the second.

options.text: A is false but R is true.
Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
A is true but R is false.
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A.

correct_option_texts: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A.
questions.explanation:
Mutual impulsive forces act at the time of the collision. So, it does not depend on the nature of the force.
In all collisions, total linear momentum is conserved since the force exerted on the first particle is equal and opposite to the force exerted on the second particle (from Newton's third law of motion).
So, assertion and reason both are true and the reason explains the assertion.

questions.explanation:
Torque = Force $\times$ perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation
$\tau=\vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ or $\tau=r F \sin \theta$
$\therefore \tau_{\max }=r F$, when sin $\theta$ = 1 or $\theta$ = 90^o. thus torque is maximum when the applied force is perpendicular to r.

questions.question: A truck covers 40.0 m in 8.50 s while smoothly slowing down to a final speed of 2.80 m/s. Find its original speed in m/s.
questions.question_raw: A truck covers 40.0 m in 8.50 s while smoothly slowing down to a final speed of 2.80 m/s. Find its original speed in m/s.
questions.explanation:
We know that, for a particle moves in a straight line with a constant acceleration a_x, its motion is described by the kinematic equation given by:
 $\Delta x=\frac{1}{2}\left(v_{x i}+v_{x f}\right) \Delta t $
Rearranging it, we have
$v_{i}=\frac{2 \Delta x}{\Delta t}-v_{f}$
$\text{Substituting the values of} ~\Delta x, \Delta t ~and ~v_{f}\text{ into this equation, we get}$
$v_{i}=\frac{2(40.0 \mathrm{m})}{8.50 \mathrm{s}}-(2.80 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s})=6.61 \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.question: Impulse is:
questions.question_raw: Impulse is:

options.text: the average of force over a short period
the derivative of force over a short period
the integral of force over a short period
the difference of force over a short period

correct_option_texts: the integral of force over a short period
questions.explanation:
A resultant force causes acceleration and a change in the velocity of the body for as long as it acts. The change in momentum is equal to the product of the average force and duration. Conversely, a small force applied for a long time produces the same change in momentum, the same impulse, as a larger force applied for a short time interval.
Impulse J is
J = F_average(t_2−t_1)
The impulse is the integral of the resultant force (F) with respect to time
$J = \int\limits_{{t_1}}^{{t_2}} {F.dt} $

questions.question:
Instructions for Question 52 to 55:
If a satellite is revolving in a circular orbit of radius r, where r = R_EARTH + h
Speed of the satellite is given by V_SAT = $\sqrt[R]{\frac{g}{R+g}}$
So, higher the height of the satellite smaller is its speed.
Period of revolution is given by,
T^2 = $\frac{4 \pi^{2}}{G M}(R+h)^{3}$
So, greater the height, larger is the time period. The smallest period corresponds to a satellite which is very close to the surface of earth. Putting h $\approx$ 0,
T^2 = $\frac{4 \pi^{2}}{G M} R^{3}$
= $4 \pi^{2} \frac{R}{g}$
$\therefore$ T = $2 \pi^{2} \sqrt{\frac{R}{g}}$
Putting, R = 6.4 $\times$ 10^6 m and g = 10 m/s^2
T $\approx$ 85 minutes
At a height 36000 km, the time period becomes 24 hours.
If the period of an artificial earth satellite is 24 hours and if it is revolving in the same direction in which the earth is rotating the satellite stays over the same place on the sky and is called geostationary satellite. Its orbit is called parking orbit.
A satellite will not have a stable orbit if the plane of orbit does not pass through the centre of earth. Hence for geostationary satellite, the plane of the orbit should be the equatorial plane.
Launching of satellite is a critical application of escape velocity. Escape velocity for earth is 11.2 km/s. Let v be the velocity of projection of the satellite. Then
If v < 8 km/s, the path of satellite will be parabolic and it will return to earth.
If v > 8 km/s, the path of satellite will be hyperbolic.
If v = 8 km/s, the path of satellite will be a circle.
If 8 < v < 11.2, the path of satellite will be elliptical.
If v > 11.2, the satellite will escape from the gravitational pull and will be lost in the space.
Why a geostationary satellite is placed at a height 36000 km?

options.text: It is easy to place the satellite there.
At this height, the time period of the satellite is 24 hours.
It is easy to locate the satellite there.
The satellite is visible from all points on the earth.

correct_option_texts: At this height, the time period of the satellite is 24 hours.
questions.explanation:
Period of revolution is give by,
T^2 = $\frac{4 \pi^{2}}{G M}(R+h)^{3}$
At a height 36000 km, the time period becomes 24 hours which is same as that of the earth.
